A data smoothing processor then reconstructs an augmented signal tensor to resolve rank deficiency caused by coherent signals. Finally, an enhanced rotational subspace algorithm is employed to jointly decouple the distance, velocity, and angle parameters by exploiting the structural fitting to the restored signal. Both simulation and field experiments under real-world noise demonstrate that our proposed framework achieves significant noise reduction while improving target SNR and parameter estimation accuracy. These advancements make the proposed framework a robust solution for high-precision MIMO FMCW radar applications in dynamic, noise-polluted environments.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a tensor decomposition-based framework for FMCW MIMO radar that jointly performs noise reduction and 4D parameter estimation (distance, velocity, angle) under low SNR conditions, outperforming conventional matrix methods in both simulation and field experiments.
AI.motivation: Conventional matrix-based signal processing methods suffer performance degradation when handling higher-order signals under low SNR conditions caused by electromagnetic interference in automotive FMCW radar systems.
AI.method: A tensor decomposition framework using tensor train (TT) decomposition to exploit low-rank structure and multidimensional correlations, a data smoothing processor to resolve rank deficiency from coherent signals, and an enhanced rotational subspace algorithm to jointly decouple distance, velocity, and angle parameters.
AI.result: Both simulation and field experiments show significant noise reduction, improved target SNR, and enhanced parameter estimation accuracy compared to conventional methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed tensor-based framework provides a robust solution for high-precision MIMO FMCW radar applications in dynamic, noise-polluted environments.

title: Synthetic Emotions vs. Gamification: Exploring Engagement Strategies for Small Social Robots in Different Age Groups
summary: Many children experience challenges in emotional regulation and social interaction, which can limit their participation in everyday activities and therapeutic programs. For socially assistive robots to be effective in this context, it is essential that children remain consistently and meaningfully engaged. We explore engagement strategies for a tactile robot designed to support children suffering from anxiety disorders through daily interactions. The robot delivers either synthetic emotional feedback or point rewards to encourage user participation. We evaluated these strategies through two studies: a preference assessment with 16 school children aged 6-8 years, and a behavioral study with 14 university students aged 20-27 years in naturalistic environments. The study with school children indicated a preference for emotional engagement over points-based approaches. The follow up study with university students across a full day of interactions revealed contrasting results: points-based systems produced significantly higher task accuracy (p < 0.05) and sustained performance over time. Findings from different user groups suggest that stated preferences and behavioral outcomes can diverge depending on engagement context, highlighting the importance of validating design assumptions through observed interaction. This work contributes insights into age-related differences in engagement strategy effectiveness in human-robot interaction design.

AI.tldr: This paper compares emotional feedback vs. point-based rewards for a tactile robot supporting children with anxiety. School children preferred emotional engagement, but university students showed better task performance with points, revealing a divergence between stated preferences and actual behavioral outcomes across age groups.
AI.motivation: Children with emotional regulation and social interaction challenges often struggle to engage in therapeutic programs. The authors aim to find effective engagement strategies for socially assistive robots to keep children consistently and meaningfully engaged.
AI.method: Two studies were conducted: (1) a preference assessment with 16 school children aged 6-8, and (2) a behavioral study with 14 university students aged 20-27. A tactile robot delivered either synthetic emotional feedback or point rewards to encourage participation.
AI.result: School children preferred emotional engagement over points. However, university students showed significantly higher task accuracy (p < 0.05) and sustained performance with points-based systems. Stated preferences and behavioral outcomes diverged depending on engagement context.
AI.conclusion: Design assumptions about engagement strategies should be validated through observed interaction rather than stated preferences alone. Age-related differences significantly impact the effectiveness of engagement strategies in human-robot interaction.

title: Beyond Motion Primitives: Behavioral Activity Recognition from Head-Mounted IMU
summary: AR smart glasses need continuous behavioral context to offer proactive assistance, yet their most practical always-on sensor, the head-mounted Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU), detects only motion primitives such as walking or standing. We push beyond motion primitives to behavioral-level recognition, defining five categories that balance AR application need with sensor observability. To this end, we construct a 160K-sample Ego4D dataset with a four-tier quality assurance framework spanning 8 activity scenarios, and propose HiT-HAR, a 703K-parameter hierarchical model that outperforms prior head-mounted IMU models on five-class action and eight-class scenario recognition. We further map the observability frontier of head-mounted IMU through per-class separability analysis, identifying which behavioral categories are reliably observable (Locomotion), which benefit from temporal context (Object Transfer, Task Operation), and where scenario-dependent signal overlap poses remaining challenges. Our results indicate that architectural choices exploiting temporal context and scenario structure outperform simply scaling model size. The code and dataset are publicly available at https://github.com/Harvard-AI-and-Robotics-Lab/HiT-HAR.

AI.tldr: The paper presents HiT-HAR, a hierarchical model using head-mounted IMU data to recognize human behavioral context beyond simple motion primitives, achieving superior performance on a curated 160K-sample Ego4D dataset across 5 action and 8 scenario categories.
AI.motivation: AR smart glasses need continuous behavioral context for proactive assistance, but the head-mounted IMU—the most practical always-on sensor—can only detect motion primitives (e.g., walking, standing). This paper pushes beyond motion primitives to achieve behavioral-level recognition for AR applications.
AI.method: Constructs a 160K-sample Ego4D dataset with a four-tier quality assurance framework spanning 8 activity scenarios, and proposes HiT-HAR, a 703K-parameter hierarchical model that exploits temporal context and scenario structure.
AI.result: HiT-HAR outperforms prior head-mounted IMU models on five-class action and eight-class scenario recognition. Per-class separability analysis reveals that Locomotion is reliably observable, Object Transfer and Task Operation benefit from temporal context, and scenario-dependent signal overlap remains challenging.
AI.conclusion: Architectural choices that leverage temporal context and scenario structure outperform simply scaling model size. The code and dataset are publicly available.

title: Tackling Multimodal Learning Challenges with Mixture-of-Expert: A Survey
comment: This survey paper has just been accepted by IJCAI 2026. Results were released by 30 April 2026. As I could not find a particular place to drop the acceptance email. I have upload the acceptance email alongside the LaTeX files of the paper, named as Acceptance_email.pdf
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) presents a naturally compatible and scalable framework for multimodal learning, demonstrating strong adaptability across diverse modalities and tasks. Despite its growing success, a comprehensive and systematic review on the MoE metho addressing multimodal challenges remains lacking. Existing surveys tend to evaluate either multimodal learning or MoE independently from method taxonomy, overlooking the unique interplay between them. This survey fills that gap by answering a central question: \textit{How does MoE effectively resolve multimodal challenges?} We approach this from three key perspectives: (1) \textbf{MoE as an Efficient Multimodal Engine:} enabling scalable multimodal modeling by decoupling computational cost from parameter growth and mitigating modality redundancy through selective expert activation; (2) \textbf{MoE as a Multimodal Representation Learner:} integrating complementary multi-opinion expert knowledge to enrich alignment and interaction representations; and (3) \textbf{MoE as a Multimodal Adapter:} providing a modular and flexible mechanism to model imperfect data scenarios such as modality imbalance and missing modality. Through our extensive literature review, we identify critical research gaps, including interpretable routing, expert communication, modality integration, and lifelong multimodal learning. We position this survey as a foundation for future research toward interpretable and sustainable multimodal Mixture-of-Experts system.

AI.tldr: This survey systematically reviews how Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) addresses multimodal learning challenges from three perspectives: as an efficient engine, representation learner, and modular adapter, while identifying key research gaps for future work.
AI.motivation: Existing surveys evaluate multimodal learning or MoE independently, overlooking their unique interplay. This survey fills the gap by answering how MoE effectively resolves multimodal challenges.
AI.method: The authors analyze MoE from three key perspectives: (1) Efficient Multimodal Engine - scalable modeling via decoupling computation from parameters and selective expert activation; (2) Multimodal Representation Learner - integrating complementary multi-opinion expert knowledge; (3) Multimodal Adapter - handling imperfect data like modality imbalance and missing modality.
AI.result: Identifies critical research gaps: interpretable routing, expert communication, modality integration, and lifelong multimodal learning.
AI.conclusion: Positions the survey as a foundation for future research toward interpretable and sustainable multimodal Mixture-of-Experts systems.

title: Why LLMs Fail at Causal Discovery and How Interventional Agents Escape
summary: Causal discovery is a cornerstone of scientific reasoning, yet whether large language models can perform it reliably remains an open question. Recent benchmarks show that even fine-tuned models plateau on simple causal graphs and degrade as complexity grows, but why they fail has not been established. We prove the failure is fundamental: supervised fine-tuning, direct preference optimization, and in-context learning all produce predictors that cannot distinguish between causal graphs generating similar observational data, and any attempt to do so requires the model's internal representations to grow unboundedly, violating the very conditions under which these methods work. We formalize this as a kernel obstruction theorem, establishing that the limitation is intrinsic to the learning paradigm, \emph{not any particular model or dataset}. We propose Agentic Causal Bayesian Optimization (A-CBO), wherein a frozen language model serves as an interventional oracle answering targeted queries about intervention effects, while an external Bayesian loop concentrates beliefs over candidate graphs in logarithmically many rounds. Because the decision operates outside the space where the obstruction applies, A-CBO provably converges while the underlying model remains unchanged. On Corr2Cause, A-CBO matches fine-tuned baselines without any training. On Extended Corr2Cause, a new benchmark scaling to 24 variables with 18K test samples, A-CBO significantly outperforms both fine-tuning and preference optimization, with the advantage growing

AI.tldr: This paper proves that supervised fine-tuning, direct preference optimization, and in-context learning cannot reliably perform causal discovery because they cannot distinguish between causally equivalent graphs. To overcome this, the authors propose Agentic Causal Bayesian Optimization (A-CBO), where a frozen LLM acts as an interventional oracle while an external Bayesian loop iteratively narrows candidate graphs, achieving provable convergence and superior performance on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To understand why large language models fail at causal discovery tasks, despite recent benchmarks showing performance plateaus even with fine-tuning as graph complexity increases. The authors aim to prove this failure is fundamental rather than a model or data issue.
AI.method: The authors (1) prove a kernel obstruction theorem showing that SFT, DPO, and ICL cannot distinguish causally equivalent graphs without unbounded internal representations; (2) propose A-CBO, where a frozen LLM answers interventional queries and an external Bayesian optimization loop concentrates posterior beliefs over candidate graphs in O(log n) rounds.
AI.result: On Corr2Cause, A-CBO matches fine-tuned baselines without any training. On the new Extended Corr2Cause benchmark (24 variables, 18K test samples), A-CBO significantly outperforms both fine-tuning and preference optimization, with the advantage growing with problem complexity.
AI.conclusion: The limitation of LLMs for causal discovery is intrinsic to current learning paradigms (SFT, DPO, ICL) and not solvable by scaling models or data. However, by offloading the decision process to an external Bayesian loop while using the LLM only as an interventional oracle, provably correct causal discovery is achievable.

title: Cross-Predictive Sparse Bayesian Learning with Application to XL-MIMO Channel Estimation
comment: To be presented at the European Signal Processing Conference (EUSIPCO) 2026
summary: Accurate channel estimation is a key requirement in extremely large-scale multiple-input multiple-output (XL-MIMO) systems. Sparse Bayesian learning (SBL) is a well-established framework for exploiting channel sparsity, but its performance depends on parametric prior assumptions and hyperparameter optimization based on marginal likelihood, which may be sensitive to noise, limited pilot observations, and model mismatch. In this work, we propose \textit{cross-predictive SBL (CP-SBL)}, a data-driven variant of SBL in which the sparsity-inducing weights are learned by minimizing a randomized cross-predictive objective rather than through likelihood maximization. The proposed method preserves the hierarchical Bayesian structure of SBL while replacing parametric prior learning with a predictive consistency criterion derived from random data splitting. Numerical results for near-field XL-MIMO channel estimation show that CP-SBL consistently achieves lower normalized mean squared error than the baseline SBL across a wide range of signal-to-noise ratios, pilot lengths, numbers of antennas, and numbers of propagation paths, with comparable complexity and without requiring manual hyperparameter tuning.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes CP-SBL, a data-driven variant of sparse Bayesian learning that replaces likelihood-based hyperparameter optimization with a cross-predictive objective, achieving better channel estimation performance in XL-MIMO systems without manual tuning.
AI.motivation: Standard SBL for channel estimation in XL-MIMO systems is sensitive to noise, limited pilot observations, and model mismatch due to its reliance on parametric prior assumptions and marginal likelihood optimization.
AI.method: CP-SBL replaces parametric prior learning with a randomized cross-predictive objective derived from random data splitting, while preserving the hierarchical Bayesian structure of SBL.
AI.result: CP-SBL achieves consistently lower NMSE than baseline SBL across various SNRs, pilot lengths, numbers of antennas, and numbers of propagation paths, with comparable complexity and no manual hyperparameter tuning.
AI.conclusion: Cross-predictive learning is an effective alternative to likelihood maximization in SBL, offering robust and tuning-free channel estimation for near-field XL-MIMO systems.

title: Uni-LaViRA: Language-Vision-Robot Actions Translation for Unified Embodied Navigation
summary: Embodied navigation requires an agent to map language and visual observations to a stream of spatial actions that drive a real robot through environments it has never seen. The dominant approach has been to scale vision-language-action (VLA) foundation models on ever-larger collections of robot trajectories. This paper argues that, for navigation specifically, generality can be obtained structurally, not only through data scale. The underlying decision structure of navigation reduces to a single Language-Vision-Robot Actions Translation. The language action emits semantic-level directional command and the vision action emits a pixel-level visual target. Both outputs lie inside the natural output manifold of pretrained multimodal large language models (MLLMs), so the task can be reasoned about by an agent rather than learned from robot data. Therefore, we present Uni-LaViRA, a unified agentic architecture that extends the same insight to four task families (VLN-CE, ObjectNav, EQA, and Aerial-VLN) and to four heterogeneous real robots (Wheeled, Quadruped, Humanoid robot, and a self-built UAV) in a zero-shot manner. Two agent-loop mechanisms make this unification practical. TODO List Memory (TDM) rewrites a structured checklist of pending sub-goals at every step, reciting the unfinished items back into the agent's most recent attention window. Second Chance Backtrack (SCB) rolls the robot back to the pre-error state and conditions the agent's next plan on the failed sub-trajectory, turning single-pass navigation into a self-correcting process. With zero training effort, Uni-LaViRA reaches 60.7% SR on VLN-CE R2R, 51.3% on VLN-CE RxR, 77.7% on HM3D-v2, 60.0% on HM3D-OVON, 54.7% on MP3D-EQA, and 40.0% on OpenUAV, matching or even surpassing recent training navigation foundation models that consume millions of samples and thousands of GPU-hours.

AI.tldr: Uni-LaViRA is a zero-shot, training-free unified architecture for embodied navigation that uses pretrained MLLMs to translate language and visual inputs into actions via a Language-Vision-Robot Actions Translation framework, achieving competitive results across 4 task families and 4 robot types without any robot training data.
AI.motivation: The authors argue that navigation generality can be achieved structurally rather than solely through scaling robot trajectory data, as the decision structure of navigation reduces to a simple translation between language, vision, and robot actions.
AI.method: Uni-LaViRA is a unified agentic architecture using pretrained MLLMs to emit semantic-level directional commands (language action) and pixel-level visual targets (vision action). Two agent-loop mechanisms are introduced: TODO List Memory (TDM) for structured sub-goal tracking and Second Chance Backtrack (SCB) for self-correction by rolling back to pre-error states.
AI.result: Achieves 60.7% SR on VLN-CE R2R, 51.3% on VLN-CE RxR, 77.7% on HM3D-v2, 60.0% on HM3D-OVON, 54.7% on MP3D-EQA, and 40.0% on OpenUAV, matching or surpassing prior training-based navigation foundation models.
AI.conclusion: Zero-shot navigation can match or exceed trained foundation models by leveraging the structural decision space of navigation and pretrained MLLMs, without requiring any robot training data or fine-tuning.

title: AdaMerge: Salience-Aware Adaptive Token Merging for Training-Free Acceleration of Vision Transformers
comment: 11 pages, 3 figures, 5 tables. Submitted to NeurIPS 2026
summary: The quadratic cost of self-attention in Vision Transformers (ViTs) constitutes a fundamental bottleneck for practical deployment, motivating a vibrant line of research on token reduction. Among existing approaches, token merging (ToMe) has emerged as an elegant training-free solution; yet its design rests on an unspoken premise of token equality, which contravenes the well-documented non-uniformity of self-attention and leads to information loss in high-salience tokens under aggressive compression. We address this limitation with AdaMerge, a token-merging framework based on two complementary mechanisms. First, salience-weighted similarity leverages column-wise feature-affinity centrality as a token-importance proxy and incorporates the resulting salience scores into the bipartite matching score, ensuring that pivotal tokens contribute more strongly to the merged representation. Second, adaptive merging intensity uses pre-computed layer-wise similarity statistics to dynamically modulate the per-layer reduction count in accordance with input-specific redundancy. On ImageNet-1k with ViT-B/16, AdaMerge consistently outperforms ToMe, PiToMe, and DSM across all FLOPs-matched regimes. The accuracy gap widens monotonically with compression: at the 13.4G FLOPs operating point, AdaMerge sustains a Top-1 degradation of only -1.06%, compared to -1.45% for PiToMe and -4.62% for DSM. To our knowledge, AdaMerge is the first to combine salience-weighted similarity and adaptive per-layer reduction into a single training-free token merging framework, advancing the accuracy-FLOPs Pareto frontier of ViT acceleration.

AI.tldr: AdaMerge improves token merging in Vision Transformers by using salience-weighted similarity and adaptive merging intensity, outperforming existing methods like ToMe, PiToMe, and DSM across all FLOPs regimes on ImageNet-1k.
AI.motivation: Existing token merging (ToMe) assumes all tokens are equally important, contradicting the non-uniform nature of self-attention, which causes information loss in high-salience tokens under aggressive compression.
AI.method: Two complementary mechanisms: (1) salience-weighted similarity using column-wise feature-affinity centrality as token importance proxy, incorporating scores into bipartite matching; (2) adaptive merging intensity using pre-computed layer-wise similarity statistics to dynamically adjust per-layer reduction count based on input redundancy.
AI.result: Outperforms ToMe, PiToMe, and DSM across all FLOPs-matched regimes on ImageNet-1k with ViT-B/16. At 13.4G FLOPs, achieves -1.06% Top-1 degradation vs -1.45% (PiToMe) and -4.62% (DSM). Accuracy gap widens monotonically with compression.
AI.conclusion: AdaMerge is the first training-free token merging framework combining salience-weighted similarity and adaptive per-layer reduction, advancing the accuracy-FLOPs Pareto frontier for ViT acceleration.

title: Metric-Aware PCA as a Linear Instance of Geometric Deep Learning
summary: Geometric deep learning organises neural architectures around the symmetries of their data domain, with the choice of symmetry group serving as a geometric prior that determines what representations can be learned. Metric-Aware Principal Component Analysis (MAPCA) parameterises principal component analysis by a positive-definite metric matrix, with a canonical subfamily interpolating between standard PCA and output whitening and a diagonal-metric point recovering Invariant PCA (IPCA). This paper positions MAPCA within the geometric deep learning framework. The metric is read as the geometric prior; the orthogonal group preserving it is the symmetry group it induces; MAPCA solutions are equivariant under this group with the resulting spectrum invariant; and MAPCA's defining constraint is the linear analogue of the Schur-type weight constraints used in equivariant networks. Across six axes - domain, symmetry group, equivariance, invariance, architectural primitive, and geometric prior - we construct a precise dictionary between MAPCA and geometric deep learning. The technical anchor is a uniqueness theorem characterising IPCA as the unique linear data-derived metric in the MAPCA family that is equivariant under arbitrary diagonal rescaling and projects onto the fixed-point set of the action, equivalent under normalisation to the variance-maximisation criterion in its precise form. The paper closes with three bridges: kernel PCA as the nonlinear extension, spectral graph methods as MAPCA on graphs, and a deep MAPCA construction extending the positioning into deep equivariant networks

AI.tldr: This paper positions Metric-Aware PCA (MAPCA) within geometric deep learning, showing its equivariance properties and establishing a precise dictionary between MAPCA and geometric deep learning concepts across six axes, with a uniqueness theorem for Invariant PCA.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between classical PCA methods and the geometric deep learning framework by reinterpreting MAPCA through the lens of symmetry groups, equivariance, and geometric priors.
AI.method: The paper constructs a formal dictionary between MAPCA and geometric deep learning across six axes: domain, symmetry group, equivariance, invariance, architectural primitive, and geometric prior. It proves a uniqueness theorem characterizing Invariant PCA (IPCA) as the unique linear data-derived metric in MAPCA that is equivariant under arbitrary diagonal rescaling.
AI.result: MAPCA is shown to be equivariant under the orthogonal group preserving its metric; IPCA is uniquely characterized; three bridges are established: kernel PCA as nonlinear extension, spectral graph methods as MAPCA on graphs, and deep MAPCA extending into deep equivariant networks.
AI.conclusion: MAPCA naturally fits into the geometric deep learning framework, with the metric serving as a geometric prior that induces a symmetry group, and the approach can be extended to nonlinear and deep architectures.

title: RULER: Representation-Level Verification of Machine Unlearning
summary: Machine unlearning aims to remove the influence of specific training records from a deployed model without retraining from scratch. Current protocols verify this at the output level through membership inference, retain accuracy, and forget-set accuracy, but a model can satisfy all three whilst still encoding forgotten records in its intermediate representations. We introduce RULER, a set of representation-level verification metrics. The oracle-comparative metric M2 measures whether forget-set records occupy the same representational position as in a model retrained without them. The oracle-free metric M4 detects residuals from the unlearned model's internal similarity structure alone, without retraining. Four approximate unlearning methods all pass output-level evaluation, yet under a linear mixed-effects model M2 detects significant residuals in 10 of 12 conditions (p<0.05), with effect sizes growing as the forget fraction increases. A fifth method, Bad Teacher, shows the same residuals despite a different forgetting mechanism. M4 acts as a pre-unlearning diagnostic across tabular, image, clinical text, and face-identity settings: it detects identity-level memorisation in face recognition models where no tested method fully erases the signal.

AI.tldr: RULER introduces representation-level metrics (M2 and M4) that detect residual memorization in machine unlearning methods even when they pass standard output-level verification, revealing that current unlearning approaches are insufficient across multiple data types and settings.
AI.motivation: Current machine unlearning verification protocols (membership inference, retain accuracy, forget-set accuracy) can be satisfied by models that still encode forgotten records in intermediate representations, motivating the need for more rigorous representation-level verification metrics.
AI.method: RULER proposes two metrics: (1) M2, an oracle-comparative metric that measures whether forget-set representations match those from a retrained model, and (2) M4, an oracle-free metric that detects residuals from internal similarity structure alone. Evaluation uses linear mixed-effects models across four approximate unlearning methods plus Bad Teacher.
AI.result: All four approximate unlearning methods pass output-level evaluation, but M2 detects significant residuals in 10 of 12 conditions (p<0.05) with effect sizes growing as forget fraction increases. Bad Teacher shows similar residuals. M4 detects identity-level memorisation in face recognition models where no tested method fully erases the signal.
AI.conclusion: Current output-level verification is insufficient for validating machine unlearning; representation-level metrics like RULER are necessary to detect residual memorization that persists across diverse unlearning methods and data modalities (tabular, image, clinical text, face-identity).

title: Spatiotemporal Tracking in Cooperative ISAC Networks: A Stochastic Geometry Framework
summary: We adopt a stochastic-geometry framework to study continuous target tracking in integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) networks, with base-station locations modelled as a Poisson point process. The single-BS analysis shows that the antenna energy-conservation identity forces the mean inter-BS coupling gain to unity, making densification an antenna-irreducible liability for monostatic sensing, while a first-passage-time analysis reveals a target-distance-dependent beamwidth trap. These findings rule out single-BS tracking under densification, motivating a multi-BS cooperative treatment. The static-cluster cooperative mean tracking lifetime is then shown to exhibit a sharp percolation phase transition, with the resulting sensing-capacity ceiling saturating above a critical macro density. Yet the static-cluster idealisation itself misrepresents modern network deployments, where the cooperating cluster is dynamically re-selected as the target drifts; we therefore lift this assumption with a dynamic clustering model that maps the $K$-nearest-neighbour handover onto a 2D Brownian motion with stochastic resetting, and obtain a Bessel-function closed form for the dynamic mean tracking lifetime that dissolves the phase transition under any positive handover rate. With a per-link reliability floor, the dynamic clustering framework preserves classical linear density scaling throughout the realistic 6G regime and delivers an order-of-magnitude capacity lift at small-cell densities. Monte-Carlo simulations corroborate all theoretical predictions.

AI.tldr: This paper uses stochastic geometry to analyze target tracking in ISAC networks, showing single-BS tracking fails under densification due to antenna energy-conservation and beamwidth traps. Multi-BS cooperative tracking is studied: static-cluster models show a phase transition with a capacity ceiling, while dynamic clustering (modeled as Brownian motion with stochastic resetting) eliminates the phase transition and achieves linear density scaling with order-of-magnitude capacity gains at small-cell densities.
AI.motivation: To understand fundamental limits of continuous target tracking in ISAC networks under network densification, and to determine whether single-BS or multi-BS cooperative approaches can overcome inherent physical constraints.
AI.method: Stochastic geometry (Poisson point process), single-BS analysis using energy-conservation identity and first-passage-time analysis, static-cluster percolation analysis, dynamic clustering modeled as 2D Brownian motion with stochastic resetting yielding Bessel-function closed-form solutions.
AI.result: Single-BS tracking is infeasible under densification. Static-cluster cooperation shows a sharp phase transition with sensing-capacity ceiling saturating above critical macro density. Dynamic clustering dissolves the phase transition at any positive handover rate, preserves linear density scaling in the 6G regime, and provides order-of-magnitude capacity lift at small-cell densities.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic clustering is essential for practical ISAC network deployments; it overcomes fundamental limitations of both single-BS and static-cluster approaches, enabling scalable target tracking performance under densification.

title: Agentic Language-to-Objective Synthesis for Optofluidic Assembly
summary: Light-based advanced manufacturing increasingly requires programmable, closed-loop tools that translate human design intent into executable operations at small length scales. Yet a key bottleneck persists across robotic and manufacturing modalities: turning user intent into machine-readable objectives that are reliably executable. While micro-robotics offers versatile manipulation via optical actuation of fluids, mathematically tractable goal specification remains manual and hard to reuse. Here, we introduce Speak-to-Objective, a modular agentic pipeline that uses a conditioned Large Language Model (LLM) to translate spoken or written commands into fully differentiable objective functions for assembling microparticles in a constraint-aware inverse solver (SLSQP) and on an experimental optofluidic platform. The approach employs a compact loop - perceive -> compose -> propose -> act -> report & learn - that treats the objective as the interface between intent and actuation, separating what to assemble or pattern from how to actuate, while learning from user feedback. The pipeline composes geometry, spacing, and assignment/topology terms to generate robust descriptive objectives that assemble from partial traces and recover after perturbations, as well as explicit objectives for precise placement, all in an actuator-agnostic fashion. Using laser-induced thermoviscous flows as the physical actuation modality, we demonstrate natural-language-programmable, light-based microscale assembly of particle patterns in a microfluidic environment. Beyond its immediate impact on programmable microassembly, and using laser-induced optofluidic actuation as a reduced-complexity experimental platform, our work points toward self-driving, AI-assisted optical manufacturing platforms in which natural language, differentiable objectives, and laser-based actuation are coupled into a reusable digital workflow.

AI.tldr: A modular pipeline (Speak-to-Objective) uses LLMs to translate natural language into differentiable objective functions for controlling optofluidic microassembly, separating intent from actuation in a closed-loop, actuator-agnostic framework.
AI.motivation: Current micro-robotic and manufacturing systems lack programmable, closed-loop tools that can reliably translate user intent into machine-executable objectives. Goal specification remains manual, mathematically intractable, and hard to reuse.
AI.method: Speak-to-Objective: a modular agentic pipeline using a conditioned LLM to convert spoken/written commands into differentiable objective functions. It follows a perceive→compose→propose→act→report & learn loop. Objectives include geometry, spacing, and topology terms. It uses SLSQP as the inverse solver and optofluidic laser-induced thermoviscous flows for actuation.
AI.result: The pipeline enables natural-language-programmable microscale assembly of particle patterns in a microfluidic environment. It handles partial traces, recovers from perturbations, and supports precise placement in an actuator-agnostic manner.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates a reusable digital workflow coupling natural language, differentiable objectives, and laser-based actuation, pointing toward self-driving, AI-assisted optical manufacturing platforms.

title: Diffusion-Based Ukrainian Handwritten Text Generation with Cross-Domain Style Transfer
summary: Handwritten text generation (HTG) conditioned on writer style has been widely studied for Latin scripts, but remains underexplored for low-resource and non-Latin writing systems, leaving open how well existing models generalise beyond the Latin domain. Cyrillic, particularly Ukrainian, lacks both large-scale writer-labeled datasets and empirical evidence of such generalisation. To address this gap, we construct a Ukrainian handwritten word dataset of 126,177 images from 308 writers using connected-component segmentation, quality filtering, and targeted oversampling of underrepresented Ukrainian characters. We retrain DiffusionPen, a MobileNetV2 triplet-loss style encoder with a CANINE-conditioned latent diffusion U-Net, on this dataset without architectural modification, testing direct transfer from Latin to Cyrillic. We evaluate cross-domain style transfer in three settings: cross-lingual transfer from IAM English samples, zero-shot transfer to an early 20th-century Ukrainian manuscript, and few-shot imitation of contemporary writers. The model produces legible, style-consistent word images, indicating that few-shot latent diffusion models generalize beyond the Latin-script domain. We release the dataset, trained models, and evaluation protocol as a reproducible benchmark for writer-aware Cyrillic HTG, providing a foundation for extending stylized HTG to other underrepresented writing systems.

AI.tldr: The paper constructs a Ukrainian handwritten word dataset (126,177 images from 308 writers) and retrains a diffusion-based HTG model (DiffusionPen) on it without architectural changes, demonstrating that few-shot latent diffusion models generalize from Latin to Cyrillic scripts through cross-lingual, zero-shot, and few-shot evaluations.
AI.motivation: Handwritten text generation conditioned on writer style is well-studied for Latin scripts but underexplored for non-Latin and low-resource writing systems like Cyrillic/Ukrainian, which lack large-scale datasets and evidence of model generalizability.
AI.method: Constructed a Ukrainian handwritten word dataset (126,177 images, 308 writers) via segmentation, filtering, and oversampling; retrained DiffusionPen (MobileNetV2 triplet-loss encoder + CANINE-conditioned latent diffusion U-Net) without architectural modification; evaluated on cross-lingual, zero-shot, and few-shot transfer settings.
AI.result: The model produces legible, style-consistent word images across all three transfer settings, indicating successful generalization from Latin to Cyrillic domains.
AI.conclusion: Few-shot latent diffusion models generalize beyond Latin-script domains, and the released dataset, models, and protocol provide a reproducible benchmark for extending stylized HTG to other underrepresented writing systems.

title: Comparative Analysis of Liquid Neural Networks and LSTM for Sequential Pattern Recognition: Robustness, Efficiency, and Clinical Utility
comment: 9 pages, 7 figures, 6 tables, The conference paper will appear in Proceedings of JCSSE 2026
summary: Traditional Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) units operate on discrete time steps, often failing to capture the fluid temporal dynamics of real-world physical processes. Liquid Neural Networks (LNNs), specifically Closed-form Continuous-time (CfC) networks, address this by modeling the hidden state evolution as a continuous differential equation. In this paper, we conduct a comprehensive benchmarking study across four distinct sequential modalities: neuromorphic event-based data (N-MNIST), stroke-based drawing (QuickDraw), visual handwriting (IAM), and physiological time-series (PhysioNet Sepsis-3). Furthermore, we perform a rigorous stress test using temporal dropout to evaluate model robustness against missing data. Our findings reveal that LNNs consistently provide superior parameter efficiency and significantly higher robustness in natively temporal domains and clinical environments where data sparsity is prevalent. This extended preprint provides additional background on related datasets and the LNN theoretical lineage, supplemented with a detailed appendix documenting our full implementation and experimental settings.

AI.tldr: This paper benchmarks Liquid Neural Networks (CfC networks) against traditional RNNs/LSTMs across four temporal modalities and stress tests with missing data, finding LNNs achieve superior parameter efficiency and robustness, especially in sparse, time-sensitive domains.
AI.motivation: Standard RNNs and LSTMs operate on discrete time steps and fail to capture the continuous temporal dynamics of real-world physical processes. The authors aim to evaluate whether Liquid Neural Networks (CfC) can better model such fluid dynamics and handle data sparsity.
AI.method: The authors benchmark CfC networks across four sequential modalities: neuromorphic data (N-MNIST), stroke-based drawing (QuickDraw), visual handwriting (IAM), and physiological time-series (PhysioNet Sepsis-3). They additionally perform stress tests using temporal dropout to simulate missing data and assess model robustness.
AI.result: LNNs (CfC) consistently outperform traditional RNNs/LSTMs in parameter efficiency and robustness, particularly in natively temporal and clinical domains where data sparsity is common.
AI.conclusion: LNNs are better suited for real-world temporal tasks with irregular or missing data, and the paper provides extended background, related datasets, and a detailed appendix on implementation and experimental settings.

title: LaneRoPE: Positional Encoding for Collaborative Parallel Reasoning and Generation
summary: Parallel LLM test-time scaling techniques (e.g., best-of-$N$) require drawing $N>1$ sequences conditioned on the same input prompt. These methods boost accuracy while exploiting the computational efficiency of batching $N$ generations. However, each sequence in the batch is traditionally generated independently and hence does not reuse intermediate generations, computations, or observations from other sequences. In this paper, we propose LaneRoPE to enable coordination and collaboration among $N>1$ sequences at generation time. LaneRoPE involves two key ideas: (a) an inter-sequence attention mask to make sampling of sequences dependent on one another; and (b) a RoPE extension that injects positional information that captures relative positions between tokens, both within and outside a particular sequence. We evaluate our approach on mathematical reasoning tasks and find promising results: LaneRoPE enables collaboration among sequences, yielding additional accuracy gains under limited generated sequence length. Importantly, since LaneRoPE enables coordination with minimal changes to the underlying LLM architecture and introduces a negligible overhead at inference time, it is appealing to rapidly incorporate parallel reasoning into existing LLM inference pipelines.

AI.tldr: LaneRoPE introduces inter-sequence attention and extended RoPE to enable collaboration among parallel LLM sequences during test-time scaling, improving accuracy on math reasoning tasks with minimal architecture changes and negligible inference overhead.
AI.motivation: Traditional best-of-N sampling generates N sequences independently without reusing intermediate computations or observations across sequences, wasting the potential for coordination among parallel generations.
AI.method: (a) An inter-sequence attention mask that makes sampling of sequences dependent on one another; (b) A RoPE extension that injects positional information capturing relative positions between tokens both within and outside a particular sequence.
AI.result: LaneRoPE enables collaboration among sequences, yielding additional accuracy gains under limited generated sequence length on mathematical reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: Since LaneRoPE requires minimal architectural changes and introduces negligible inference overhead, it is appealing for rapidly incorporating parallel reasoning into existing LLM inference pipelines.

title: A Lightweight Method for Multiple Signal Direction Estimation with Adaptive Notch Filters
comment: Original article language is Turkish. This author version has the English-translated version before the original Turkish version. Accepted for presentation at SİU 2026. Final Turkish version to appear in IEEE Xplore
summary: For multi-signal detection and direction-of-arrival (DoA) estimation, conventional Capon beamforming degrades when there are more transmitters than receive antennas minus one. This paper proposes a lightweight method using adaptive notch filters (ANFs) with only two receive antennas for simultaneous DoA of two or more narrowband signals. Cascaded ANF stages form isolated channels per signal, and Capon estimates direction on each. The method has very low computational cost, and in simulation, for transmitters separable in time, frequency, and angle, performance approaches that of an oracle with prior signal knowledge. As with ANFs themselves, multiple components at closely spaced frequencies are poorly resolved, forming the main limitation of the proposed method. Simulations are complemented by an experimental implementation on a low-cost software-defined radio (ADALM-Pluto).

AI.tldr: A lightweight method using adaptive notch filters (ANFs) with only two receive antennas for multi-signal DoA estimation, achieving oracle-like performance for signals separable in time/frequency/angle, though limited by poor resolution at closely spaced frequencies.
AI.motivation: Conventional Capon beamforming degrades when there are more transmitters than receive antennas minus one, motivating a lightweight approach that works with only two antennas.
AI.method: Cascaded adaptive notch filter (ANF) stages isolate signals per channel, then Capon beamforming estimates direction on each isolated channel.
AI.result: Very low computational cost; simulation performance approaches that of an oracle with prior signal knowledge for signals separable in time, frequency, and angle. Implemented on ADALM-Pluto SDR.
AI.conclusion: The method is effective for separable signals but poorly resolves closely spaced frequency components, which is the main limitation.

title: Trinity: Unifying Class-Agnostic Terrain and Semantic Segmentation for Unstructured Outdoor Environments by Leveraging Synthetic Data
summary: Terrain understanding is fundamental for mobile robots operating in unstructured outdoor environments. Existing vision-based traversability estimation methods rely on robot-specific annotations or semantic class mappings, limiting transferability across platforms and requiring costly re-annotation when robot capabilities change, while standard semantic segmentation methods only focus on specific predefined classes, which do not capture the variety of terrains. In this work, we propose a transformer-based architecture that jointly performs class-specific semantic segmentation and class-agnostic terrain segmentation within a unified network, called Trinity. Terrain regions are segmented based solely on visual appearance, without predefined semantic labels or robot-dependent traversability scores. This formulation enables the learning of robot-agnostic visual terrain priors that can be combined with robot-specific experience for downstream tasks such as traversability estimation, visual odometry, and mission planning. To enable large-scale training with diverse terrain appearances, we extend the OAISYS simulator and introduce RUGDSynth, a synthetic dataset inspired by RUGD with class-agnostic terrain samples. Furthermore, we present the EXTerra Dataset, providing real-world images annotated with both class-specific and class-agnostic terrain labels. Experiments demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed task and the effectiveness of our joint segmentation approach in complex outdoor environments. Code and datasets will be released with this publication (after review).

AI.tldr: This paper proposes Trinity, a transformer-based architecture for joint class-specific semantic segmentation and class-agnostic terrain segmentation in outdoor environments. It introduces RUGDSynth (synthetic dataset) and EXTerra (real-world dataset), enabling robot-agnostic visual terrain priors that can be combined with robot-specific experience for downstream tasks like traversability estimation.
AI.motivation: Existing vision-based traversability estimation methods rely on robot-specific annotations or semantic class mappings, limiting transferability across platforms. Standard semantic segmentation only focuses on predefined classes and fails to capture the variety of terrains. A unified, robot-agnostic approach is needed.
AI.method: Proposes a transformer-based architecture (Trinity) that jointly performs class-specific semantic segmentation and class-agnostic terrain segmentation. Terrain regions are segmented based solely on visual appearance without predefined semantic labels or robot-dependent traversability scores. The OAISYS simulator is extended to create RUGDSynth dataset, and the EXTerra real-world dataset is introduced.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed joint segmentation task and the effectiveness of Trinity in complex outdoor environments.
AI.conclusion: Learning robot-agnostic visual terrain priors enables better transferability across platforms. The joint segmentation approach can support downstream tasks such as traversability estimation, visual odometry, and mission planning without costly re-annotation.

title: Representation-Conditioned Diffusion Models for Guided Training Data Generation
summary:
Data availability remains a critical bottleneck in many deep learning applications. Large-scale datasets are often expensive to collect, curate and annotate, which can limit the scalability and applicability of supervised learning methods. In this work, we evaluate the classification performance of models trained on synthetic image datasets produced by generative deep learning. In particular, we use latent diffusion models conditioned on learned representations from DINOv2, DINOv3, and CLIP. Our results demonstrates that this representation-conditioned formulation significantly outperforms class-conditioned generation by a large margin (+10.76 p.p. top-1 accuracy on ImageNet100), by improving sample quality and mode coverage. Furthermore, by scaling the size of the synthetic dataset, we are able to outperform a classifier trained on the real data (+2.0 p.p top-1 accuracy).
  We also demonstrate how generated images can be used for augmentation purposes, outperforming classical augmentation methods, and how the conditioning space can be used for sample filtering to further improve training value. Collectively, these findings highlight that representation-conditioned diffusion models provide a promising approach for augmenting, complementing, or potentially replacing real-world datasets in large-scale visual learning tasks.

AI.tldr: Representation-conditioned diffusion models (using DINOv2, DINOv3, CLIP) generate synthetic training data that outperforms class-conditioned generation (+10.76 p.p.) and even real data (+2.0 p.p. top-1 accuracy on ImageNet100), while also improving data augmentation and enabling sample filtering.
AI.motivation: Data availability and annotation costs are major bottlenecks in deep learning; the authors aim to reduce reliance on real-world datasets by leveraging synthetic data from generative models.
AI.method: Uses latent diffusion models conditioned on learned representations (DINOv2, DINOv3, CLIP) instead of class labels, to generate synthetic images for training classifiers.
AI.result: Representation-conditioned generation achieves +10.76 p.p. top-1 accuracy over class-conditioned on ImageNet100; scaling synthetic data yields +2.0 p.p. over real-data-trained classifiers; also outperforms classical augmentation methods.
AI.conclusion: Representation-conditioned diffusion models are a promising approach for augmenting, complementing, or potentially replacing real-world datasets in large-scale visual learning tasks.

title: Architecture-driven Shift: towards a lightweight selector for capturing the trends of logit shift
summary: Continual Learning (CL) is a practical paradigm to utilize power of deep pre-trained neural networks, but which pre-trained model has a better ability to balance ``Plasticity-Stability", deserving to be chosen? The logit shift serves as a natural proxy because it represents the logit shift in CL scenarios. However, obtaining the logit shift requires huge computational cost, which hinders large-scale model selection. Existing theoretical analyses fail to offer an efficient alternative because of the assumption of uniform hidden layer widths, which ignores the structural heterogeneity (variable width and depth) of real-world architectures. This raises a critical question: what theoretically relationship can be identified between heterogeneous architecture and logit shift on prior tasks (that the model has been trained on)? To answer the question, we decouple logit shift into architecture dependency and data dependency to establish our framework, which reveals that the combination of two dependency, defined as Architecture-driven Shift (ADS), that can capture the logit shift tendency well computable with few data samples. Specifically, for a well-optimized model on prior tasks, higher ADS is associated with a larger logit shift after training on the current task, which derived based on three mechanistic components: (1) spectral norm scaling of weight matrix gradients with layer width, (2) the optimization path length of the new task, and (3) the asymptotic task conflict in wide networks. Extensive empirical results across more than 175 diverse architectures demonstrate a strong monotonic correlation (the weakest Spearman's $r_s=0.731$) between ADS and logit shift. Practically, we demonstrate that ADS can serve as a lightweight proxy of the expected calibration error, which is a widely used metric for reliable CL model selection, on three datasets across six scenarios.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes Architecture-driven Shift (ADS), a lightweight theoretical proxy that captures logit shift tendency in Continual Learning by decoupling architecture and data dependencies, enabling efficient model selection across heterogeneous neural architectures without costly computation.
AI.motivation: To identify which pre-trained model best balances plasticity-stability in Continual Learning efficiently, as obtaining logit shift directly is computationally expensive and existing theoretical analyses fail due to assuming uniform hidden layer widths.
AI.method: Decouples logit shift into architecture dependency and data dependency, defining ADS based on three components: (1) spectral norm scaling of weight matrix gradients with layer width, (2) optimization path length of the new task, and (3) asymptotic task conflict in wide networks.
AI.result: Strong monotonic correlation (Spearman's r_s ≥ 0.731) between ADS and logit shift across 175+ architectures. ADS serves as a lightweight proxy for expected calibration error for CL model selection across 3 datasets and 6 scenarios.
AI.conclusion: ADS provides an efficient, theoretically grounded alternative for model selection in Continual Learning, overcoming computational bottlenecks and architectural heterogeneity challenges.

title: Discovery Agents for Real-Time Analytics: Toward Proactive Insight Systems
comment: Accepted at Supporting Our AI Overlords (SAO) at the ACM Conference on AI and Agentic Systems (CAIS), May 26 2026, San Jose, CS, USA
summary: Modern analytics systems are fundamentally reactive, requiring users to define queries over increasingly complex and continuously evolving data. In real-time streaming environments, this paradigm breaks down, as the space of potential insights becomes too large to enumerate manually. We present a multi-agent architecture for autonomous insight discovery over real-time data streams. The system implements a continuous discovery loop in which agents generate hypotheses, compile them into executable analytics, validate generated artifacts, and produce visualizations and deployable applications. The architecture leverages Apache Kafka for event-driven coordination, Apache Flink for stream processing, and large language models to implement specialized agents. A key contribution is a contract-driven design based on typed intermediate artifacts, enabling modularity, observability, lineage, and safer execution of dynamically generated analytics. Through use cases in retail, finance, and public data, we show how this architecture supports a shift from query-driven analytics to proactive, discovery-driven systems.

AI.tldr: A multi-agent architecture using LLMs, Kafka, and Flink for autonomous, proactive insight discovery over real-time data streams, shifting from query-driven to discovery-driven analytics.
AI.motivation: Traditional analytics systems are reactive and break down in real-time streaming environments where the space of potential insights is too large to manually enumerate.
AI.method: A continuous discovery loop with specialized LLM agents that generate hypotheses, compile executable analytics, validate artifacts, and produce visualizations. Uses Apache Kafka for event-driven coordination, Apache Flink for stream processing, and a contract-driven design with typed intermediate artifacts for modularity and safe execution.
AI.result: Demonstrated through use cases in retail, finance, and public data, showing the architecture enables proactive, discovery-driven analytics.
AI.conclusion: The proposed architecture supports a paradigm shift from reactive query-driven analytics to proactive, autonomous discovery over real-time data streams.

title: A Gray-Box Approach for Decentralized Grid-Equivalent Model Identification
summary: We propose a decentralized, frequency-domain identification algorithm that estimates the grid-equivalent model from the perspective of local converters. Since local electric signals in a multi-converter setup are affected by voltage inputs from other converters, considered as the grid, estimating a single apparent impedance yields biased and inaccurate results. To overcome this, we design a framework that decouples the effect of the equivalent impedance (passive) from that of the equivalent voltage (active). The parameters and equivalent grid voltages are then estimated using a constrained least squares and a Kalman filter algorithm, respectively, applied across frequency samples. We then demonstrate the accuracy and performance of our algorithm on an interconnected 5-converter system in grid-forming mode, with minimal voltage excitations and non-ideal operating conditions.

AI.tldr: Proposes a decentralized frequency-domain identification algorithm that decouples passive impedance from active voltage effects to estimate grid-equivalent models for local converters, validated on a 5-converter system.
AI.motivation: Local electric signals in multi-converter setups are affected by voltage inputs from other converters, so estimating a single apparent impedance yields biased and inaccurate results.
AI.method: A framework decoupling equivalent impedance (passive) from equivalent voltage (active) effects, using constrained least squares for impedance and Kalman filter for grid voltages across frequency samples.
AI.result: Demonstrated accuracy and performance on an interconnected 5-converter grid-forming system with minimal voltage excitations and non-ideal operating conditions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed decentralized frequency-domain approach effectively overcomes bias from multi-converter interactions, enabling accurate grid-equivalent estimation.

title: Design of a Real-time Asynchronous Monocular Odometry for Planetary Exploration
summary: We describe our preliminary design of a real-time asynchronous event-based monocular odometry for planetary exploration. Operating under strict computational constraints, planetary rovers frequently encounter complex, unpredictable environments that demand high-speed sensing and robustness to high dynamic range (HDR) lighting. Event cameras address these needs by reporting asynchronous, pixel-wise brightness changes with microsecond resolution, significantly reducing data bandwidth while maintaining robustness in extreme lighting conditions. We propose an approach based on an Error-State Kalman Filter (ESKF) that leverages this asynchronous event stream to continuously estimate camera ego-motion. The camera state is updated with every tracked position output generated by RATE, a real-time asynchronous feature tracker.

AI.tldr: A real-time asynchronous event-based monocular odometry system for planetary rovers using Error-State Kalman Filter with event camera feature tracking.
AI.motivation: Planetary rovers face computational constraints and challenging environments (HDR lighting, unpredictable terrain) requiring high-speed robust sensing.
AI.method: Uses an Error-State Kalman Filter (ESKF) with asynchronous event stream from event cameras, updated via RATE real-time asynchronous feature tracker.
AI.result: Preliminary design demonstrating continuous ego-motion estimation under strict computational constraints using event-based sensing.
AI.conclusion: Event cameras combined with ESKF show promise for robust planetary rover odometry in extreme lighting conditions.

title: Clinical Validation of the Melanoscope AI Mobile Dermoscopy Clinical Decision Support System
summary:
Introduction. Early detection of malignant skin lesions is critical for prognosis, yet dermatologist shortages in Russian regions limit screening coverage. Mobile dermoscopy clinical decision support systems (CDSS) offer a promising approach, with model interpretability and standardised patient routing remaining key barriers to adoption.
  Aim. To develop a quantitative interpretability assessment method for cascade deep learning models and a three-zone patient routing algorithm, and to conduct a preliminary single-centre prospective clinical validation of the Melanoscope AI CDSS in Russian outpatient practice.
  Material and methods. Two-stage cascade classification of dermoscopic images; attention map visualisation (attention rollout for ViT and Swin; Grad-CAM for ConvNeXt and EfficientNetV2); quantitative IoU-based agreement assessment between activation maps and expert annotations; prospective single-centre validation across four "Melanoma Day" sessions (Orel, Russia, June 2025 - April 2026).
  Results. On 176 patients: agreement with expert assessment 88.6%; no false negatives among 5 malignant lesions (95% CI: 47.8-100.0%); specificity 88.3%. Three melanomas and two basal cell carcinomas were histologically confirmed; six dysplastic naevi placed under follow-up. Mean IoU (n=180): ViT - 0.69; Swin - 0.64; ConvNeXt - 0.53; EfficientNetV2 - 0.51. Routing thresholds: P<0.15 / 0.15-0.50 / >=0.50.
  Conclusion. No false negatives were observed; specificity was 88.3%, supporting screening use. The integrated cascade classification, attention map visualisation with IoU assessment, and three-zone routing provide reproducible, interpretable clinical decision support adaptable to varying resource levels.

AI.tldr: This paper presents Melanoscope AI, a cascade deep learning CDSS for skin lesion screening, with attention map interpretability (IoU-based) and a three-zone patient routing algorithm. In a prospective single-centre study (176 patients), it achieved 88.6% agreement with experts, 0% false negatives for malignancies, and 88.3% specificity.
AI.motivation: Dermatologist shortages in Russian regions limit skin cancer screening coverage; existing CDSS lack interpretability and standardized patient routing, hindering clinical adoption.
AI.method: Two-stage cascade classification using ViT, Swin, ConvNeXt, and EfficientNetV2; attention map visualization (attention rollout/Grad-CAM); quantitative IoU-based agreement with expert annotations; prospective validation across four Melanoma Day sessions (June 2025–April 2026).
AI.result: 88.6% expert agreement; 0 false negatives among 5 malignancies (95% CI: 47.8-100%); 88.3% specificity; IoU scores: ViT 0.69, Swin 0.64, ConvNeXt 0.53, EfficientNetV2 0.51; routing thresholds at P<0.15, 0.15-0.50, >=0.50.
AI.conclusion: No false negatives and 88.3% specificity support screening feasibility; the cascade architecture with IoU-based interpretability and three-zone routing offers reproducible, resource-adaptive clinical decision support.

title: Detect by Yourself: Self-Designing Agentic Workflows for Few-Shot Graph Anomaly Detection
summary: Graph anomaly detection aims to identify anomaly nodes in attributed graphs and plays an important role in real-world applications. However, existing graph anomaly detection methods still face two key challenges: 1) fixed pipelines, which restrict their adaptability across different graph tasks under limited supervision; 2) weak evidence, which prevents them from explicitly incorporating contextual and structural anomaly signals into the detection process. In this paper, we propose a novel framework, self-designing agentic workflows for few-shot graph anomaly detection (SignGAD). Specifically, we propose a novel paradigm that reformulates graph anomaly detection task from training a fixed anomaly detector to designing task-conditioned detection workflows. By constructing detection workflows, SignGAD selects suitable graph encodings and detector designs to exploit task-specific anomaly evidence. Meanwhile, we introduce a guarded final refit strategy to refine the selected workflow by calibrating refit acceptance, enhancing reliability under limited supervision. Extensive experiments conducted on several real-world datasets demonstrate that SignGAD achieves strong performance against state-of-the-art methods, highlighting its effectiveness on graph anomaly detection tasks.

AI.tldr: SignGAD reformulates graph anomaly detection as designing task-conditioned detection workflows rather than training fixed detectors, achieving strong performance via adaptive graph encodings and detector designs with a guarded refit strategy.
AI.motivation: Existing graph anomaly detection methods suffer from fixed pipelines that lack adaptability across tasks under limited supervision, and weak evidence due to inability to explicitly incorporate contextual and structural anomaly signals.
AI.method: 1) Reformulates GAD from training a fixed detector to designing task-conditioned detection workflows. 2) Selects suitable graph encodings and detector designs to exploit task-specific anomaly evidence. 3) Introduces a guarded final refit strategy to calibrate refit acceptance under limited supervision.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate SignGAD achieves strong performance against state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: SignGAD is an effective framework for graph anomaly detection, especially under few-shot settings, by enabling adaptive, self-designing workflows that improve reliability and performance.

title: Agyn: An Open-Source Platform for AI Agents with Scalable On-Demand Execution, Agent Definition as a Code, and Zero-Trust Access
summary: As organizations move toward production deployments of AI agents, which execute non-deterministic workflows, maintain stateful sessions, and often operate with privileged access to internal services, the engineering challenge shifts from building individual agents to operating them at scale with proper isolation, governance, and security. In this paper we present Agyn, an open-source platform designed around three key principles tailored for agent workloads: a signal-driven, stateful serverless runtime on Kubernetes; a Terraform provider for agent and harness definition; and a security model grounded in zero-trust and least-privilege principles. Agyn is agent-agnostic, model-agnostic, and cloud-agnostic.

AI.tldr: Agyn is an open-source platform for deploying AI agents at scale, built on a signal-driven serverless Kubernetes runtime, Terraform-based infrastructure-as-code, and a zero-trust security model.
AI.motivation: Organizations face challenges operating AI agents in production due to non-deterministic workflows, stateful sessions, privileged access, and the need for isolation, governance, and security.
AI.method: Agyn implements three key design principles: (1) a signal-driven, stateful serverless runtime on Kubernetes; (2) a Terraform provider for declarative agent and harness definition; (3) a security model based on zero-trust and least-privilege principles.
AI.result: The paper presents Agyn as an open-source platform that is agent-agnostic, model-agnostic, and cloud-agnostic for production agent deployment.
AI.conclusion: Agyn addresses the engineering shift from building individual AI agents to operating them at scale with proper infrastructure and security foundations.

title: A Unified Maximum-Likelihood Framework for 3D InISAR Phase Unwrapping with Outlier Rejection
comment: 6 pages, 4 figures. Accepted for publication in the Proceedings of the 2026 IEEE Radar Conference (RadarConf26)
summary: This paper presents a novel mathematical framework for phase unwrapping in three-dimensional interferometric ISAR (3D InISAR) imaging. The approach works on a scatterer-by-scatterer basis and does not rely on any spatial continuity assumptions, making it suitable for sparse point clouds. The formulation is derived from the Mixed-Integer Least Squares (MILS) theory, an optimal maximum-likelihood framework for joint estimation of integer and real unknowns in the presence of Gaussian noise. This provides a unified way to handle generic sensor geometries, multi-baseline, multi-frequency, or hybrid setups. The method also produces a natural a posteriori quality metric for each unwrapped phase, which can be used to build a statistical test to reject outliers. The algorithm is simple to implement and has a computational cost suitable for operational systems. This paper presents the theoretical foundations of the framework and a first validation study on a standard L-shaped dual-frequency setup using Monte Carlo simulations. Results show that the proposed framework enables reliable 3D reconstruction in challenging ambiguity conditions.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a novel MILS-based mathematical framework for phase unwrapping in 3D InISAR imaging that operates per-scatterer without spatial continuity assumptions, works with generic sensor geometries, provides a quality metric for outlier rejection, and demonstrates reliable 3D reconstruction in challenging ambiguity conditions.
AI.motivation: To develop a phase unwrapping method for 3D InISAR that does not rely on spatial continuity assumptions, can handle sparse point clouds, and works across diverse sensor geometries (multi-baseline, multi-frequency, hybrid setups) while providing robust performance under challenging ambiguity conditions.
AI.method: Derives a novel framework from Mixed-Integer Least Squares (MILS) theory for joint estimation of integer and real unknowns under Gaussian noise, operating on a scatterer-by-scatterer basis, with a built-in a posteriori quality metric for statistical outlier rejection.
AI.result: Monte Carlo simulations on a standard L-shaped dual-frequency setup demonstrate that the framework enables reliable 3D reconstruction in challenging ambiguity conditions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed MILS-based framework provides a unified, computationally efficient, and outlier-robust solution for 3D InISAR phase unwrapping suitable for operational systems.

title: Simulation-Informed Diffusion for Decentralized Multi-robot Motion Planning
summary: Decentralized multi-robot motion planning requires each robot to generate collision-free trajectories from local observations, without global sensing or reliable communication. However, most existing planners, whether classical or learning-based, generate trajectories from a static snapshot of the local observation, which limits their ability to anticipate the future behavior of neighboring robots. This limitation is critical as the number of robots increases and the environment becomes more cluttered. To overcome this challenge, this paper introduces Simulation-Informed Diffusion (SID), a decentralized framework built on constraint-aware diffusion models (CADM). SID first uses CADM to simulate the future trajectories of neighboring robots from their currently observed states, and then uses the same CADM to plan each robot's own trajectory under safety constraints informed by these simulations. Crucially, the accurate simulation of neighbors enables a minimal communication scheme that triggers coordination only when necessary in highly congested scenarios. Experiments across diverse environments show that SID consistently outperforms baseline methods in terms of planning effectiveness and constraint satisfaction, and scales to scenarios with 108 robots and 160 obstacles.

AI.tldr: SID uses diffusion models to simulate neighbor trajectories and plan collision-free paths for decentralized multi-robot systems with minimal communication.
AI.motivation: Existing decentralized multi-robot planners rely on static local observations and fail to anticipate neighbor behavior, which becomes critical in crowded, large-scale scenarios.
AI.method: A constraint-aware diffusion model (CADM) first simulates future trajectories of neighboring robots, then uses those simulations to plan each robot's own safe trajectory. A minimal communication scheme triggers coordination only when necessary.
AI.result: Outperforms baselines in planning effectiveness and constraint satisfaction; scales to 108 robots and 160 obstacles across diverse environments.
AI.conclusion: Simulation-informed planning via diffusion models improves decentralized multi-robot coordination without requiring global sensing or reliable communication.

title: What-If World: A Causal Benchmark for General World Models in Embodied Scenarios
summary: Video generation models are increasingly used as world simulators for tasks like driving and robotic manipulation. What matters in these settings is not whether a single video looks right, but whether the model's output changes when its input changes. We test this by giving a model two prompts describing the same scene with one physical detail varied, and checking whether the two videos diverge the way physics predicts. The wording difference between the prompts is small by design, since only one variable is changed, but the correct physical difference is not. A model that misses this can still produce two videos that each look plausible individually, and existing benchmarks score videos one at a time and cannot detect this failure. We introduce What-If World, 319 such prompt pairs built on real frames from nuScenes and DROID, organized by a taxonomy of six physical variables shared across driving and manipulation. Each pair is scored with APEO, a four-part rubric checking whether each video follows its prompt (Adherence), is physically consistent (Physics), preserves the shared scene (Environment), and ends in the correct difference (Outcome). Across nine state-of-the-art models, no system exceeds 52% on the paired score, and open-source models cluster near 28%. Every model tested fails on a large fraction of causal interventions, indicating substantial room before these models can reliably support action-conditioned simulation or model-based planning. Where models do score well, performance appears to track the visual prominence of the intervention rather than the tractability of its underlying physics. Some visually subtle interventions score as low as 14.2%, while visually pronounced ones reach 40.4%.

AI.tldr: What-If World benchmark tests video generation models on causal physical reasoning using 319 prompt pairs across driving/manipulation domains. No model exceeds 52% paired score; open-source models ~28%. Models rely on visual prominence rather than physical understanding.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks score videos individually and fail to detect whether models correctly respond to physical input changes—a key requirement for world simulation in driving and robotics.
AI.method: 319 prompt pairs built on nuScenes/DROID frames, varying one of six physical variables. Scored with APEO rubric (Adherence, Physics, Environment, Outcome).
AI.result: Best model achieves 52% paired score; open-source models ~28%. Visually subtle interventions score 14.2% vs visually pronounced 40.4%.
AI.conclusion: Current video generation models fail at causal physical reasoning, relying on visual cues rather than physics. Substantial improvement needed for action-conditioned simulation or planning.

title: HEAL: Resilient and Self-* Hub-based Learning
summary: Decentralized learning enhances privacy, scalability, and fault tolerance by distributing data and computation across nodes. A popular approach is Federated learning, which relies on a central aggregator, yet faces challenges such as server vulnerabilities, scalability issues, privacy risks and most importantly, the single point of failure. Alternatively Gossip Learning and Epidemic Learning offer fully decentralization through peer-to-peer exchanges of model updates, ensuring robustness and privacy, at the price of slower model convergence. In this work, we introduce a novel decentralized learning framework called HEAL. HEAL is the first cross-layer decentralized learning framework that exploits an optimized self-organizing and self-healing underlying P2P overlay combining the strengths of Federated Learning, Gossip and Epidemic Learning. Leveraging the recently proposed Elevator algorithm, HEAL promotes dynamically chosen nodes to act as aggregators. Through simulations, we demonstrate that HEAL has similar performances to that of Federated Learning in crash-free settings, while being fully decentralized and fault-tolerant. In crash and churn prone environments HEAL outperforms Gossip and Epidemic Learning.

AI.tldr: HEAL is a novel cross-layer decentralized learning framework that combines Federated, Gossip, and Epidemic Learning via a self-organizing P2P overlay, achieving FL-like performance in crash-free settings and outperforming Gossip/Epidemic Learning under crashes and churn.
AI.motivation: To overcome the single point of failure in Federated Learning (central aggregator) while addressing the slower convergence of fully decentralized approaches like Gossip and Epidemic Learning.
AI.method: HEAL leverages the Elevator algorithm to dynamically promote nodes as aggregators within an optimized self-organizing and self-healing P2P overlay, integrating FL-style aggregation with Gossip/Epidemic-style peer-to-peer exchanges.
AI.result: In crash-free settings, HEAL achieves performance similar to Federated Learning; in crash- and churn-prone environments, it outperforms both Gossip and Epidemic Learning.
AI.conclusion: HEAL offers a fully decentralized, fault-tolerant learning framework that matches FL's effectiveness under ideal conditions and surpasses existing decentralized methods under node failures.

title: You Are in Control of Your State: Why Human Outcomes Are Controllable Through Causal State Intervention
comment: 20 pages, 12 figures, 37 references. Companion to a prior SSRN preprint on causal architecture for human modelling
summary:
A central puzzle for the behavioural sciences and for human-facing artificial intelligence is the persistence of within-person variability. The same individual, presented with the same observable input, produces different outcomes on different occasions, and different individuals produce divergent outcomes that no observable covariate fully predicts.
