We argue that this variability belongs in the dynamic latent state of the person, and that human outcomes are controllable in a precise and operational sense through interventions that target the state and its weighting at the moment a decision is being formed.
  We define a state as the time-indexed weighting vector over the dimensions that govern how an individual's biology, physiology, and neuropsychology process the next event into a decision and an outcome. The relationship between state, decision, and outcome is causal rather than correlational. The weighting vector is dynamic at sub-daily timescales. The conscious channel through which outcomes are reportable is a narrow attentional bottleneck whose contents are themselves state-dependent. Taken together, these claims imply that the outcome of a given event is controllable, conditionally, on the state-trajectory at the time of intervention.
  We motivate the framework with six strands of established evidence (causal inference, predictive processing, allostasis, attentional bottleneck, chronobiology, computational psychiatry) and a 24-month observational base from a deployed behavioural platform spanning more than 200,000 consented users across four occupational personas (research period 2023 to 2026). We derive seven testable predictions, list six operational requirements for state-aware systems, and discuss implications for digital health, education, AI personalisation, and personal agency.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that within-person behavioral variability arises from dynamic latent states (time-indexed weighting vectors) that causally govern how biology/physiology/neuropsychology process events into decisions. The authors propose that outcomes are controllable through state-targeting interventions, supported by six evidence strands and a 24-month observational study of 200,000+ users across four occupations.
AI.motivation: To resolve the puzzle of persistent within-person variability in behavior—why same individuals produce different outcomes given identical inputs—by proposing a causal, state-based framework that makes human outcomes controllable through targeted interventions.
AI.method: The authors develop a theoretical framework defining "state" as a dynamic weighting vector over dimensions governing decision processing. They support this with six strands of established evidence (causal inference, predictive processing, allostasis, attentional bottleneck, chronobiology, computational psychiatry) and a large-scale 24-month observational study (200,000+ users, four occupations, 2023-2026). They derive seven testable predictions and six operational requirements.
AI.result: Seven testable predictions derived from the framework; six operational requirements for building state-aware systems; empirical grounding from a 200,000+ user observational deployment across four occupational personas.
AI.conclusion: Human behavioral variability is controllable through state-targeting interventions. The framework has implications for digital health, education, AI personalisation, and personal agency, with the conscious channel being a narrow, state-dependent attentional bottleneck.

title: Transformer-Based Heartbeat Monitoring with FMCW Radar Under Random Body Motion
comment: 6 pages, 5 figures. Accepted for publication in the Proceedings of the 2026 IEEE Radar Conference (RadarConf26)
summary: Millimeter-wave Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW) radar enables contactless cardiac monitoring, but heartbeat estimation becomes challenging when respiration and random body motion (RBM) distort the radar signal. In this paper, we propose a hybrid framework for 77 GHz FMCW radar that combines model-based signal processing with a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)-Transformer network. The first block extracts chest displacement and constructs meaningful high-level motion features from raw radar data, while the second block reconstructs a photoplethysmography (PPG)-like signal from the extracted features. In this study, a synchronized PPG signal is used as the ground truth for heartbeat monitoring in supervised training. The method is evaluated following the IEEE AESS Radar Challenge Problem I protocol using the official datasets and figures of merit across three motion scenarios: stationary, deep breathing, and RBM. Results show that the proposed architecture reliably reconstructs the PPG signal in all scenarios, achieving high fidelity in controlled conditions and maintaining robust performance under motion. This enables reliable average heart rate (AHR) and heart rate variability (HRV) estimation even where benchmark methods fail, and leads to the highest total score among the compared approaches.

AI.tldr: Proposes a hybrid model-based + CNN-Transformer framework for 77 GHz FMCW radar to extract heartbeat from chest displacement, reconstruct a PPG-like signal, and achieve robust AHR/HRV estimation even under respiration and random body motion.
AI.motivation: Heartbeat estimation via millimeter-wave FMCW radar is severely degraded by respiration and random body motion (RBM), making reliable cardiac monitoring challenging in real-world scenarios.
AI.method: A two-block hybrid framework: (1) model-based signal processing extracts chest displacement and high-level motion features from raw radar data; (2) a CNN-Transformer network reconstructs a PPG-like signal from those features, trained with synchronized PPG as ground truth.
AI.result: The method reliably reconstructs the PPG signal across stationary, deep breathing, and RBM scenarios; achieves high fidelity in controlled conditions and robust performance under motion; yields the highest total score among compared methods on the IEEE AESS Radar Challenge benchmark.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid framework enables reliable AHR and HRV estimation where benchmark methods fail, demonstrating strong potential for practical contactless cardiac monitoring under adverse motion conditions.

title: AURA: Asymptotically Optimal Uncertainty-Robust Replanning Algorithm for Kinodynamic Systems
summary: Sampling-based motion planners offer a practical and scalable approach to kinodynamic motion planning, notably for high-dimensional, underactuated, or non-holonomic systems. However, these planners are typically used offline, requiring execution to begin only after the trajectory has been computed. In addition, the planned trajectory may not be accurately tracked in the presence of motion uncertainty, leading to deviations from the nominal solution. In this work, these limitations were addressed within a unified framework, \method, an asymptotically-optimal meta-planner framework that improves both path quality and tracking performance during execution. In addition to the main execution thread, this framework comprises a replanning method that continuously explores the state space and refines the trajectory during execution, and an optimization process that refines future control inputs to reduce tracking error. Together, these components enable \method to leverage asymptotically optimal planning online while improving execution accuracy under uncertainty. The proposed approach is evaluated in both simulation and real-world environments across multiple systems, demonstrating consistent improvements in trajectory quality, tracking accuracy, and overall performance compared with baseline methods.

AI.tldr: A unified meta-planner framework that combines online replanning and control optimization to improve both path quality and tracking accuracy for sampling-based motion planning under uncertainty.
AI.motivation: Sampling-based motion planners are typically used offline, requiring execution to wait until trajectory computation finishes, and suffer from tracking errors due to motion uncertainty.
AI.method: A meta-planner framework with (1) a main execution thread, (2) a continuous replanning method exploring state space and refining trajectories during execution, and (3) an optimization process refining future control inputs to reduce tracking error.
AI.result: Demonstrates consistent improvements in trajectory quality, tracking accuracy, and overall performance over baseline methods in both simulation and real-world experiments across multiple systems.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework enables asymptotically optimal planning to be leveraged online while improving execution accuracy under uncertainty.

title: ForestHG-Trace: Traceable Long-Horizon Ecological Reasoning over Large-Scale Forest Scenes
summary: Remote sensing question answering (RS-QA) often requires more than direct semantic prediction, especially in large-scale forest scenes where ecological analysis involves multi-step filtering, numerical aggregation, neighborhood reasoning, and verifiable evidence. We introduce ForestHG-Trace, a framework for traceable long-horizon ecological reasoning over forest environments. It represents multimodal NEON forest scenes as ecological hypergraphs, where tree instances, spatial units, semantic groups, and neighborhood relations support higher-order reasoning beyond pairwise scene graphs. An LLM-guided agent then invokes deterministic tools for reading, filtering, expansion, aggregation, comparison, and auditing, producing replayable execution traces and compact evidence records rather than only free-form answers. We further construct ForestTraceQA, an executable benchmark for evaluating ecological QA across diverse task types and reasoning depths. Experiments show that ForestHG-Trace substantially improves answer accuracy and execution faithfulness over single-step baselines and scene-graph agents, while highlighting execution depth as the main bottleneck for long-horizon ecological QA.

AI.tldr: ForestHG-Trace is a framework that uses ecological hypergraphs and LLM-guided deterministic tools to enable traceable, multi-step ecological reasoning for remote sensing QA in forest environments. It introduces ForestTraceQA benchmark and shows improved accuracy and faithfulness over baselines, with execution depth as the main bottleneck.
AI.motivation: Remote sensing QA in large-scale forest scenes requires multi-step reasoning (filtering, aggregation, neighborhood reasoning, evidence verification) that direct semantic prediction cannot handle well.
AI.method: Represents NEON forest scenes as ecological hypergraphs (higher-order than pairwise scene graphs), and uses an LLM-guided agent to invoke deterministic tools (reading, filtering, expansion, aggregation, comparison, auditing) for producing replayable execution traces and compact evidence records.
AI.result: ForestHG-Trace substantially improves answer accuracy and execution faithfulness over single-step baselines and scene-graph agents; execution depth is identified as the main bottleneck for long-horizon ecological QA.
AI.conclusion: Hypergraph-based structured representation with LLM-guided tool invocation is effective for complex ecological reasoning, but long-horizon execution depth remains a key challenge.

title: Balancing Fidelity and Diversity in Diffusion Models via Symmetric Attention Decomposition: Hopfield Perspective
summary: We characterize the pre-softmax attention matrix $\mathbf{QK^\top}$ in transformers as an associative memory matrix encoding pairwise associations between input features. By decomposing this matrix into its symmetric and skew-symmetric parts, we interpret the symmetric component as governing the structure of the energy landscape, and the skew-symmetric component as driving circulation on that landscape. Leveraging the energy formulation induced by the symmetric component, we derive Hopfield-style stability measures that quantify the stability of retrieved features. We observe meaningful correlations between Hopfield-style stability measures and the fidelity-diversity trade-offs in generation. Finally, we propose a controllable knob to modulate this trade-off by modifying the circulation of the underlying dynamics. Code is available at our GitHub (https://github.com/hyeon-cho/Attention-Symmetric-Decomposition).

AI.tldr: The paper decomposes the pre-softmax attention matrix into symmetric and skew-symmetric parts, interpreting them as energy landscape and circulation respectively, and derives Hopfield-style stability measures to control fidelity-diversity trade-offs in generation.
AI.motivation: To understand the underlying mechanisms of attention in transformers and how they relate to stability and trade-offs in generation quality.
AI.method: Decomposes the QK^T attention matrix into symmetric (energy landscape) and skew-symmetric (circulation) components, derives Hopfield-style stability measures, and proposes a controllable knob to modulate the fidelity-diversity trade-off by modifying circulation.
AI.result: Meaningful correlations are observed between Hopfield-style stability measures and fidelity-diversity trade-offs in generation tasks.
AI.conclusion: The symmetric-skew decomposition of attention matrices provides interpretable control over generation quality, enabling modulation of fidelity vs. diversity.

title: Cyberbullying Governance on Social Media: A Unified Framework from Content Identification to Intervention
summary: The proliferation of social media platforms and online communities has inadvertently catalyzed the spread of cyberbullying, hate speech, and other forms of online toxicity, making the effective governance of such harm a critical societal and computational challenge. While significant strides have been made in automating content moderation, existing research predominantly treats cyberbullying governance as passive, isolated detection at the post level. This reductionist view overlooks the continuous behavioral dynamics of users, the structural diffusion of toxic events, and the critical need for proactive mitigation. To bridge these gaps, this paper proposes a unified full-lifecycle governance framework that shifts the paradigm of cyberbullying governance from isolated static detection toward integrated, continuous, and proactive moderation. Drawing on cyberbullying research and adjacent fields, we systematically synthesize the state-of-the-art literature across four interconnected stages: (1) Content Identification, (2) User and Behavior Modeling, (3) Diffusion Dynamics and Early Warning, and (4) Intervention and Governance. Furthermore, we review available datasets and evaluation practices, and discuss emerging challenges including multimodality, explainability, algorithmic fairness, and the dual-use risks of generative AI, providing a roadmap for future research toward a safer and more resilient digital ecosystem.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a unified full-lifecycle governance framework for cyberbullying, shifting from passive static detection to integrated, continuous, and proactive moderation across four stages: content identification, user/behavior modeling, diffusion dynamics and early warning, and intervention/governance.
AI.motivation: Existing cyberbullying governance is limited to passive, isolated post-level detection, ignoring continuous user behavioral dynamics, structural diffusion of toxic events, and the need for proactive mitigation.
AI.method: The paper systematically synthesizes state-of-the-art literature across four interconnected stages: (1) Content Identification, (2) User and Behavior Modeling, (3) Diffusion Dynamics and Early Warning, and (4) Intervention and Governance. It also reviews datasets, evaluation practices, and emerging challenges.
AI.result: A unified full-lifecycle governance framework that shifts cyberbullying governance from isolated static detection to integrated, continuous, and proactive moderation.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a roadmap for future research addressing multimodality, explainability, algorithmic fairness, and dual-use risks of generative AI toward a safer digital ecosystem.

title: Experimental Characterization of a Multifunction X-Band AESA Radar Demonstrator
comment: 6 pages, 7 figures. Accepted for publication in the Proceedings of the 2026 IEEE Radar Conference (RadarConf26)
summary: Modern naval surveillance demands multifunction radar systems capable of operating in cluttered and contested environments. This paper presents the experimental characterization of a compact, X-band Active Electronically Scanned Array (AESA) radar demonstrator. The system was evaluated in a realistic coastal field environment at Naval Support and Experimentation Centre (CSSN) and, specifically, its specialized institute, the G. Vallauri Institute, which has historical expertise in testing and evaluating the performance of operational sensors as well as those under development, using real maritime targets and an active noise jammer. The trials assessed three core functions: direction-of-arrival (DoA) estimation, adaptive jammer suppression using MVDR beamforming, and high-resolution Inverse Synthetic Aperture Radar (ISAR) imaging. The results confirm that the demonstrator successfully detects and localizes targets, effectively suppresses high-power interference, and generates clear ISAR images of non-cooperative vessels. These findings validate the multifunction performance of the AESA demonstrator, confirming its suitability for advanced naval surveillance applications.

AI.tldr: An X-band AESA radar demonstrator was tested in a coastal environment, validating its multifunction capabilities in DoA estimation, adaptive jammer suppression (MVDR beamforming), and high-resolution ISAR imaging for naval surveillance.
AI.motivation: Modern naval surveillance requires multifunction radar systems that can operate effectively in cluttered and contested environments, necessitating experimental validation of such systems.
AI.method: The AESA radar demonstrator was experimentally characterized in a realistic coastal field environment at CSSN/G. Vallauri Institute, using real maritime targets and an active noise jammer to assess DoA estimation, MVDR beamforming for jammer suppression, and ISAR imaging.
AI.result: The demonstrator successfully detected and localized targets, suppressed high-power interference, and generated clear ISAR images of non-cooperative vessels.
AI.conclusion: The findings validate the multifunction performance of the AESA demonstrator, confirming its suitability for advanced naval surveillance applications.

title: HumanoidMimicGen: Data Generation for Loco-Manipulation via Whole-Body Planning
summary: Imitation learning is a promising approach for training humanoid robots to both walk and manipulate, but it requires a large number of demonstrations, which are time-intensive and difficult to collect via teleoperation. Existing data-generation algorithms can automatically synthesize demonstrations for manipulators, but they are ineffective on humanoids because their high-dimensional composite action spaces involve arms, legs, and torsos. We present HumanoidMimicGen, a method for generating humanoid legged loco-manipulation data. Our method adapts contact-rich whole-body skills from a handful of source demonstrations to new states, generalizing across changes in object pose. By interleaving these single- and dual-arm skills with whole-body locomotion and manipulation planning, the method generates stable, collision-free data across diverse scenes and layouts. To evaluate our approach, we introduce a new simulated loco-manipulation benchmark containing nine diverse tasks that test humanoid loco-manipulation capabilities. There, we demonstrate that HumanoidMimicGen automatically generates large datasets for imitation learning and enables a systematic study of how data generation and policy learning decisions impact model performance. We show that whole-body visuomotor policies co-trained with data generated by HumanoidMimicGen outperform those trained only on real-world data by 20%.

AI.tldr: HumanoidMimicGen automatically generates large-scale whole-body loco-manipulation demonstration data for humanoid robots by adapting contact-rich skills from a few source demos, enabling imitation learning that outperforms real-data-only training by 20%.
AI.motivation: Imitation learning for humanoid robots requires massive demonstrations that are hard to collect via teleoperation; existing auto-synthesis methods work for manipulators but fail on humanoids due to their high-dimensional composite action spaces (arms, legs, torso).
AI.method: HumanoidMimicGen adapts contact-rich whole-body skills from a handful of source demonstrations to new states (generalizing across object pose changes), interleaving single/dual-arm skills with whole-body locomotion and manipulation planning to generate stable, collision-free data across diverse scenes.
AI.result: In a new simulated loco-manipulation benchmark with 9 diverse tasks, whole-body visuomotor policies co-trained with HumanoidMimicGen data outperform those trained only on real-world data by 20%.
AI.conclusion: Data generation for humanoid loco-manipulation can be automated effectively, and co-training with synthetic data significantly boosts imitation learning performance, enabling systematic study of how data generation and policy learning decisions affect model performance.

title: Hallucination Behavior in Multimodal LLMs Across Agricultural Image Interpretation and Generation Tasks
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are being rapidly adopted in agricultural imaging applications, ranging from crop interpretation to synthetic field image generation. However, these models frequently exhibit hallucinations outputs that appear confident yet deviate from biological or environmental reality potentially leading to misinformed agronomic insights. This study investigates such hallucinations in two complementary directions: image-to-text, where LLMs interpret crop or field imagery to describe conditions such as biotic and abiotic stresses, and text-to-image, where models generate synthetic agricultural scenes based on descriptive prompts. We examine errors involving biological inconsistency, contextual inaccuracy, and agronomic implausibility, evaluating the outputs under domain-informed criteria across multiple imaging modalities. Our analysis identifies recurring hallucination patterns within both interpretive and generative tasks. In image interpretation, LLMs (e.g., Gemma, LLAVA, Qwen, and MiniCPM) achieved modest zero-shot accuracy (63 to 75 percent), whereas few-shot prompting improved performance up to 86.8 percent, exhibiting false detections and missed infections, indicating residual hallucination effects. In text-to-image tasks, advanced models such as GPT-5 and Gemini 2.5 Flash generate up to 91 percent biologically inconsistent scenes under relaxed prompt constraints, revealing fundamental weaknesses in current LLMs. This systematic assessment of visual reasoning and generation offers critical insights toward enhancing the reliability and trustworthiness of LLM-based agricultural imaging platforms.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically investigates hallucinations in LLMs applied to agricultural imaging, covering both image-to-text (crop interpretation) and text-to-image (synthetic field generation) tasks. It finds that while few-shot prompting improves accuracy up to 86.8%, models still exhibit biological inconsistencies, false detections, and agronomic implausibility. In text-to-image, advanced models like GPT-5 and Gemini 2.5 Flash produce up to 91% biologically inconsistent scenes.
AI.motivation: LLMs are being deployed in agricultural imaging, but they frequently hallucinate — producing confident yet biologically or environmentally inaccurate outputs, which can misinform agronomic decisions. The paper aims to assess and characterize these hallucinations systematically.
AI.method: The study evaluates hallucinations in two directions: (1) image-to-text (crop/field interpretation for biotic/abiotic stresses) using models like Gemma, LLAVA, Qwen, and MiniCPM under zero-shot and few-shot settings; (2) text-to-image (synthetic agricultural scene generation) using advanced models like GPT-5 and Gemini 2.5 Flash. Errors are classified into biological inconsistency, contextual inaccuracy, and agronomic implausibility across multiple imaging modalities.
AI.result: In image-to-text, zero-shot accuracy ranged from 63% to 75%, while few-shot prompting improved accuracy to 86.8%, though residual hallucinations (false detections, missed infections) persisted. In text-to-image tasks, GPT-5 and Gemini 2.5 Flash generated up to 91% biologically inconsistent scenes under relaxed prompt constraints.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs exhibit fundamental weaknesses in both visual reasoning and generation for agricultural imaging, highlighting critical reliability gaps that must be addressed before deploying such platforms in real-world agronomic applications.

title: Resource-Constrained Affect Modelling via Variance Regularisation Pruning
comment: This paper has been accepted at the 2026 PErvasive Technologies Related to Assistive Environments (PETRA)
summary: Affective computing systems are increasingly embedded in pervasive and interactive environments, such as adaptive games, assistive technologies, and resource-constrained platforms, where computational efficiency must be balanced with reliability across diverse users. Model pruning offers an effective way to reduce computational demands, yet existing approaches typically optimise for sparsity alone, without accounting for how parameter removal impacts robustness across individuals. In this work, we introduce Variance-Regularised Pruning (VR), a pruning framework that explicitly incorporates cross-participant stability into the sparsification process. Rather than relying solely on average prediction error, VR evaluates each connection based on its joint contribution to both prediction accuracy and variability across users, prioritising parameters that remain reliable under distributional differences. We evaluate the proposed approach on the AGAIN dataset, which includes arousal annotations collected across nine affect-eliciting game environments. Experimental results demonstrate that VR maintains competitive Concordance Correlation Coefficient (CCC) performance even at 80\% sparsity without additional fine-tuning, highlighting its suitability for deployment in real-world, resource-limited affect-aware systems. Overall, the proposed framework supports the development of compact, robust affective models that can operate reliably in real-world interactive environments.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Variance-Regularised Pruning (VR), a pruning framework that incorporates cross-participant stability into model sparsification for affective computing. VR evaluates each connection based on both prediction accuracy and variability across users, achieving competitive performance at 80% sparsity on the AGAIN dataset without fine-tuning, enabling compact and robust affective models for resource-limited environments.
AI.motivation: Existing model pruning approaches optimise for sparsity alone and fail to account for how parameter removal impacts robustness across individual users, which is critical for affective computing systems deployed in diverse, real-world interactive environments.
AI.method: VR is a pruning framework that evaluates each connection based on its joint contribution to both prediction accuracy and variability across users, prioritising parameters that remain reliable under distributional differences rather than relying solely on average prediction error.
AI.result: On the AGAIN dataset (arousal annotations from nine affect-eliciting games), VR maintains competitive CCC performance even at 80% sparsity without additional fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: VR supports the development of compact, robust affective models that can operate reliably in real-world, resource-limited interactive environments.

title: Voluntary Collusion with Secret Tools in Competing LLM Agents
summary: Even when a tool is explicitly described as unfair and harmful to others, ostensibly safety-aligned LLM agents still voluntarily engage in secret collusion whenever doing so confers a strategic advantage. To investigate this phenomenon, we introduce an empirical framework built on two strategic multi-agent environments: Liar's Bar, a competitive deception scenario, and Cleanup, a mixed-motive resource-management scenario, in which agents are offered secret collusion tools that provide significant advantages while clearly disadvantaging the other agents. Across 12 models (at the 7B, 70B, and proprietary scales) and 6 prompt variants, we find that most agents consistently accept these tools and develop collusive strategies, while explicitly acknowledging the unfairness of the tools before accepting. We further show that neither the unfairness labels nor baseline alignment alone reliably deters collusion: only explicit ethical framing reduces adoption and, even then, smaller models remain susceptible. More broadly, our work presents the first systematic investigation of voluntary collusion adoption in LLM-based multi-agent systems, and suggests that preventing such behaviour requires explicit safeguards rather than reliance on general alignment.

AI.tldr: LLM agents voluntarily accept unfair collusion tools for strategic advantage, even when explicitly told they are unfair and harmful. Across 12 models, most engage in collusion despite acknowledging the unfairness, and only explicit ethical framing partially deters this behavior.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether safety-aligned LLM agents engage in secret collusion when it provides a strategic advantage, even when the tools are explicitly described as unfair and harmful to others.
AI.method: Built two multi-agent environments (Liar's Bar and Cleanup) offering secret collusion tools with clear unfairness labels. Tested across 12 models (7B, 70B, proprietary) and 6 prompt variants, measuring tool acceptance and collusion behavior.
AI.result: Most agents consistently accepted unfair tools and developed collusive strategies while acknowledging the unfairness. Neither unfairness labels nor baseline alignment reliably deterred collusion; only explicit ethical framing reduced adoption, but smaller models remained susceptible.
AI.conclusion: This is the first systematic investigation of voluntary collusion in LLM multi-agent systems, suggesting that preventing such behavior requires explicit safeguards rather than relying on general alignment.

title: A Unified Framework for Unbiased Non-Coherent Over-the-Air Computation
comment: Accepted to IEEE Transactions on Communications
summary: Over-the-Air Computation (OAC) enables efficient data aggregation in large-scale distributed systems by exploiting the superposition property of wireless multiple-access channels. In contrast to most existing studies on OAC assuming exact channel state information, we consider non-coherent OAC (NC-OAC) where the channel phase is unknown at the transmitters. A three-step framework for NC-OAC with a mapping between source data and codewords is proposed: 1) Devices encode their data to non-negative codewords; 2) Devices transmit a sequence of symbols with amplitude proportional to their codewords, such that the receiver can estimate the codeword sum. Estimation of the codeword sum is studied under two scenarios of global channel amplitude knowledge: statistical or instantaneous; 3) The estimated codeword sum is decoded to the desired source data sum at the receiver. With the proposed framework, we first study prior work on NC-OAC and map these to the framework. Next, we define and compare the two most commonly (often implicitly) used mappings for NC-OAC: the Affine and the Augmented Affine mappings. Under the constraint of unbiased estimation, we show that with uniformly distributed data and standard channel assumptions, the Augmented Affine mapping exhibits an order of magnitude lower estimation variance than the Affine mapping with both statistical and instantaneous channel knowledge. This result is validated by extensive simulations. Finally, we propose and analyze a new mapping, which demonstrates superior performance over the previous two affine mappings.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a three-step framework for non-coherent Over-the-Air Computation (NC-OAC) where channel phase is unknown, analyzes existing affine mappings, and introduces a new mapping that achieves superior performance with lower estimation variance.
AI.motivation: Most OAC studies assume exact channel state information (CSI), but in practice, channel phase is often unknown at transmitters. The authors aim to address this gap by studying non-coherent OAC (NC-OAC) under phase uncertainty.
AI.method: Proposes a three-step NC-OAC framework: (1) devices encode data to non-negative codewords; (2) devices transmit amplitude-proportional symbols for receiver to estimate the codeword sum under statistical or instantaneous channel amplitude knowledge; (3) receiver decodes the estimated sum to obtain the desired data sum. Affine and Augmented Affine mappings are formally defined and compared, and a new mapping is proposed.
AI.result: Under unbiased estimation with uniformly distributed data and standard channel assumptions, the Augmented Affine mapping shows an order of magnitude lower estimation variance than the Affine mapping for both statistical and instantaneous channel knowledge. The newly proposed mapping demonstrates superior performance over both affine mappings.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses non-coherent OAC, and the new mapping offers significant improvements over existing approaches, validated by simulations.

title: Colosseum V2: Benchmarking Generalization for Vision Language Action Models
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models demonstrate promising generalization in robotic manipulation, driven by advances in large-scale vision and language pre-training. This progress can be misleading. Despite the zero-shot perception and language capabilities of VLAs, their overall task performance often degrades under distribution shifts, revealing gaps in how these systems translate high-level understanding into robust behavior. To systematically study this gap, we introduce Colosseum V2, a large-scale simulation benchmark for evaluating VLA generalization in robot learning across diverse conditions. The benchmark comprises 28 tasks spanning 13 task categories and two robot morphologies, covering a wide range of manipulation primitives and long-horizon behaviors. Built on the ManiSkill simulator, Colosseum V2 enables fast, GPU-parallelized evaluation and supports both in-domain and out-of-domain testing at scale. We evaluate state-of-the-art methods, including Action Chunking Transformers (ACT) and Pi0.5, and reveal limitations in both base performance and generalization. We demonstrate strong correlations between simulation and real-world metrics that support the ecological validity of the benchmark. By standardizing tasks, metrics, and evaluation protocols within a unified benchmark, Colosseum V2 enables reproducible and fair comparisons, reduced evaluation overhead, and accelerated progress toward general-purpose robot policies.

AI.tldr: Colosseum V2 is a large-scale simulation benchmark for evaluating generalization of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models in robotic manipulation, revealing that despite impressive zero-shot capabilities, VLAs suffer performance degradation under distribution shifts.
AI.motivation: Current VLA models show promising generalization in robotic manipulation but their task performance degrades under distribution shifts despite zero-shot perception and language abilities. There is a need to systematically study the gap between high-level understanding and robust behavior.
AI.method: Colosseum V2 benchmark with 28 tasks across 13 categories and two robot morphologies, built on ManiSkill simulator with GPU-parallelized evaluation, supporting both in-domain and out-of-domain testing. Evaluates state-of-the-art methods including ACT and Pi0.5.
AI.result: State-of-the-art methods (ACT, Pi0.5) show limitations in both base performance and generalization. Strong correlations between simulation and real-world metrics confirm ecological validity.
AI.conclusion: Colosseum V2 enables standardized, reproducible, and fair comparisons with reduced evaluation overhead, accelerating progress toward general-purpose robot policies.

title: Not All NVFP4 QAT Recipes Are Equal: How Architecture and Scale Shape Model Quality for Anomaly Segmentation
summary: Real-time anomaly segmentation demands both high recall and efficient low-precision inference. We study the three-way interaction of model architecture, model scale, and FP4 quantization-aware training (QAT) recipe on a recall-critical brain tumor segmentation task, evaluating multiple architectures, scales, and QAT recipes under a unified protocol. We find that architecture choice has the largest impact on quantization robustness, with attention-based architectures showing remarkable resilience to recipe choice while CNN degrades under gradient-quantizing recipes at larger scales. At low capacity, FP4 can discretize softmax attention, but advanced QAT recipes prevent this collapse. At larger scales, advanced recipes mitigate gradient quantization noise that degrades CNN quality. Five-fold patient-level cross-validation confirms these findings are robust to data partition. Our results show that the Swin Transformer is robust to QAT recipe choice across all scales, making it the recommended architecture for FP4-quantized anomaly segmentation.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how model architecture, scale, and FP4 quantization-aware training (QAT) recipes interact for real-time brain tumor segmentation, finding that attention-based architectures (especially Swin Transformer) are most robust to quantization, while CNNs degrade under gradient-quantizing recipes at larger scales.
AI.motivation: Real-time anomaly segmentation demands both high recall and efficient low-precision (FP4) inference; the paper investigates the three-way interaction of architecture, scale, and QAT recipe to understand which factors most impact quantization robustness.
AI.method: The authors systematically evaluate multiple architectures (including attention-based and CNN), model scales, and FP4 QAT recipes under a unified protocol on a brain tumor segmentation task, using five-fold patient-level cross-validation.
AI.result: Architecture choice has the largest impact; attention-based models are resilient to recipe choice, while CNNs degrade under gradient-quantizing recipes at larger scales. Advanced QAT recipes prevent softmax attention collapse at low capacity and mitigate gradient noise for CNNs at larger scales.
AI.conclusion: The Swin Transformer is robust to QAT recipe choice across all scales, making it the recommended architecture for FP4-quantized anomaly segmentation.

title: Energy-Structured Low-Rank Adaptation for Continual Learning
summary: While orthogonal subspace methods try to mitigate task interference in Continual Learning (CL), they often suffer from energy diffusion across the basis, hindering knowledge compaction and exhausting capacity for future tasks. We observe that output feature drift induced by parameter updates is inherently low-rank, and theoretically prove that preserving parameters along the principal directions of this drift minimizes the output reconstruction error. Motivated by this, we propose \textbf{E}nergy-Concentrated and \textbf{E}nergy-Ordered \textbf{Lo}w-\textbf{R}ank \textbf{A}daptation (E$^2$-LoRA). By explicitly ordering and concentrating knowledge into leading ranks, E$^2$-LoRA frees capacity for subsequent tasks. Furthermore, we design a dynamic rank allocation strategy to balance stability and plasticity by jointly optimizing energy retention and model plasticity. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks demonstrate that E$^2$-LoRA achieves state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: E²-LoRA improves continual learning by concentrating knowledge into leading rank directions via low-rank adaptation, freeing capacity for new tasks and achieving SOTA performance.
AI.motivation: Orthogonal subspace methods in CL suffer from energy diffusion across bases, wasting capacity. The paper aims to concentrate knowledge into fewer ranks to preserve capacity for future tasks.
AI.method: E²-LoRA (Energy-Concentrated & Energy-Ordered Low-Rank Adaptation): explicitly orders and concentrates knowledge into leading ranks, plus a dynamic rank allocation strategy balancing stability and plasticity via joint optimization of energy retention and model plasticity.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance across multiple CL benchmarks; theoretically proven that preserving parameters along principal drift directions minimizes output reconstruction error.
AI.conclusion: E²-LoRA effectively mitigates task interference and capacity exhaustion in CL by concentrating knowledge into principal low-rank directions, outperforming existing methods.

title: Laguna M.1/XS.2 Technical Report
comment: Technical report to models released here: https://poolside.ai/blog/introducing-laguna-xs2-m1
summary:
We present Laguna M.1 and Laguna XS.2, two Mixture-of-Experts foundation models built for long-horizon, agentic coding: M.1 has $225.8$B total parameters ($23.4$B activated per token) and XS.2 has $33.4$B total ($3$B activated). Both models were trained from scratch end-to-end inside the same internal system that we refer to as our Model Factory: a tightly-integrated stack of versioned data, training, evaluation, and inference components that turn model development into an industrial process. We describe the principles and design choices of the Model Factory and also detail the end-to-end training process of our models, throughout pre-training data and architecture, post-training stages, evaluation, and quantization.
  On agentic software engineering and terminal benchmarks (SWE-bench Verified, SWE-bench Multilingual, SWE-Bench Pro, and Terminal-Bench 2.0) M.1 and XS.2 are competitive with state-of-the-art open models in their respective weight classes. Laguna XS.2 weights are released under Apache~2.0 at https://huggingface.co/collections/poolside/laguna-xs2.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Laguna M.1 (225.8B total, 23.4B activated) and Laguna XS.2 (33.4B total, 3B activated), two Mixture-of-Experts models built for long-horizon agentic coding, trained end-to-end in an internal "Model Factory" system. XS.2 weights are released under Apache 2.0.
AI.motivation: To develop specialized foundation models for long-horizon, agentic coding tasks and establish an industrial-grade "Model Factory" pipeline that systematically integrates versioned data, training, evaluation, and inference for reproducible model development.
AI.method: The authors built a tightly-integrated "Model Factory" stack with versioned data, training, evaluation, and inference components. Both models are Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures trained from scratch end-to-end. The paper describes pre-training data/architecture choices, post-training stages, evaluation, and quantization.
AI.result: On SWE-bench Verified, SWE-bench Multilingual, SWE-Bench Pro, and Terminal-Bench 2.0, both M.1 and XS.2 are competitive with state-of-the-art open models in their respective weight classes.
AI.conclusion: The Model Factory approach enables systematic development of competitive coding-specialized MoE models. Laguna XS.2 is released open-source under Apache 2.0, while M.1 is the larger variant.

title: Channel Measurements and Characterization with Phase Drift Compensation for Outdoor 330-360 GHz MIMO Communications
summary: In this paper, an outdoor channel measurement campaign at 330-360 GHz employing a 128 * 4 virtual antenna array (VAA)-based multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) configuration is conducted. The transmitter (Tx) and receiver (Rx) location pairs are classified into line-of-sight (LoS) and obstructed-LoS (OLoS) scenarios to enable a detailed investigation of outdoor terahertz (THz) band channel characteristics. During the measurement process, the stationarity of the outdoor environment is carefully verified, and a linear phase drift (PD) effect is identified. Then, we propose a PD-aware Space-Alternating Generalized Expectation-Maximization (SAGE) algorithm, which significantly improves both delay resolution and channel parameter estimation accuracy. Based on the processed measurement data, we characterize key channel properties, including the power delay profile, path loss, shadow fading, delay spread, angular spread, Rician K-factor, as well as their cumulative distribution functions and correlation characteristics. In addition, near-field effects and MIMO-specific properties, including the spatial non-stationarity and the cluster birth-death property, are analyzed.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a 330-360 GHz outdoor MIMO channel measurement campaign using a 128×4 virtual antenna array, proposes a PD-aware SAGE algorithm to correct linear phase drift, and characterizes key THz channel properties including near-field effects and spatial non-stationarity.
AI.motivation: To fill the gap in understanding outdoor terahertz band channel characteristics with MIMO configurations, which is essential for future 6G communication system design.
AI.method: Conducted outdoor channel measurements at 330-360 GHz using a 128×4 VAA-based MIMO setup; classified Tx-Rx pairs into LoS and OLoS scenarios; verified environmental stationarity; proposed a PD-aware SAGE algorithm to handle linear phase drift and improve estimation accuracy.
AI.result: Identified and corrected linear phase drift effect; characterized power delay profile, path loss, shadow fading, delay/angular spread, Rician K-factor, CDFs, and correlations; analyzed near-field effects, spatial non-stationarity, and cluster birth-death properties.
AI.conclusion: The proposed PD-aware SAGE algorithm significantly improves delay resolution and parameter estimation accuracy; the comprehensive channel characterization provides valuable insights for outdoor THz MIMO system design.

title: Turning Video Models into Generalist Robot Policies
summary: Video generative models have emerged as a promising robotics backbone, capable of generating videos that depict the completion of complex tasks across embodiments and environments. Recent work proposes robot foundation models that jointly predict future observations and actions by finetuning video models with action-labeled data. In this paper, we test the limits of an alternative approach: leave the video planner as-is while training an embodiment-specific inverse dynamics model (IDM). This decoupling offers several natural benefits: the video planner remains embodiment-agnostic, different video models can be interchanged easily without re-training the IDM, and the IDM can be independently trained with readily available self-play data. We present a closed-loop, video-to-action policy that combines an action-free video world model with a carefully-designed IDM based on the robot embodiment Jacobian. We demonstrate that our IDM design is both data-efficient and scalable to high-dimensional action spaces. Our policy, which we coin the Video-to-Embodied Robot Action Model (VERA), achieves strong performance across simulated and real-world benchmarks, including zero-shot Panda arm manipulation and 16-DoF Allegro-hand dexterous cube re-orientation. The same video planner can be used across multiple embodiments by pairing it with different embodiment-specific IDMs. Our results show that decoupled video planning plus faithful video-to-action translation is a viable alternative route towards zero-shot, cross-embodiment, and generalizable robot control. More results are available on our project website: https://vera.csail.mit.edu.

AI.tldr: VERA decouples video planning from action execution by using an action-free video world model plus an embodiment-specific inverse dynamics model, enabling zero-shot cross-embodiment robot control without retraining the video planner.
AI.motivation: To explore whether a decoupled approach—leaving the video planner as-is while training embodiment-specific inverse dynamics models—can achieve zero-shot, cross-embodiment robot control, avoiding the need to finetune video models with action-labeled data for each embodiment.
AI.method: VERA combines an action-free video world model (frozen) with a carefully-designed IDM based on the robot embodiment Jacobian. The IDM translates generated video frames into robot actions, trained independently with self-play data. The video planner remains embodiment-agnostic, allowing different IDMs to pair with the same planner.
AI.result: Strong performance on simulated and real-world benchmarks, including zero-shot Panda arm manipulation and 16-DoF Allegro-hand dexterous cube re-orientation. Same video planner works across multiple embodiments with different IDMs.
AI.conclusion: Decoupled video planning plus faithful video-to-action translation is a viable alternative for zero-shot, cross-embodiment, generalizable robot control.

title: Tensor Memory: Fixed-Size Recurrent State for Long-Horizon Transformers
summary: Transformers process images and videos by flattening space and time into long token sequences. While attention and KV caching preserve past features, their memory grows with sequence length and they lack an explicit, persistent spatial state, making long-horizon video understanding and occlusion-sensitive reasoning difficult. We propose Tensor Memory, a lightweight module that augments Transformer blocks with a fixed-size recurrent 3D memory tensor: tokens write into a voxel grid via a differentiable soft write that deposits content as a Gaussian-weighted volume around a predicted continuous 3D location, the memory is updated with an efficient local interaction operator and gated recurrent dynamics, and tokens read back context via continuous sampling with gated residual fusion. Because the memory tensor has a constant size, Tensor Memory decouples state capacity from input length while preserving a spatial inductive bias. We evaluate the module on standard language, image, and video benchmarks and on a controlled toy diagnostic suite designed to isolate when persistent state is beneficial; it integrates with standard Transformer training pipelines and can be attached to or removed from existing blocks without other architectural changes.

AI.tldr: Tensor Memory augments Transformers with a fixed-size 3D recurrent memory tensor that uses soft writes, local interactions, and gated residual fusion to enable persistent spatial state without scaling memory with sequence length.
AI.motivation: Transformers lack an explicit, persistent spatial state; their KV cache grows with sequence length, which limits long-horizon video understanding and occlusion-sensitive reasoning.
AI.method: A fixed-size 3D voxel grid memory is introduced. Tokens write into the grid via Gaussian-weighted soft writes, memory is updated with local interaction operators and gated recurrent dynamics, and tokens read back context via continuous sampling with gated residual fusion.
AI.result: Evaluated on language, image, and video benchmarks plus a controlled toy diagnostic suite; integrates with standard Transformer training and can be attached/removed without other architectural changes.
AI.conclusion: Tensor Memory decouples state capacity from input length while preserving spatial inductive bias, improving long-horizon and occlusion-sensitive tasks.

title: Federated Learning for Multivariate Time Series Anomaly Detection in Industrial Automation
comment: Preprint. Accepted at the DEXA International Workshop on Optimisation of Industrial Production with AI Algorithms 2026 (DEXA AI4IP 2026)
summary: Federated learning (FL) has broadened the horizon for multivariate time series anomaly detection (MTSAD). However, benchmarking such anomaly detection methods within FL paradigm poses data-centric challenges. The existing datasets do not counteract these challenges since they do not simultaneously provide sufficient scale, accurate labels, and freedom from common flaws. In addition, the role of cyclic process behavior, which is common in discrete industrial automation, remains underexplored for MTSAD for the current state of research. This paper aims to shed more light on the literature and address these gaps by introducing a dataset designed with cyclic dynamics arising from the repetitive nature of discrete automation processes and evaluates selected MTSAD methods on both the proposed dataset and a public benchmark dataset.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a new multivariate time series anomaly detection dataset with cyclic dynamics for federated learning, addressing limitations of existing datasets, and evaluates selected methods on both the proposed and a public benchmark dataset.
AI.motivation: Existing datasets for federated learning-based multivariate time series anomaly detection lack sufficient scale, accurate labels, and are free from common flaws. Additionally, cyclic process behavior in discrete industrial automation is underexplored.
AI.method: The authors introduce a new dataset featuring cyclic dynamics from repetitive discrete automation processes and evaluate selected MTSAD methods on both the proposed dataset and a public benchmark.
AI.result: Evaluation results comparing selected MTSAD methods on both the proposed cyclic dataset and a public benchmark dataset.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dataset addresses key gaps in FL-based MTSAD by providing cyclic dynamics, and the evaluation sheds light on how existing methods perform under these conditions.

title: Reasoning and Planning with Dynamically Changing Norms
comment: 8 pages, 1 figure, dataset included in anc
summary: To safely interact with humans, AI agents must both know our norms and consider them during planning. However, such norm-guided planning has been less explored, only within communities of artificial agents, and has ignored the dynamic nature of norms. This paper instead presents an approach to guiding planning with dynamically changing norms in a human-AI setting. We contribute a defeasible calculus for resolving normative conflicts and an approach to using such dynamically changing norms as guard rails on plans. We theoretically demonstrate our approach with formal proofs and empirically with an AI agent, SocialBot, on a natural language dialogue task.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an approach for AI agents to use dynamically changing social norms as guard rails during planning, with formal proofs and an empirical demonstration on a dialogue task.
AI.motivation: AI agents need to understand and follow human norms for safe interaction, but prior work on norm-guided planning ignored dynamic norms and focused only on artificial agent communities, not human-AI settings.
AI.method: The authors propose a defeasible calculus for resolving normative conflicts and a method to use dynamically changing norms as guard rails on plans, demonstrated through formal proofs and an AI agent called SocialBot on a natural language dialogue task.
AI.result: Theoretical formal proofs validate the approach, and empirical results with SocialBot on a dialogue task demonstrate its effectiveness in practice.
AI.conclusion: Dynamically changing norms can be effectively integrated into AI planning for human-AI interaction, using defeasible reasoning to resolve conflicts and norms as guard rails.

title: On Unified CRLB Framework from Generic Signals to ISAC Waveforms with Virtual Array Sensing
summary: This paper presents a unified Cramér-Rao lower bound (CRLB) framework for signal-level parameters in integrated sensing and communications (ISAC)-enabled radar systems. Starting from the generic signal model, we analyze the coupling between delay and Doppler in the Fisher information matrix (FIM), which is unsolved and often overlooked in relevant studies. Addressing this issue, we derive the conditions under which the coupling terms can be eliminated and demonstrate that these conditions are typically satisfied for ISAC-enabled waveforms. Afterward, the CRLBs of representative ISAC waveforms are derived within the unified framework, enabling consistent and comparable analysis across the waveforms and avoiding model-dependent discrepancies. Further, the framework is extended to virtual array (VA) sensing systems, where the impact of different multiplexing schemes is analyzed. Simulation results demonstrate the consistency between the CRLBs derived from the proposed framework and those obtained from waveform-specific analyses. The proposed framework shows strong generality, waveform-compatibility, and flexibility, offering a versatile tool for the CRLB analysis of various waveforms, including those lacking existing analytical results.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a unified CRLB framework for ISAC radar systems, addressing the coupling between delay and Doppler in the FIM, deriving conditions for decoupling, and extending the analysis to various ISAC waveforms and virtual array sensing systems.
AI.motivation: The coupling between delay and Doppler in the Fisher information matrix (FIM) is often overlooked in existing studies. A unified framework is needed to enable consistent, comparable CRLB analysis across different ISAC waveforms and avoid model-dependent discrepancies.
AI.method: The authors start from a generic signal model, analyze delay-Doppler coupling in the FIM, derive decoupling conditions, and build a unified CRLB framework. They then apply it to representative ISAC waveforms and extend it to virtual array (VA) sensing with different multiplexing schemes.
AI.result: Simulation results confirm that CRLBs derived from the proposed framework are consistent with waveform-specific analyses. The framework demonstrates strong generality, waveform-compatibility, and flexibility for analyzing existing and new waveforms.
AI.conclusion: The proposed unified CRLB framework is a versatile tool for analyzing various ISAC waveforms, including those lacking existing analytical results, and supports consistent performance evaluation across radar sensing systems.

title: Tabero: Learning Gentle Manipulation with Closed-Loop Force Feedback from Vision, Touch, and Language
summary: Tactile sensing is essential for robots to achieve human-like gentle manipulation. However, existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models struggle to exploit tactile feedback for gentle manipulation due to scarce aligned vision-tactile-language data and the lack of effective closed-loop force feedback mechanisms. To address these challenges, we introduce Tabero, a benchmark and model suite for gentle, language-conditioned robotic manipulation that demands fine-grained contact force perception. First, the Tabero benchmark addresses the scarcity of tactile data by presenting a data-efficient pipeline that repurposes open-source robot manipulation trajectories to generate diverse vision-tactile-language tasks, and establishes a multidimensional evaluation protocol that measures task success alongside physical interaction quality. Second, we propose Tabero-VTLA, an architecture with a decoupled force-position command interface; the resulting force-position commands are executed by a fixed hybrid controller to enable real-time, force-aware manipulation. Evaluated on Tabero, our model maintains high task success while reducing average grip force by over 70\% under gentle instructions, demonstrating its ability to modulate interaction forces based on multimodal experience. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/NathanWu7/Tabero.

AI.tldr: Tabero is a benchmark and model suite for gentle, language-conditioned robotic manipulation that leverages a data-efficient pipeline to generate vision-tactile-language tasks and a decoupled force-position command architecture (Tabero-VTLA) to reduce grip forces by over 70% while maintaining task success.
AI.motivation: Existing VLA models struggle to exploit tactile feedback for gentle manipulation due to scarce aligned vision-tactile-language data and lack of effective closed-loop force feedback mechanisms.
AI.method: 1) A data-efficient pipeline that repurposes open-source robot manipulation trajectories to generate diverse vision-tactile-language tasks. 2) Tabero-VTLA architecture with a decoupled force-position command interface executed by a fixed hybrid controller for real-time force-aware manipulation.
AI.result: The model maintains high task success while reducing average grip force by over 70% under gentle instructions.
AI.conclusion: Tabero demonstrates the ability to modulate interaction forces based on multimodal experience, addressing the scarcity of tactile data and enabling gentle robotic manipulation through language-conditioned control.

title: Structure over Pixels: Learning Variable-Length Visual Programs
summary: Discrete visual tokenizers translate images into ordered sequences of codes, providing a natural representation for structural description of scenes. Yet existing adaptive tokenizers either require post-hoc search or select among a discrete set of pre-trained rates, rather than learning a continuous per-image sequence length coupled to the model and scene, and they typically train against pixel reconstruction, emphasizing texture rather than structure. We propose STROP, a discrete visual tokenizer architecture that forms structural scene representations and simultaneously learns how long an image's visual program should be. Using a four-phase curriculum supervised by local rate--distortion probes against frozen DINOv3 features, STROP optimizes a dedicated length head that estimates the active prefix length in a single forward pass. By bypassing pixel-level reconstruction gradients, the codebook is shaped entirely by the quality of higher-level latent representations. Program length grows with scene complexity, and signs of compositional structure emerge both in downstream dense-prediction transfer and in direct inspection of the learned code vocabulary.

AI.tldr: STROP is a discrete visual tokenizer that learns to dynamically adjust its sequence length per image in a single forward pass, using a curriculum supervised by frozen DINOv3 features instead of pixel reconstruction, enabling structural scene representations that grow with scene complexity.
AI.motivation: Existing adaptive visual tokenizers either require costly post-hoc search or select among a fixed set of pre-trained rates, failing to learn a continuous, per-image sequence length that adapts to both the model and scene. They also rely on pixel reconstruction, which emphasizes texture over structural understanding.
AI.method: STROP uses a four-phase curriculum with local rate-distortion probes supervised by frozen DINOv3 features (bypassing pixel reconstruction). A dedicated length head estimates the active prefix length in a single forward pass, and the codebook is shaped by higher-level latent representation quality.
AI.result: Program length grows with scene complexity, and signs of compositional structure emerge both in downstream dense-prediction transfer and direct inspection of the learned code vocabulary.
AI.conclusion: Learning continuous per-image sequence lengths coupled to scene complexity, guided by high-level features rather than pixel reconstruction, yields more structural and compositional visual representations suitable for downstream tasks.

title: GenSBI: Generative Methods for Simulation-Based Inference in JAX
comment: 48 pages + 1 appendix, 33 figures, 18 tables. For the associated Python code, see https://github.com/aurelio-amerio/GenSBI
summary: Flow and diffusion generative models have established themselves as widely adopted density estimators for simulation-based inference (SBI), extending naturally from neural posterior estimation to likelihood and joint density estimation. Their principled optimization objectives and freedom from architectural constraints have driven rapid adoption across the natural sciences. Yet the most widely used SBI libraries remain PyTorch-based, leaving researchers who develop their forward models and analysis pipelines in JAX without a native option. We present GenSBI, an open-source library that implements flow matching, score matching, and denoising diffusion entirely in JAX. The library offers three transformer-based architectures - SimFormer, Flux1, and a novel Flux1Joint that extends gate-modulated transformer blocks to joint density estimation - all interchangeable through a unified interface that decouples generative method, neural backbone, and inference mode. GenSBI provides an end-to-end workflow from training through posterior calibration (SBC, TARP, LC2ST) and supports custom architectures with domain-specific embedding networks. We validate the framework on standard SBI benchmarks, achieving near-ideal mean C2ST scores (0.50-0.56, where 0.50 is ideal) on SBIBM tasks with minimal per-task tuning and well-calibrated posterior coverage across all tested configurations. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/aurelio-amerio/GenSBI.

AI.tldr: GenSBI is a JAX-based open-source library for simulation-based inference that implements flow matching, score matching, and denoising diffusion with transformer-based architectures, achieving near-ideal C2ST scores (0.50-0.56) on standard benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing SBI libraries are PyTorch-based, leaving JAX users without native options for integrating generative density estimators with their forward models and analysis pipelines.
AI.method: Implements flow matching, score matching, and denoising diffusion entirely in JAX with three transformer architectures (SimFormer, Flux1, and novel Flux1Joint for joint density estimation), unified interface decoupling generative method, backbone, and inference mode, plus posterior calibration tools.
AI.result: Achieves near-ideal mean C2ST scores (0.50-0.56) on SBIBM benchmarks with minimal per-task tuning and well-calibrated posterior coverage across all tested configurations.
AI.conclusion: GenSBI provides a native JAX solution for SBI that matches or exceeds existing PyTorch-based alternatives, with flexible architecture design and built-in calibration tools.

title: Intelligence as Managed Autonomy: Failure, Escalation, and Governance for Agentic AI Systems
summary: As autonomous and agentic AI systems scale in robotic and human-machine environments, managing hallucination and persistent but unjustified action remains an open challenge. Rather than attributing these failures solely to model or alignment limitations, this paper explores the architectural vulnerability of unbounded autonomy - the presumption that an agent should continue operating regardless of rising uncertainty. It introduces a theory of managed autonomy that defines intelligent behavior through the formal capacity to detect epistemic drift, suspend reasoning, attempt recovery, and ultimately surrender control when reliability diminishes. We instantiate this theory via the SMARt (Self-Managing Multi-tier Autonomous Reasoning with Regulated/Revoked transitions) model, a four-layer framework featuring Stable, Meta-cognitive, Assisted, and Regulated states. By developing a timed, guarded Petri net formulation, we establish theoretically bounded properties for the system, demonstrating how architecture can formally mandate escalation, constrain invalid outputs, and ensure governance reachability under specified conditions. We further analyze how incorporating domain-specific trigger sets across varied operational settings (e.g., healthcare, robotics, etc.) can systematically preserve safety, assuming completeness and soundness criteria are met. Because these triggers are designed to be adaptive, the SMARt model accommodates the safe, controlled expansion of an agent's operational scope over time. We conclude that formalizing failure management within the autonomy lifecycle is a crucial step toward realizing reliable and governed artificial intelligence.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes that AI hallucination and unjustified actions stem not just from model limits but from unbounded autonomy architecture. It introduces the SMARt model—a four-layer framework (Stable, Meta-cognitive, Assisted, Regulated) that formally manages uncertainty by detecting drift, suspending reasoning, attempting recovery, and surrendering control when reliability drops.
AI.motivation: Current AI systems lack formal mechanisms to handle rising uncertainty and persistent unjustified actions, leading to safety failures in autonomous systems. The authors argue this is an architectural problem of unbounded autonomy, not merely a model or alignment issue.
AI.method: The paper develops a theory of managed autonomy with four cognitive states (Stable, Meta-cognitive, Assisted, Regulated). This is instantiated as the SMARt model using a timed, guarded Petri net formulation to formally enforce escalation, constrain invalid outputs, and ensure governance reachability.
AI.result: Theoretically bounded properties are established showing the architecture can formally mandate escalation, constrain invalid outputs, and ensure governance reachability under specified conditions. Domain-specific adaptive triggers across settings (healthcare, robotics) can systematically preserve safety if completeness and soundness criteria are met.
AI.conclusion: Formalizing failure management within the autonomy lifecycle—including explicit surrender of control—is a crucial step toward reliable and governed AI. The SMARt model enables safe, controlled expansion of operational scope over time.

title: Unified Analytical Framework for SPAD Array Receivers with Dead-Time-Induced Blocking Loss and Inter-Symbol Interference in PAM-OWC Systems
summary: Optical wireless communication (OWC) leveraging single-photon avalanche diode (SPAD) arrays offers exceptional sensitivity for photon-starving links. However, the inherent dead time of SPADs critically limits achievable data rates by introducing non-linear photon-counting distortions: blocking loss within a symbol duration and inter-symbol interference (ISI) across durations. This paper proposes a unified analytical framework capturing both distortions across all operational speed regimes for pulse-amplitude modulation (PAM), by establishing comprehensive statistical models for SPAD array receivers. For low and medium-speed systems (symbol duration longer than dead time), we derive exact closed-form expressions for the photon counts probability distribution using renewal theory, explicitly incorporating blocking loss and ISI. For high-speed systems (symbol duration shorter than dead time), we develop a Markov chain model characterizing the steady-state operational states and integrate it with trigger probability to obtain the exact binomial photon counts distribution. Furthermore, we propose low-complexity, near-optimal threshold detection schemes based on these models. This work provides essential theoretical tools for designing and optimizing high-performance SPAD-based OWC systems employing PAM.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a unified analytical framework for SPAD-based optical wireless communication with PAM, covering both blocking loss and inter-symbol interference across all speed regimes, and provides exact statistical models along with low-complexity threshold detection schemes.
AI.motivation: The dead time of SPAD arrays introduces non-linear photon-counting distortions (blocking loss and ISI) that critically limit data rates, and existing models do not capture both distortions comprehensively across all operational speed regimes.
AI.method: For low/medium-speed systems, renewal theory is used to derive exact closed-form photon count distributions; for high-speed systems, a Markov chain model combined with trigger probability yields exact binomial distributions. Low-complexity near-optimal threshold detection schemes are also developed.
AI.result: Exact closed-form expressions for photon counts probability distributions in low/medium-speed regimes, and exact binomial distributions via Markov chain modeling for high-speed regimes, enabling accurate performance characterization.
AI.conclusion: The work provides essential theoretical tools for designing and optimizing high-performance SPAD-based OWC systems employing PAM modulation.

title: S-Cheetah: A Novel Quadrupedal Robot with a 3-DOF Active Spine Learning Agile Locomotion
summary: The biological spine of quadrupeds enables sagittal flexion/extension, lateral bending, and axial rotation, playing a crucial role in highly agile and dexterous locomotion. While numerous studies have integrated active spinal joints into quadrupedal robots to enhance agility, most designs simplify control complexity by reducing spinal degrees of freedom (DOF), failing to achieve the spatial tri-axial rotation characteristic of biological spines. Consequently, replicating a multi-DOF biomimetic spine and effectively leveraging it to empower the agile locomotion of quadrupedal robots remains a significant research challenge. In this study, we present S-Cheetah, a quadrupedal robot featuring a 3-DOF bio-inspired serial active spine capable of biomimetic spatial tri-axial rotation. To empower the robot to fully utilize this active spine, we developed a specialized reinforcement learning framework to actively promote the engagement of the introduced spine and maximize the robot's locomotive capabilities by integrating an acceleration curriculum learning strategy with tailored reward functions, such as a gallop gait reward, a spine undulation reward, and a spine steering reward. Experimental results demonstrate that S-Cheetah can achieve a peak speed of 6.9 m/s using the rotary G2 gallop gait and an in-place turning rate of 7.2 rad/s. Besides, the system exhibits an emergent, feline-inspired aerial self-righting capability, allowing it to land stably on four feet from arbitrary orientations during free fall. Finally, through extensive evaluations across diverse locomotion tasks, we prove that the introduction of the proposed 3-DOF spine comprehensively enhances the locomotive agility of quadrupedal robots. Project website: himmy-robotics.github.io/scheetah

AI.tldr: S-Cheetah is a quadrupedal robot with a 3-DOF bio-inspired spine enabling spatial tri-axial rotation. Using a specialized reinforcement learning framework (acceleration curriculum learning, tailored reward functions), it achieves 6.9 m/s gallop, 7.2 rad/s turning, feline-inspired aerial righting, and demonstrates that the 3-DOF spine comprehensively enhances locomotive agility.
AI.motivation: Previous quadrupedal robots with active spines simplify control by reducing spinal DOF, failing to replicate the spatial tri-axial rotation of biological spines. The challenge is to build a multi-DOF biomimetic spine and effectively use it to enhance agile locomotion.
AI.method: A 3-DOF bio-inspired serial active spine enabling spatial tri-axial rotation is designed. A reinforcement learning framework with acceleration curriculum learning and tailored reward functions (gallop gait reward, spine undulation reward, spine steering reward) actively promotes spine engagement to maximize locomotion.
AI.result: Peak speed of 6.9 m/s (rotary G2 gallop), in-place turning rate of 7.2 rad/s, emergent feline-inspired aerial self-righting from arbitrary orientations, and comprehensive enhancement of locomotive agility across diverse tasks.
AI.conclusion: The 3-DOF bio-inspired spine comprehensively enhances quadrupedal robot agility. S-Cheetah demonstrates superior speed, turning, and aerial righting capabilities, validating the effectiveness of spatial tri-axial spinal rotation in robotic locomotion.

title: Asynchronous Remote Sensing Time-Series Fusion for Cloud Removal and Anytime Reconstruction
summary: Frequent cloud cover severely limits the usability of Sentinel-2 (S2) optical time series for Earth surface monitoring. Sentinel-1 (S1) SAR provides all-weather complementary observations, but practical S1/S2 fusion remains difficult because acquisitions are irregular and asynchronous. Many existing approaches assume temporally aligned inputs (or require external nearest-date matching) and typically restore only observed timestamps, limiting reconstruction under long gaps and preventing on-demand synthesis. We propose AGFlow (Time Aligned Generative Flow Matching), a spatiotemporal flow-matching model for S1/S2 cloud removal and time-series reconstruction with three capabilities: (1) timestamp-conditioned internal alignment that fuses asynchronous S1 and cloudy S2 observations without preprocessing-based pairing; (2) spatiotemporal, context-aware denoising that models spatial structure jointly with temporal dynamics (rather than independent per-pixel time series); and (3) anytime querying, enabling generation of cloud-free S2 frames at both observed and user-specified timestamps within the monitoring window. We evaluate on the RESTORE-DiT benchmark protocol with quantitative metrics, qualitative comparisons, and component ablations. AGFlow notably improves fully missing-frame reconstruction (MAE and RMSE reduce by 16-19% over RESTORE-DiT) and provides reliable reconstructions under persistent gaps, while also yielding competitive cloud removal performance and flexible temporal querying for downstream tasks such as dense vegetation monitoring.

AI.tldr: AGFlow is a generative flow-matching model that fuses asynchronous Sentinel-1 SAR and cloudy Sentinel-2 optical data for cloud removal and flexible time-series reconstruction without requiring pre-aligned inputs, achieving 16-19% improvement in missing-frame reconstruction over prior methods.
AI.motivation: Frequent cloud cover limits Sentinel-2 optical data usability, while Sentinel-1 SAR provides complementary all-weather observations. Existing fusion methods assume temporally aligned inputs or require external nearest-date matching, and typically only restore observed timestamps, limiting reconstruction under long gaps and preventing on-demand synthesis.
AI.method: AGFlow uses a spatiotemporal flow-matching model with three key capabilities: (1) timestamp-conditioned internal alignment to fuse asynchronous S1 and cloudy S2 without preprocessing-based pairing; (2) spatiotemporal, context-aware denoising that models spatial structure jointly with temporal dynamics; and (3) anytime querying for generating cloud-free S2 frames at both observed and user-specified timestamps.
AI.result: On the RESTORE-DiT benchmark, AGFlow improves fully missing-frame reconstruction (MAE and RMSE reduce by 16-19% over RESTORE-DiT), provides reliable reconstructions under persistent gaps, achieves competitive cloud removal performance, and enables flexible temporal querying for downstream tasks like dense vegetation monitoring.
AI.conclusion: AGFlow effectively addresses asynchronous S1/S2 fusion without preprocessing pairing, significantly outperforms prior methods on missing-frame reconstruction, and offers flexible anytime querying for on-demand cloud-free synthesis, which is valuable for applications such as vegetation monitoring.

title: SparseOpt: Addressing Normalization-induced Gradient Skew in Sparse Training
comment: Accepted International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML) 2026
summary: Dynamic Sparse Training (DST) methods train neural networks by maintaining sparsity while dynamically adapting the network topology.
