AI.result: Achieves superior optimization efficiency in white-box settings and sets new SOTA for cross-architecture and cross-domain black-box transferability across OpenVLA, UniVLA, and CronusVLA.
AI.conclusion: VLA-Hijack breaks the transferability bottleneck by targeting the shared visual self-localization process, effectively severing the semantic relationship between the agent's true embodiment and its control policy.

title: SPAR: Support-Preserving Action Rectification
summary: Offline policy improvement faces an inherent conflict between maximizing value and fitting the data distribution. While in-sample weighted regression is stable, it suffers from over-conservatism that suppresses high-value actions in the distribution tail; conversely, gradient-based approaches often exhibit a fitting-optimization conflict of gradients, which drives the policy off the data manifold. To address this, we propose Support-Preserving Action Rectification (SPAR), which reframes global learning as a local residual rectification anchored to a frozen pure behavior cloning policy. This framework performs fine-grained fitting and local policy improvement in the residual space, thereby contracting the search space. We further introduce Latent Self-Imitation, utilizing a latent-sampling weighted-regression mechanism to address fitting-improvement gradient conflict in the residual space. Theoretically, we prove this mechanism eliminates the manifold-normal drift of standard value gradients, while extensive D4RL experiments show SPAR extracts significant gains from suboptimal baselines to achieve state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: SPAR reframes offline policy improvement as local residual rectification anchored to a frozen behavior cloning policy, using Latent Self-Imitation to resolve gradient conflicts, achieving SOTA on D4RL.
AI.motivation: Offline policy improvement faces conflict between maximizing value and fitting data distribution; in-sample weighted regression is over-conservative, while gradient-based approaches suffer from fitting-optimization gradient conflicts that drive policy off the data manifold.
AI.method: (1) Support-Preserving Action Rectification (SPAR): reframes global learning as local residual rectification anchored to a frozen pure behavior cloning policy, contracting the search space. (2) Latent Self-Imitation: a latent-sampling weighted-regression mechanism to address fitting-improvement gradient conflict in the residual space.
AI.result: Theoretically eliminates manifold-normal drift of standard value gradients; extensive D4RL experiments show SPAR extracts significant gains from suboptimal baselines to achieve state-of-the-art performance.
AI.conclusion: SPAR effectively resolves the fitting-optimization conflict in offline policy improvement by localizing learning to a residual space anchored to behavior cloning, achieving stable and high-performance results.

title: DiagramRAG: A Lightweight Framework to Retrieve Scientific Diagram for Figure Generation
summary: Scientific diagrams are essential for communicating complex methodologies in academic papers. A natural way for researchers to specify such diagrams is through rough sketches, where text labels, connectors, and spatial arrangements express early semantic and topological intentions. However, sketches are usually incomplete, making them insufficient for directly producing publication-quality diagrams. Existing sketch-based generation methods mainly reconstruct the sketch itself, while recent text-driven diagram generation frameworks rely on textual semantics and do not fully exploit the topological structure contained in sketches. In this paper, we introduce DiagramRAG, a lightweight retrieval-augmented framework for sketch-based scientific diagram completion. Given a user sketch, DiagramRAG retrieves reference diagrams that are both semantically relevant to the sketch content and topologically compatible with its structure, and uses them to guide downstream diagram generation. To enable efficient structure-aware retrieval, we represent diagrams as knowledge graphs, synthesize sketch variants at different simplification levels, and train an embedding model to align sketches with compatible diagrams in a shared space. The retrieved references further provide content, topology, and visual priors for completing and rendering the final diagram. Experiments show that DiagramRAG achieves F1-scores of 0.848 and 0.802 on DiagramBank and FigureBench, respectively, and improves generation quality with the best VLM-as-a-Judge score of 7.170, while reducing inference latency to 35.48 seconds per sample. Our code and data are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/DiagramRAG-A262 and https://huggingface.co/datasets/anonymous-review-a262/DiagramSketch.

AI.tldr: DiagramRAG is a lightweight retrieval-augmented framework that takes rough user sketches and retrieves semantically and topologically compatible reference diagrams to complete them into publication-quality scientific diagrams, achieving strong performance on DiagramBank and FigureBench.
AI.motivation: Scientific diagrams are essential but hard to produce from rough sketches; existing sketch-based methods only reconstruct the sketch, and text-driven methods ignore the topological structure in sketches.
AI.method: DiagramRAG represents diagrams as knowledge graphs, synthesizes sketch variants at multiple simplification levels, trains an embedding model to align sketches with compatible diagrams, then retrieves references to provide content, topology, and visual priors for completion and rendering.
AI.result: F1-scores of 0.848 (DiagramBank) and 0.802 (FigureBench); best VLM-as-a-Judge score of 7.170; inference latency reduced to 35.48 seconds per sample.
AI.conclusion: DiagramRAG effectively leverages both semantic and topological information from sketches via retrieval-augmented generation, outperforming existing methods in quality and efficiency.

title: How VLAs Fail Differently: Black-Box Action Monitoring Reveals Architecture-Specific Failure Signatures
comment: Accepted at IEEE ICRA 2026 Workshop "From Data to Decisions: VLA Pipelines for Real Robots", Vienna, June 2026. Non-archival workshop. 5 pages, 2 figures, 22 references
summary: We discover that VLA architectures fail in fundamentally different, predictable ways at the motor-command level. Running VQ-BeT, Diffusion Policy, and ACT on identical evaluation protocols (n=450 episodes across PushT and ALOHA 14-DOF bimanual manipulation), we find: (1) direction reversal rate is a universal failure predictor across all three architectures (AUROC=0.93, 0.79, 0.91; p<0.001); (2) jerk monitoring is predictive only for discrete-token architectures, following a discrete-to-continuous gradient (0.88, 0.69, 0.41); (3) velocity violations alone are non-predictive everywhere (AUROC 0.41-0.69), yet velocity checking is the most common safety mechanism in VLA deployment code; and (4) for continuous-family VLAs, velocity monitoring provides effectively zero predictive signal (AUROC=0.52 on ACT, 0.41 on Diffusion), proving that architecture-matched monitor selection is essential. These results quantify a monitoring consequence of the well-known discrete/continuous VLA distinction: the two families produce qualitatively different failure signatures that require different monitors. No single monitor works universally; architecture-matched selection is required. This finding was enabled by SafeContract, a training-free, black-box action monitoring toolkit with conformal calibration. Code: https://github.com/krishnam94/vla-edge

AI.tldr: VLA architectures fail in fundamentally different ways at the motor-command level, requiring architecture-matched safety monitors rather than universal ones. Direction reversal rate is a universal failure predictor, while jerk monitoring works only for discrete-token architectures, and velocity monitoring provides no predictive signal for continuous-family VLAs.
AI.motivation: To understand how different VLA (Vision-Language-Action) architectures fail at the motor-command level and identify whether universal or architecture-specific failure monitoring approaches are needed for safety.
AI.method: Evaluated three VLA architectures (VQ-BeT, Diffusion Policy, ACT) on 450 episodes across PushT and ALOHA 14-DOF bimanual manipulation tasks, testing direction reversal rate, jerk monitoring, and velocity violations as failure predictors, using SafeContract toolkit with conformal calibration.
AI.result: Direction reversal rate is a universal failure predictor across all architectures (AUROC 0.93, 0.79, 0.91); jerk monitoring works only for discrete-token architectures (0.88, 0.69, 0.41 gradient); velocity violations alone are non-predictive everywhere (0.41-0.69); velocity monitoring provides zero predictive signal for continuous-family VLAs (0.52 on ACT, 0.41 on Diffusion).
AI.conclusion: Discrete and continuous VLA families produce qualitatively different failure signatures requiring different monitors. No single monitor works universally; architecture-matched selection is essential. Velocity checking, the most common safety mechanism in VLA deployment code, is ineffective, highlighting the need for proper monitor selection.

title: Qwen-Image-Bench: From Generation to Creation in Text-to-Image Evaluation
summary: Text-to-Image generation has evolved from basic image synthesis into a frequently used core capability in professional creative workflows, where simple text-image alignment can no longer satisfy users' pressing demands for faithful real-world reconstruction and genuine creative expression. Existing benchmarks, however, remain anchored in these foundational criteria and do not yet capture the nuanced capabilities that matter in authentic artistic practice, making it difficult to reliably distinguish state-of-the-art T2I models. To address the gap, we introduce Qwen-Image-Bench, a creator-centric benchmark co-designed with professional artists and grounded in real-world creation scenarios. Qwen-Image-Bench enriches conventional evaluation with two application-driven dimensions: Real-world Fidelity and Creative Generation. Drawing on the staged reasoning inherent in professional artistic workflows, we organize these five pillars into a top-down hierarchical taxonomy that further decomposes into 23 second-level sub-capabilities and 56 third-level verifiable rubrics. To ensure broad coverage, we curate 1000 stratified prompts with each prompt jointly exercising more than four fine-grained facets across multiple pillars. We train a unified judge model Q-Judger based on Qwen3.6-27B, supervised by 80 professional annotators from global art academies under blind labeling and triple-review protocols, that scores every image across all 56 verifiable facets, producing fine-grained, rubric-grounded, and fully attributable diagnostics rather than a single opaque score. Empirically, Qwen-Image-Bench reliably distinguishes leading T2I models, achieving the greatest separation on the two application-driven dimensions of Real-world Fidelity and Creative Generation where existing benchmarks provide little insight, while also providing a trustworthy optimization signal for production-level T2I development.

AI.tldr: Qwen-Image-Bench is a creator-centric benchmark co-designed with professional artists that evaluates T2I models on 5 pillars, 23 sub-capabilities, and 56 verifiable rubrics using 1000 stratified prompts, scored by a trained judge model Q-Judger with 80 professional annotators under blind triple-review protocols.
AI.motivation: Existing T2I benchmarks remain anchored in basic text-image alignment criteria and fail to capture nuanced capabilities important in authentic artistic practice, making it difficult to reliably distinguish state-of-the-art T2I models.
AI.method: The benchmark is co-designed with professional artists, organized into 5 pillars (including Real-world Fidelity and Creative Generation) with a hierarchical taxonomy (23 sub-capabilities, 56 verifiable rubrics), using 1000 stratified prompts. A unified judge model Q-Judger (based on Qwen3.6-27B) is trained with 80 professional annotators under blind labeling and triple-review protocols.
AI.result: Qwen-Image-Bench reliably distinguishes leading T2I models, achieving the greatest separation on the two application-driven dimensions (Real-world Fidelity and Creative Generation) where existing benchmarks provide little insight.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark provides a trustworthy optimization signal for production-level T2I development and captures the nuanced capabilities that matter in authentic artistic practice.

title: FedEHR-Gen: Federated Synthetic Time-Series EHR Generation via Latent Space Alignment and Distribution-Aware Aggregation
comment: 8 pages main paper with 14 pages supplementary appendix
summary: Synthetic Electronic Health Record (EHR) generation provides a promising avenue for data augmentation and cross-hospital modeling in privacy-constrained healthcare settings. However, most existing EHR generative models are centralized and require pooling data across hospitals, which is often infeasible when real-world data sharing is restricted. While federated EHR generation offers a natural solution, direct federated modeling often collapses or diverges due to the high dimensionality, sparsity, and cross-hospital heterogeneity of EHR data. In this work, we propose FedEHR-Gen, the first federated framework for synthetic time-series EHR generation across distributed hospitals. FedEHR-Gen uses a two-stage learning paradigm. First, we introduce a federated autoencoder that projects high-dimensional and sparse EHR features onto a compact latent space. To ensure semantic consistency across hospitals, we develop a layer-wise matching aggregation mechanism that aligns local encoders into a unified global latent space. Second, operating on this aligned latent space, we train a federated temporal conditional variational autoencoder (TCVAE) with distribution-aware aggregation, enabling stable temporal generative modeling under severe cross-hospital heterogeneity. Extensive experiments on the eICU and MIMIC-III datasets demonstrate that FedEHR-Gen achieves generation fidelity, downstream utility, and privacy risk comparable to centralized training, while consistently outperforming the standard federated baseline.

AI.tldr: FedEHR-Gen is the first federated framework for generating synthetic time-series EHR data across distributed hospitals. It uses a two-stage approach: (1) a federated autoencoder with layer-wise matching for unified latent space, and (2) a federated TCVAE with distribution-aware aggregation for stable temporal generation under cross-hospital heterogeneity, outperforming standard federated baselines on eICU and MIMIC-III datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing EHR generative models are centralized and require pooling data across hospitals, which is infeasible when data sharing is restricted due to privacy constraints. Direct federated EHR generation collapses or diverges due to high dimensionality, sparsity, and cross-hospital heterogeneity of EHR data.
AI.method: Two-stage federated learning paradigm: (1) Federated autoencoder with layer-wise matching aggregation to project high-dimensional sparse EHR data into a compact, semantically consistent latent space across hospitals. (2) Federated temporal conditional variational autoencoder (TCVAE) with distribution-aware aggregation for stable temporal generative modeling under heterogeneity.
AI.result: On eICU and MIMIC-III datasets, FedEHR-Gen achieves generation fidelity, downstream utility, and privacy risk comparable to centralized training, and consistently outperforms standard federated baselines.
AI.conclusion: FedEHR-Gen effectively enables privacy-preserving synthetic EHR generation across distributed hospitals, addressing the key challenges of data sharing restrictions and cross-hospital heterogeneity in healthcare settings.

title: Do Agents Think Deeper? A Mechanistic Investigation of Layer-Wise Dynamics in Sequential Planning
summary: Recent mechanistic studies suggest that large language models (LLMs) may utilize their depth inefficiently in standard single-turn tasks. Whether this still holds in autonomous agent settings, where models must perform multi-turn planning, tool use, and iterative state updates, remains unclear. We study this question through a systematic layer-wise analysis of complete user-agent trajectories spanning three domains: Deep Research, Code Generation, and Tabular Processing. Using residual stream probes, causal layer-skipping interventions, and effective-depth measurements, we show that agentic reasoning exhibits a distinct depth profile from static tasks. As trajectories unfold, models progressively recruit more and deeper layers, with stronger long-range inter-layer dependencies emerging in later turns. At the same time, residual updates become increasingly correction-dominant, indicating a shift from stable feature accumulation toward repeated recalibration. Effective-depth analysis further reveals a substantial construction-refinement gap: semantic direction often forms relatively early, while deep layers remain necessary for stabilizing final outputs. Across model families, this gap is pronounced in Qwen and Minimax, whereas GLM shows a more domain-dependent depth allocation pattern. These results provide mechanistic evidence that autonomous LLM agents allocate depth adaptively as reasoning complexity grows.

AI.tldr: Large language models use depth inefficiently in static tasks but adaptively recruit deeper layers in autonomous agent settings across multi-turn trajectories, with a construction-refinement gap where semantic direction forms early but deep layers stabilize outputs.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether LLMs' inefficient depth utilization observed in static tasks persists in autonomous agent settings involving multi-turn planning, tool use, and iterative state updates.
AI.method: Systematic layer-wise analysis of complete user-agent trajectories across Deep Research, Code Generation, and Tabular Processing using residual stream probes, causal layer-skipping interventions, and effective-depth measurements.
AI.result: Agentic reasoning shows distinct depth profiles: progressive recruitment of deeper layers as trajectories unfold, stronger long-range inter-layer dependencies in later turns, shift toward correction-dominant residual updates, and a construction-refinement gap where semantic direction forms early but deep layers stabilize outputs.
AI.conclusion: Autonomous LLM agents allocate depth adaptively as reasoning complexity grows, with model-specific patterns (Qwen and Minimax show pronounced construction-refinement gap, GLM shows domain-dependent depth allocation).

title: Imitation Learning for Robot Assistance in Open Surgery: A Multi-Policy Evaluation on Suture Following
summary: This study presents the first evaluation of general-purpose imitation learning for surgeon-robot collaborative assistance in open surgery, targeting suture following: the grab-pull-release motion an assistant performs at every stitch. We collect 160 teleoperated demonstrations (32,374 frames) on an open-source robot arm, benchmark four architecturally diverse imitation learning policies (ACT, Diffusion Policy, SmolVLA, $π_0$) across 28 trained models evaluated in 32 configurations along three clinically motivated dimensions: dataset size, camera viewpoint, and background variation. Our results demonstrate that under ideal conditions, the four policies achieve $50$-$75\%$ task success, with depth error as the dominant failure mode across all architectures. Among all policies, $π_0$ achieves the strongest results with a pretrained vision-language backbone, demonstrating superior data efficiency, greater robustness to background variation, and smoother trajectories compatible with surgical workflow. When deployed in a surgeon-robot suturing trial, $π_0$ yields a $92\%$ stitch completion rate. These findings establish collaborative robotic assistance in open surgery as a feasible target for imitation learning and highlight depth perception and end-effector design as key priorities for clinical translation.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates four imitation learning policies for robot-assisted suture following in open surgery, finding that π₀ with a pretrained vision-language backbone achieves the best results (75% task success, 92% stitch completion rate) and that depth error is the dominant failure mode across all architectures.
AI.motivation: To evaluate whether general-purpose imitation learning can enable effective surgeon-robot collaborative assistance in open surgery, specifically for the repetitive grab-pull-release motion required in suture following.
AI.method: Collected 160 teleoperated demonstrations (32,374 frames) on an open-source robot arm. Benchmarked four architecturally diverse policies (ACT, Diffusion Policy, SmolVLA, π₀) across 28 trained models in 32 configurations, varying dataset size, camera viewpoint, and background. Deployed the best policy in a surgeon-robot suturing trial.
AI.result: Under ideal conditions, all four policies achieve 50-75% task success. π₀ outperforms others with superior data efficiency, robustness to background variation, and smoother trajectories. In a surgeon-robot trial, π₀ achieved 92% stitch completion rate. Depth error was the dominant failure mode across all architectures.
AI.conclusion: Collaborative robotic assistance in open surgery is a feasible target for imitation learning. Depth perception and end-effector design are identified as key priorities for clinical translation.

title: Revisiting Change Detection Methods for their Application to Serac Fall Time-Lapse Monitoring
summary: In an era where climate change aggravates environmental uncertainties, the identification and detection of event precursors are becoming crucial to mitigate the impacts of disastrous natural hazards. While classical sensors such as interferometric lasers or seismometers are reliable, their widespread deployment is often hindered by logistical and economic barriers, leaving numerous blind spots. Time-lapse cameras, which already provide cost-effective, high-resolution visual context to such sensors, present a promising alternative. However, processing their output automatically faces significant challenges, notably linked to extreme shape and lighting variations. Overcoming those issues is essential to deploy them at large-scale as a monitoring tool. This paper introduces a novel sub-task of change detection, namely volumetric change detection, applied to time-lapse cameras and slope instabilities. We conduct a comprehensive review of state-of-the-art change detection methods and related tasks, analyze their core components and assess their applicability to this context. To that end, we introduce the new dataset SeracFallDet, which contains serac fall annotations and has been thoroughly annotated to meet the latter demand. Through generalization experiments, we demonstrate that dense and semi-dense feature matching, although not trained specifically for this task, exhibit robust performance. Alternatively, supervised approaches struggle with data scarcity and annotation imbalance. This suggests that hybrid methods may offer a path forward by leveraging the strengths of both tasks. These findings highlight the potential of feature matching techniques and the need for further innovation to overcome the challenges of real-world deployment in environmental monitoring.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces volumetric change detection for time-lapse cameras monitoring slope instabilities (serac falls), finds that dense/semi-dense feature matching (unsupervised) outperforms supervised methods, and introduces the SeracFallDet dataset.
AI.motivation: Climate change increases environmental uncertainties, and cost-effective monitoring tools (time-lapse cameras) are needed to detect disaster precursors, but current methods struggle with extreme shape/lighting variations and data scarcity.
AI.method: The paper defines a novel sub-task (volumetric change detection), creates the SeracFallDet dataset with serac fall annotations, and conducts generalization experiments comparing dense/semi-dense feature matching vs. supervised approaches.
AI.result: Dense and semi-dense feature matching (not specifically trained for this task) show robust performance, while supervised methods struggle due to data scarcity and annotation imbalance.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid methods combining feature matching and supervised learning may be a promising path forward; further innovation is needed for real-world deployment.

title: Where LLM Annotators Fail: Label-Free Learning on Graphs with LLMs
summary: Node classification on graphs often requires labeled nodes, yet obtaining labels at graph scale is expensive. When node attributes contain semantic content, such as paper abstracts, web pages, or product descriptions, large language models (LLMs) can provide low-cost supervision by annotating a small subset of nodes. However, these LLM-generated labels are noisy, and existing label-free graph learning methods usually treat this noise as either global or class-conditional. We find that LLM annotation errors are not only class-dependent but also region-dependent: within the same class, reliability can vary sharply across feature-space clusters. In light of this, we propose Cluster-Aware Noise Estimation (CANE), a label-free learning framework that estimates cluster-conditional LLM reliability without ground truth labels, and uses this estimate to decide which pseudo-labels to trust, and which labels to correct. Across various graph benchmarks and GNN backbones, CANE improves over the strongest label-free baselines, with the largest gains on datasets exhibiting stronger cluster-conditional noise.

AI.tldr: Proposes CANE, a label-free framework that estimates cluster-conditional noise in LLM-generated node labels for graph classification, outperforming existing methods especially on datasets with cluster-dependent label noise.
AI.motivation: LLM-generated labels for node classification are noisy, and existing methods treat this noise as global or class-conditional, ignoring that annotation errors also vary across feature-space clusters within the same class.
AI.method: Introduces Cluster-Aware Noise Estimation (CANE), which estimates cluster-conditional LLM reliability without ground truth labels and uses these estimates to decide which pseudo-labels to trust and which to correct.
AI.result: CANE outperforms existing label-free baselines across multiple graph benchmarks and GNN backbones, with largest gains on datasets exhibiting stronger cluster-conditional noise.
AI.conclusion: Accounting for cluster-conditional noise improves LLM-supervised graph learning, and CANE effectively captures this regional reliability variation without requiring ground truth labels.

title: From Talking to Singing: A New Challenge for Audio-Visual Deepfake Detection
summary: With rapid advances in audio-visual generative models, reliable forgery detection becomes increasingly critical. Existing methods for audio-visual deepfake detection typically rely on cross-modal inconsistencies. In singing, rhythmic vocalization weakens this coupling and introduces a nontrivial domain shift, substantially degrading detection performance. We construct the Singing Head DeepFake (SHDF) dataset using rhythm-aware generative models to fill the gap in singing benchmarks. To cope with cross-scenario domain shifts, we propose a Text-guided Audio-Visual Forgery Detection (T-AVFD) framework that generalizes across both talking and singing scenarios. T-AVFD comprises a facial authenticity pattern learner and a multi-modal differential weight learning module. The pattern learner aligns facial features with multi-granularity textual descriptions to learn generalizable authenticity patterns. The weight learning module preserves intrinsic audio-visual consistency and adaptively integrates it with authenticity patterns via differential weighting. Extensive experiments on multiple talking head deepfake datasets and SHDF show consistent improvements over existing baselines and strong robustness under diverse perturbations.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses audio-visual deepfake detection in singing scenarios where traditional cross-modal inconsistency methods fail. It introduces the SHDF singing dataset and a Text-guided Audio-Visual Forgery Detection (T-AVFD) framework that combines facial authenticity pattern learning with adaptive multi-modal differential weighting, achieving robust performance across talking and singing domains.
AI.motivation: Existing audio-visual deepfake detection methods rely on cross-modal inconsistencies, which work for talking scenarios but fail in singing because rhythmic vocalization weakens audio-visual coupling and introduces domain shift. There is a lack of singing-specific benchmarks and detection methods.
AI.method: Proposes T-AVFD with two components: (1) a facial authenticity pattern learner that aligns facial features with multi-granularity textual descriptions to learn generalizable authenticity patterns, and (2) a multi-modal differential weight learning module that preserves intrinsic audio-visual consistency and adaptively integrates it with authenticity patterns via differential weighting. Also constructs the SHDF dataset using rhythm-aware generative models.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multiple talking head deepfake datasets and the new SHDF dataset show consistent improvements over existing baselines and strong robustness under diverse perturbations.
AI.conclusion: The T-AVFD framework generalizes across both talking and singing scenarios, effectively overcoming the domain shift problem caused by rhythmic vocalization in singing deepfake detection.

title: Beyond Binary: Sim-to-Real Dexterous Manipulation with Physics-Grounded Contact Representation
summary: A primary bottleneck in contact-rich manipulation is the difficulty of collecting real-world data. Sim-to-real reinforcement learning offers a scalable alternative, but the simulation-reality gap prevents information-dense modalities like touch from being effectively used. Existing sim-to-real methods often mitigate this gap by simplifying tactile data into coarse low-dimensional features -- sacrificing the richness required for complex manipulation. In this work, we introduce Center-of-Pressure (CoP), an effective tactile representation grounded in physical principles that preserves dense contact information while maintaining robustness for sim-to-real transfer. To support this representation, we propose a sensor calibration scheme based on differentiable dynamics, enabling the estimation of taxel orientations without requiring ground-truth force measurements. We evaluate CoP on two blind, challenging contact-rich manipulation tasks: peg-in-hole insertion and ball balancing. Across both tasks, policies conditioned on CoP achieve zero-shot sim-to-real transfer on a multi-fingered hand, and outperform both coarse binary-contact and raw-taxel baselines. Analysis of learned policy states further suggests that CoP-conditioned policies encode task-relevant physical properties, such as object mass, as an emergent byproduct of control.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Center-of-Pressure (CoP) as a tactile representation for sim-to-real transfer in contact-rich manipulation, demonstrating zero-shot transfer on peg-in-hole and ball balancing tasks with a multi-fingered hand, outperforming binary-contact and raw-taxel baselines.
AI.motivation: Real-world data collection for contact-rich manipulation is difficult, and sim-to-real transfer of rich tactile information is hindered by the simulation-reality gap; existing methods sacrifice tactile richness by using coarse low-dimensional features.
AI.method: Proposes Center-of-Pressure (CoP) as a physics-grounded tactile representation, along with a differentiable dynamics-based sensor calibration scheme for estimating taxel orientations without ground-truth force measurements.
AI.result: Zero-shot sim-to-real transfer achieved on peg-in-hole insertion and ball balancing; CoP outperforms binary-contact and raw-taxel baselines; learned policies encode task-relevant physical properties like object mass.
AI.conclusion: CoP is an effective tactile representation that preserves dense contact information while enabling robust sim-to-real transfer, with emergent encoding of physical properties.

title: ST-ColoNet: Spatio-Temporal Colon Segment Recognition via Hybrid Attention and Edge-Guided Feature Learning
summary: Colo-segment recognition in colonoscopy videos is a key requirement for many downstream tasks, but existing automatic recognition methods only use colonoscopy images without fully exploiting the use of temporal information, leading to poor performance. Additionally, relevant public video-based datasets are in scarcity. To tackle this problem, we curate and release a labeled dataset specifically for the task of colo-segment recognition. In addition, we propose a two-stage deep learning-based framework, Colo-Segment Recognition via SpatioTemporal Network (ST-ColoNet), for the task of colo-segment recognition from colonoscopy videos which includes the Colorlaus module that uses metric learning to optimize edge-mediated spatial feature extraction, as well as the Full-Temp module which combines three self-attention patterns to better approximate full self-attention on long colonoscopy sequences and optimize temporal feature aggregation. Through extensive ablation experiments, we show that our framework is capable of achieving state-of-the-art performance on the task of colo-segment recognition, achieving an accuracy of 81.0% and F1-score of 70.7%, which is a tremendous improvement over state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: ST-ColoNet is a two-stage deep learning framework for colo-segment recognition in colonoscopy videos that uses spatiotemporal features (Colorlaus module for spatial extraction via metric learning + Full-Temp module with three self-attention patterns for temporal aggregation), achieving SOTA results (81.0% accuracy, 70.7% F1-score).
AI.motivation: Existing methods only use colonoscopy images without leveraging temporal information, leading to poor performance; also, there is a scarcity of public video-based datasets for this task.
AI.method: A two-stage framework (ST-ColoNet) with: (1) Colorlaus module using metric learning to optimize edge-mediated spatial feature extraction, and (2) Full-Temp module combining three self-attention patterns to approximate full self-attention on long video sequences for temporal feature aggregation.
AI.result: Achieved 81.0% accuracy and 70.7% F1-score, significantly outperforming existing state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively exploits spatiotemporal information for colo-segment recognition, setting a new SOTA benchmark. The released labeled dataset also addresses the data scarcity issue.

title: Cyclical Entropy Eruption: Entropy Dynamics in Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Agentic large language models are increasingly used to solve real-world tasks by reasoning over goals, invoking tools, and interacting with external environments. Reinforcement learning provides a natural framework for improving these behaviors, and recent agent RL methods have achieved strong results across domains. However, the training dynamics of agent RL remain poorly understood, limiting our ability to diagnose instabilities and design more effective training algorithms. In this work, we identify a previously underexplored phenomenon in agent RL, which we term cyclical entropy eruption. Unlike single-turn reasoning RL, where entropy typically collapses and stays low, agent RL training exhibits unique recurring cycles of sharp entropy eruption and gradual subsidence. We decompose this dynamic into three phases and provide theoretical and empirical analyses of each, explaining the mechanisms underlying its cyclical oscillation. We further show that degenerate patterns such as sentence duplication and hallucination, once acquired during eruption, can persist and accumulate across cycles. Motivated by these findings, we propose SEAL (Separation-Enhanced Agent Learning), a lightweight auxiliary loss that separates correct and incorrect trajectories in representation space, directly targeting the root cause of entropy eruption. Experiments across multiple benchmarks, models, and RL algorithms demonstrate that SEAL stabilizes training and yields stronger downstream agent performance.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies "cyclical entropy eruption" in agent RL training, where entropy repeatedly spikes and subsides, unlike single-turn reasoning RL where entropy collapses. The authors propose SEAL, an auxiliary loss that separates correct/incorrect trajectories in representation space to stabilize training and improve downstream agent performance.
AI.motivation: Agentic LLMs are increasingly used for real-world tasks, but agent RL training dynamics remain poorly understood, limiting the ability to diagnose instabilities and design better algorithms. The authors aim to uncover and understand unique training phenomena in agent RL.
AI.method: The authors (1) identify cyclical entropy eruption and decompose it into three phases with theoretical/empirical analysis, (2) show degenerate patterns persist across cycles, and (3) propose SEAL — a lightweight auxiliary loss that separates correct and incorrect trajectory representations to address the root cause.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple benchmarks, models, and RL algorithms demonstrate that SEAL stabilizes training and yields stronger downstream agent performance, addressing the cyclical entropy eruption phenomenon.
AI.conclusion: Agent RL exhibits unique cyclical entropy eruption distinct from single-turn RL. SEAL effectively stabilizes training by separating trajectory representations, leading to improved agent performance.

title: The Shape of Overthinking: Backtracking Bursts in Long Reasoning Traces
summary: Reasoning models often generate long traces in which useful self-correction and unproductive revision are hard to distinguish. We study this distinction through backtracking dynamics: local reconsideration, retraction, or re-derivation inside long-form reasoning traces. On 6{,}000 Qwen3-8B AIME traces, we annotate segment-level backtrack severity and analyze event timing, normalized depth, and local burst structure. We find that early isolated repair is often compatible with correct reasoning, whereas incorrect traces more often show moderate-to-severe backtracks that persist and cluster late. Cross-corpus checks show the same qualitative asymmetry across additional model/domain pairs. Filtering analyses instantiate the signal as a prefix-causal selective early-exit policy: at shallow and intermediate depths, burst-aware filtering outperforms fixed length-based filtering while using only prefix-available features. Moderate length cutoffs remain strong completed-trace baselines, but burst-aware control provides a deployable mechanism for separating recoverable repair from likely instability.

AI.tldr: The paper studies backtracking dynamics in reasoning model traces, finding that early isolated repair correlates with correct reasoning while persistent late clustering of backtracks indicates instability. A burst-aware early-exit filtering policy outperforms fixed-length methods.
AI.motivation: To distinguish useful self-correction from unproductive revision in long reasoning traces produced by reasoning models.
AI.method: Annotated segment-level backtrack severity on 6,000 Qwen3-8B AIME traces; analyzed event timing, normalized depth, and local burst structure; implemented a prefix-causal selective early-exit policy with burst-aware filtering.
AI.result: Early isolated repair correlates with correct reasoning; incorrect traces show moderate-to-severe backtracks that persist and cluster late. Burst-aware filtering outperforms fixed length-based filtering at shallow and intermediate depths.
AI.conclusion: Burst-aware control provides a deployable mechanism for distinguishing recoverable repair from likely instability, though moderate length cutoffs remain strong baselines.

title: CLEAR-NeRF: Collinearity and Local-region Enhanced Accurate 3D Reconstruction in Unbounded Scenes
summary: Many real-world 3D reconstruction applications demand photorealism and metric accuracy across unbounded, complex scenes with challenging lighting and imperfect captures that current Neural Radiance Field (NeRF) pipelines only partly satisfy. This study adapts NeRF-based 3D reconstruction to multi-region of interest unbounded scenes to improve robustness to lighting and pose variation while enforcing metric accuracy suitable for digital-twin applications. Our approach introduces (i) automated local region localization/detection and reconstruction to seamlessly prioritize areas of interest without proliferating submodules, (ii) collinearity-enforcing ray sampling to learn smooth planar and curved surfaces, (iii) depth-localized neighborhood point extraction to suppress surface artifacts, and (iv) geometry-relevant color aggregation to mitigate lighting- and pose-caused variations. Results indicate superior performance of the proposed pipeline over the baseline NeRF models and established Structure from Motion (SfM) - Multi-View Stereo (MVS) solutions.

AI.tldr: The paper adapts NeRF-based 3D reconstruction for multi-region unbounded scenes, introducing four key techniques to improve photorealism, metric accuracy, and robustness to lighting/pose variations, outperforming baseline NeRF and SfM-MVS methods.
AI.motivation: Real-world 3D reconstruction requires both photorealism and metric accuracy for unbounded, complex scenes with challenging lighting and imperfect captures, which current NeRF pipelines only partly satisfy.
AI.method: The approach introduces (i) automated local region detection/reconstruction, (ii) collinearity-enforcing ray sampling for smooth surfaces, (iii) depth-localized neighborhood point extraction to suppress artifacts, and (iv) geometry-relevant color aggregation to mitigate lighting/pose variations.
AI.result: The proposed pipeline achieves superior performance over baseline NeRF models and established SfM-MVS solutions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed NeRF-based pipeline effectively improves robustness to lighting and pose variations while enforcing metric accuracy, making it suitable for digital-twin applications in unbounded multi-region scenes.

title: Continual Learning in Modern Hopfield Networks with an Application to Diffusion Models
summary: Generative models, including diffusion models, are increasingly used as foundation models and adapted through sequential fine-tuning, making continual learning an essential problem setting. However, continual learning in such generative models remains poorly understood: after a task change, what aspects of the learned distribution are most easily lost, and what replay samples should be prioritized? We address these questions through the modern Hopfield energy. Recent links between modern Hopfield networks (MHNs) and diffusion models allow analyses in MHNs to be transferred to diffusion models. We introduce intrinsic forgetting as an increase in Hopfield energy after the task change. In tractable settings in an MHN, we prove that high-energy, outlier-like samples undergo a larger energy increase than cluster-like samples, implying that samples located in sharp, isolated basins are more forgettable. We further analyze memory replay and show that replay is particularly effective for high-energy samples, enabling an energy-based selection of replay samples. We validate these predictions in experiments on MHNs and two diffusion models under continual-learning settings: Stable Diffusion and a pixel-space DDPM. In these diffusion models, Hopfield energy tracks reconstruction-based forgetting, and replay experiments reveal energy-dependent mitigation of forgetting that is consistent with the MHN analysis.

AI.tldr: This paper uses modern Hopfield network theory to analyze continual learning in diffusion models, proving that high-energy (outlier-like) samples are more forgettable after task changes and benefit most from replay-based memory strategies.
AI.motivation: Continual learning in generative models like diffusion models is poorly understood—specifically, what aspects of learned distributions are easily lost after task changes and which replay samples should be prioritized.
AI.method: The authors leverage theoretical links between modern Hopfield networks (MHNs) and diffusion models, define "intrinsic forgetting" via Hopfield energy increase after task change, prove mathematically that high-energy outlier samples forget more, and validate predictions in MHNs and diffusion models (Stable Diffusion, DDPM).
AI.result: In MHNs, high-energy outlier-like samples exhibit larger energy increases (more forgettable) than cluster-like samples; replay benefits high-energy samples most. These findings transfer to diffusion models where Hopfield energy correlates with reconstruction-based forgetting.
AI.conclusion: Hopfield energy provides a principled framework for understanding and mitigating forgetting in continual learning for generative models, enabling energy-based selection of replay samples.

title: Geometry of Human Perceptual Domains Emerges Transiently in LLM Representations
summary: While large language models (LLMs) are trained purely on textual data, prior work has shown that their internal representations can exhibit rich geometric structure in embedding space. Building on this line of work, we investigate whether such structure is similar to human perceptual organisation across different domains (e.g., color, pitch, emotion, and taste). Specifically, we study the layer-wise emergence of intrinsic geometrical structure corresponding to perceptual modalities within the residual streams of multiple open-weight transformer architectures. Our results reveal three key findings. First, we observe the emergence of layer-wise geometric structure across multiple perceptual domains, despite the absence of any direct perceptual supervision during training. Second, these perceptual domains exhibit distinct emergence profiles, with both geometric structure and its alignment with human baselines following domain- and model-specific trajectories across depth. Third, this emergence follows a consistent representational trajectory: geometry is weak or diffuse in early layers, becomes progressively organised in intermediate layers, and is attenuated in later layers, suggesting that perceptual geometry arises transiently as part of the model's internal transformation pipeline. This provides new insight into how and where human-like perceptual geometry arises in LLMs, offering a principled pathway for mechanistic analysis of internal representations.

AI.tldr: LLMs develop human-like perceptual geometry (e.g., color, pitch, emotion, taste) in their internal representations, emerging transiently in intermediate layers despite no perceptual training data.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether LLMs' internal geometric structure resembles human perceptual organization across domains like color, pitch, emotion, and taste, and to understand how this structure emerges across model layers.
AI.method: Layer-wise analysis of geometric structure in residual streams of multiple open-weight transformer models across perceptual domains, comparing alignment with human baselines.
AI.result: (1) Perceptual geometric structure emerges without direct supervision; (2) Different domains show distinct emergence profiles; (3) Structure follows a consistent trajectory: weak in early layers, organized in intermediate layers, attenuated in later layers.
AI.conclusion: Human-like perceptual geometry arises transiently in LLMs' intermediate layers as part of their internal processing pipeline, providing a pathway for mechanistic analysis.

title: SAM-Enhanced Segmentation on Road Datasets: Balancing Critical Classes in Autonomous Driving
summary: Dense semantic segmentation is essential for autonomous driving, yet many multi-modal datasets lack pixel-level annotations. The Zenseact Open Dataset (ZOD) provides rich multi-sensor data but only bounding-box labels, limiting its use for segmentation research. Our primary contribution is a Segment Anything Model (SAM)-based annotation pipeline that produces dense, pixel-level annotations for ZOD by converting bounding boxes into semantic masks. In this pilot study, we process over 100,000 frames and manually curate a 2,300-frame subset (36% acceptance rate) to establish a reliable baseline. Using these annotations, we evaluate transformer-based CLFT and CNN-based DeepLabV3+ architectures across diverse weather conditions, achieving up to 48.1% mIoU with CLFT-Hybrid. To address extreme class imbalance, where pedestrians, cyclists, and signs constitute less than 1% of pixels, we explore specialized models targeting rare classes. We further validate the pipeline on the Iseauto autonomous-vehicle platform, achieving 77.5% mIoU, and show that SAM-derived representations transfer effectively across sensor configurations via bidirectional transfer learning. All code and annotations are released to support reproducible research.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a SAM-based annotation pipeline that converts bounding boxes in the Zenseact Open Dataset (ZOD) into pixel-level segmentation masks. Using these annotations, the authors evaluate segmentation models across weather conditions, address class imbalance, validate on a real autonomous platform, and release all code and labels publicly.
AI.motivation: Dense segmentation is crucial for autonomous driving, but multi-modal datasets like ZOD lack pixel-level annotations despite offering rich sensor data, limiting research progress.
AI.method: A SAM-based annotation pipeline converts bounding boxes into dense semantic masks. Over 100K frames were processed, with a manually curated 2,300-frame subset (36% acceptance rate) used as baseline. Transformer (CLFT) and CNN (DeepLabV3+) architectures were evaluated.
AI.result: Up to 48.1% mIoU with CLFT-Hybrid; 77.5% mIoU on Iseauto platform. Specialized models target rare classes (<1% pixels). Bidirectional transfer learning shows SAM representations transfer across sensor configurations.
AI.conclusion: SAM-based annotation is effective for generating dense segmentation labels from bounding boxes, enabling reproducible segmentation research on ZOD, with cross-platform transferability.

title: Which Pretraining Paradigm Better Serves Spatial Intelligence? An Empirical Comparison of Vision-Language and Video Generation Models
summary: Spatial intelligence requires visual representations that capture both semantic objects and geometric structure in the physical world. To support this, two major pre-training schemes are now widely used as foundation backbones: Vision-Language Models (VLMs), which use language supervision to align visual observations with semantic concepts, and Video Generation Models (VGMs), which learn from temporally evolving visual worlds. However, it still remains unclear which pre-training scheme provides a better representation substrate for spatial intelligence. In this paper, we present the first systematic frozen-feature probing study of VLMs and VGMs across three representative axes of spatial intelligence: semantic tagging, instance grouping, and 3D geometry prediction. Using the lightweight probe, our framework enables a controlled comparison of what information is already encoded in frozen representations from two model families. Experimental results reveal a clear complementarity: VLMs are stronger at semantic tagging and instance grouping, while VGMs provide more accessible signals for dense geometry and camera motion. Moreover, a naive fusion of the two already yields a representation that excels at both geometry and semantics, suggesting a promising direction for building stronger spatial-intelligence backbones by effectively integrating features from both model families. Our code is available at \href{https://github.com/om-ai-lab/Probing-VLM-VGM}{https://github.com/om-ai-lab/Probing-VLM-VGM}.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically compares Vision-Language Models (VLMs) and Video Generation Models (VGMs) on spatial intelligence tasks, finding VLMs better at semantics/grouping and VGMs better at geometry, with their fusion showing complementary benefits.
AI.motivation: It remains unclear which pre-training scheme (VLMs with language supervision vs. VGMs with temporal visual learning) provides a better representation substrate for spatial intelligence.
AI.method: A frozen-feature probing study comparing VLMs and VGMs across three spatial intelligence axes: semantic tagging, instance grouping, and 3D geometry prediction, using lightweight probes on frozen representations.
AI.result: VLMs excel at semantic tagging and instance grouping; VGMs provide better signals for dense geometry and camera motion. A naive fusion of both representations outperforms either alone on both geometry and semantics.
AI.conclusion: VLMs and VGMs offer complementary spatial representations, and fusing them points toward stronger spatial-intelligence backbones.

title: Law of Neural Interaction: Depth-Width Shape, Interaction Efficiency, and Generalization
summary: The guidance of scaling laws has increased the resource demands of modern large language models (LLMs), yet it remains questionable whether these models utilize resources effectively under a fixed budget. Previous research has proved superposition as a key contributor to loss. By leveraging the Neural Feature Ansatz, we extend superposition from parameter space to gradient space and define it as neural interaction. We find that under a fixed budget, good generalization is usually accompanied by efficient neural interactions, and the model can be placed in an efficient interaction interval by adjusting its depth-width ratio ($R_{D/W}$). In addition, as the budget scales up, the efficient interaction interval of the model remains relatively stable. By comparing existing small scale dense LLMs, we observe that models operating near this interval tend to perform better on the MMLU-Pro benchmark. Our findings reveal that the $R_{D/W}$ influences resource utilization efficiency and thereby affects generalization, providing insights into model shape initialization and the understanding of model generalization mechanisms. Code for Neural Interaction Law is available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Neural_Interaction_Law-D788

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the concept of "neural interaction" as an extension of superposition into gradient space, finding that efficient neural interactions under fixed budgets lead to better generalization. The depth-width ratio (R_{D/W}) influences this efficiency, and models operating near the efficient interaction interval perform better on MMLU-Pro.
AI.motivation: To understand whether large language models utilize resources effectively under fixed budgets, and to investigate how superposition (previously studied in parameter space) extends to gradient space and impacts generalization.
AI.method: The authors extend superposition from parameter space to gradient space using the Neural Feature Ansatz, defining it as "neural interaction." They analyze how depth-width ratio (R_{D/W}) affects neural interaction efficiency under fixed budgets.
AI.result: Good generalization correlates with efficient neural interactions under fixed budgets. The efficient interaction interval remains stable as budget scales up. Models operating near this interval perform better on the MMLU-Pro benchmark.
AI.conclusion: The depth-width ratio (R_{D/W}) influences resource utilization efficiency and generalization, offering insights for model shape initialization and understanding generalization mechanisms in LLMs.

title: STAB: Specification-driven Testing for Algorithmic Bottlenecks
summary: Evaluating the efficiency of algorithmic code requires test cases that expose runtime bottlenecks. Previous methods generate efficiency test cases either by increasing input size or by generating code-specific inputs that make the given implementation run slowly. Consequently, they do not address the structural input conditions that drive the algorithmic worst case. We introduce STAB, a specification-driven pipeline that generates test cases that expose algorithmic bottlenecks from a natural-language problem specification alone. STAB separates the task into constraint-bound maximization and adversarial structure injection. (i) The constraint saturator extracts constraints and resolves large admissible size assignments using rule-based saturation and CP-SAT optimization over related variables. (ii) The adversarial scenario injector retrieves implementation-level adversarial construction principles from a curated scenario catalog using keyword matching and K-nearest neighbors (KNN). STAB encodes the problem specification, resolved boundary, and retrieved construction principles into a structured generation specification, from which the LLM synthesizes a Python test case generator. On CodeContests, STAB raises the rate of generated test cases that expose algorithmic bottlenecks from 50.43% to 73.45% on average across open-source LLMs and from 57.45% to 71.85% on average across closed-source LLMs, with consistent gains across Python, Java, and C++. Our code is available at https://github.com/suhanmen/STAB.

AI.tldr: STAB is a specification-driven pipeline that generates test cases exposing algorithmic bottlenecks by combining constraint-bound maximization (via rule-based saturation and CP-SAT) with adversarial scenario injection (via keyword matching and KNN), improving bottleneck detection rates from ~50-57% to ~71-73% across open-source and closed-source LLMs.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for generating efficiency test cases only increase input size or create code-specific inputs, failing to address the structural input conditions that drive algorithmic worst-case behavior.
AI.method: STAB uses a two-stage pipeline: (1) a constraint saturator that extracts constraints and resolves large admissible size assignments via rule-based saturation and CP-SAT optimization; (2) an adversarial scenario injector that retrieves implementation-level adversarial construction principles from a curated scenario catalog using keyword matching and KNN. These are combined into a structured specification for LLM synthesis of test case generators.
AI.result: On CodeContests, STAB raised bottleneck detection rates from 50.43% to 73.45% for open-source LLMs and from 57.45% to 71.85% for closed-source LLMs, with consistent gains across Python, Java, and C++.
AI.conclusion: STAB effectively generates test cases that expose algorithmic bottlenecks by leveraging problem specifications and adversarial construction principles, outperforming prior methods across multiple programming languages and LLM types.

title: Teacher-Student Representational Alignment for Reinforcement Learning-Driven Imitation Learning
comment: 6 pages, 5 figures. Accepted as an oral presentation at the RL4IL Workshop at ICRA 2026
summary: Imitation learning (IL) from a state-based reinforcement learning (RL) policy is a common approach to overcome the curse of dimensionality in complex and high-dimensional observation spaces prevalent in robotics. This paper addresses the irreducible imitation gap that emerges when teacher and student are learned in isolation, and the teacher policy has the liberty to rely on privileged state information that the student cannot infer from its observations. Instead of improving poor student performance with RL finetuning after IL, which often requires a whole new training setup, we propose a novel algorithm which learns a shared embedding space that hides agent-specific observations and thus trains imitable teacher policies by construction. We train the shared embedding space with self-supervised contrastive learning in parallel to the teacher policy and prevent it from extracting private information by limiting its gradients from updating the encoder networks. We perform evaluations on several example domains and compare to state-of-the-art baselines showing that our algorithm enables higher student performance with substantially reduced imitation gap.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a contrastive learning method to train teacher policies in a shared embedding space that hides privileged state information, reducing the imitation gap when students learn from teachers without needing RL finetuning.
AI.motivation: To overcome the irreducible imitation gap caused when teacher policies rely on privileged state information that student policies cannot access, without requiring expensive RL finetuning after imitation learning.
AI.method: A shared embedding space is learned via self-supervised contrastive learning, trained in parallel with the teacher policy. Gradient updates from the teacher are limited to prevent extraction of private (privileged) information, forcing the teacher to learn imitable behaviors.
AI.result: The proposed algorithm achieves higher student performance with substantially reduced imitation gap compared to state-of-the-art baselines across multiple example domains.
AI.conclusion: Training imitable teacher policies by construction through a shared, privacy-preserving embedding space is an effective alternative to post-IL RL finetuning, enabling better student imitation without additional training setups.

title: Geometry-Correct Diffusion Posterior Sampling with Denoiser-Pullback Curvature Guidance and Manifold-Aligned Damping
summary: Diffusion posterior sampling conditions diffusion priors on measurements, but data-consistency updates are typically scaled by hand-tuned guidance weights and can destabilize sampling under stiff, operator-dependent curvature. We replace scalar guidance with a per-noise-level damped Gauss--Newton correction computed in diffusion-state coordinates. The correction pulls likelihood gradients back through the denoiser, uses a one-sided curvature model that avoids forward denoiser Jacobians, and applies diffusion-calibrated rank-one damping aligned with the denoiser residual. Each correction is solved with matrix-free GMRES using automatic differentiation, and sampling proceeds with a variance-preserving Langevin transition with a closed-form drift/noise split. On FFHQ and ImageNet across inverse problems, it achieves competitive PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS while running markedly faster than most of the compared baselines; on accelerated MRI reconstruction, it achieves the best PSNR/SSIM among the compared baselines.

AI.tldr: Diffusion posterior sampling with per-level damped Gauss-Newton correction, using matrix-free GMRES and variance-preserving Langevin transitions, achieving competitive results across inverse problems and best MRI reconstruction.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion posterior sampling methods rely on hand-tuned guidance weights for data-consistency updates, which can destabilize sampling under stiff, operator-dependent curvature.
AI.method: Replaces scalar guidance with per-noise-level damped Gauss-Newton correction in diffusion-state coordinates; pulls likelihood gradients through denoiser; uses one-sided curvature model avoiding forward Jacobians; applies diffusion-calibrated rank-one damping; solved with matrix-free GMRES via automatic differentiation; uses variance-preserving Langevin transition with closed-form drift/noise split.
AI.result: Competitive PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS on FFHQ and ImageNet across inverse problems; faster runtime than most baselines; best PSNR/SSIM on accelerated MRI reconstruction among compared baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed damped Gauss-Newton correction with diffusion-calibrated damping provides a more stable and effective alternative to scalar guidance for diffusion posterior sampling, especially excelling in MRI reconstruction tasks.

title: AsyncTool: Evaluating the Asynchronous Function Calling Capability under Multi-Task Scenarios
summary: Large language model (LLM)-based agents have shown strong capabilities in using external tools to solve complex tasks. However, existing evaluations often overlook the temporal dimension of tool use, especially the impact of tool response latency, and are usually limited to single-task settings. In real-world applications, multiple tasks often need to be executed concurrently, and overall efficiency depends on whether an agent can use idle time while waiting for tool responses. We refer to this capability as asynchronous tool calling. To evaluate it, we propose AsyncTool, a benchmark for assessing LLM-based agents in interactive multi-task tool-use environments with delayed tool feedback. AsyncTool presents multiple heterogeneous tasks simultaneously and simulates realistic tool response latency during execution. Using a hybrid data evolution strategy, we construct a diverse asynchronous multitasking dataset that covers multiple scenarios and tool-use patterns. We evaluate models at the step, sub-task, and task levels, and introduce efficiency-oriented metrics to measure task coordination and completion efficiency. Extensive experiments show that delayed tool feedback poses substantial challenges to current agents and leads to clear performance degradation. Models that better coordinate task switching, dependency tracking, and state maintenance achieve stronger performance on AsyncTool. Our analysis identifies key failure modes of current tool-using agents and provides practical insights for designing future systems with stronger temporal reasoning and coordination capabilities.

AI.tldr: AsyncTool benchmarks LLM-based agents on asynchronous tool calling across concurrent multi-task scenarios with realistic tool response delays, revealing that delayed feedback significantly degrades agent performance and identifying key failure modes in temporal reasoning and task coordination.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of LLM-based tool-using agents overlook the temporal dimension (tool response latency) and are limited to single-task settings; real-world applications require concurrent task execution where agents must efficiently use idle time while waiting for tool responses.
AI.method: Proposes AsyncTool benchmark with a hybrid data evolution strategy to construct diverse asynchronous multitasking datasets across multiple scenarios and tool-use patterns; evaluates models at step, sub-task, and task levels with efficiency-oriented metrics.
AI.result: Delayed tool feedback causes clear performance degradation in current agents; models with better task switching, dependency tracking, and state maintenance achieve stronger results.
AI.conclusion: Current tool-using agents lack temporal reasoning and coordination capabilities for asynchronous multitasking; the paper provides practical insights for designing future systems with stronger temporal reasoning.

title: No Safe Dose: How Training Data Drives Unsafe Image Generation
summary: Text-to-image models trained on large-scale data often inevitably ingest unsafe content. While some people observe input-output amplifications, it remains unclear whether and how training data composition directly drives model output safety or by other factors. We shed light on this question by isolating this variable: we train the same text-to-image model on datasets that differ \emph{only} in their fraction of unsafe images (0\% to 9.6\%), across several dataset scales (100K to 8M). Then we generate images with the resulting models, and evaluate them with four independent safety classifiers. Output unsafety rises monotonically from 16.6\% at 0\% contamination to 25.5\% at 5\%. A factorial design reveals that the \emph{proportion}, not the absolute count, of unsafe training images is the operative variable. The 16.6\% irreducible baseline at zero contamination implicates the other components, e.g. frozen text encoder, as a residual safety risk -- confirmed by a text encoder ablation showing that SafeCLIP reduces this floor to 9.6\%, while the dose-response effect persists across all three encoders tested. Critically, no quality degradation in terms of FID, CLIPscore and ImageReward accompanies safety filtering. These results establish that data curation and text encoder safety are complementary and independently effective interventions. At the same time, the remaining level of unsafety poses questions for future research about emerging capabilities and compositionality.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether training data composition directly affects text-to-image model safety by systematically varying the fraction of unsafe images (0% to 9.6%) in training datasets across multiple scales (100K to 8M). Results show output unsafety rises monotonically with contamination proportion (16.6% at 0% to 25.5% at 5%), with proportion mattering more than absolute count. An irreducible 16.6% baseline at zero contamination reveals text encoder as a residual risk factor, confirmed by SafeCLIP reducing this floor to 9.6%. No quality degradation accompanies safety filtering.
AI.motivation: To understand whether training data composition directly drives model output safety or if other factors are responsible, given that text-to-image models trained on large-scale data often ingest unsafe content.
AI.method: Isolated the variable of training data contamination by training identical text-to-image models on datasets differing only in their fraction of unsafe images (0% to 9.6%) across multiple dataset scales (100K to 8M). Used factorial design to distinguish proportion vs. absolute count effects. Evaluated generated images with four independent safety classifiers and performed text encoder ablation with SafeCLIP.
AI.result: Output unsafety rises monotonically from 16.6% at 0% contamination to 25.5% at 5%. Proportion of unsafe images (not absolute count) is the operative variable. SafeCLIP reduces irreducible baseline from 16.6% to 9.6%. No quality degradation (FID, CLIPscore, ImageReward) accompanies safety filtering.
AI.conclusion: Data curation and text encoder safety are complementary and independently effective interventions for improving model safety, though residual unsafety remains as a challenge for future research on emerging capabilities and compositionality.

title: Patched-DeltaNet: Token-Level Event-Driven Memory for Linear-Time Anomaly Detection
summary: Time series anomaly detection is critical for maintaining the reliability of mission-critical systems. While Transformer-based models like PatchTST have shown remarkable performance, their $\mathcal{O}(L^2)$ computational complexity severely limits deployment in resource-constrained environments. In this paper, we propose Patched-DeltaNet, a novel architecture combining time-series patching with Gated Delta Networks. By integrating these paradigms, we hypothesize and demonstrate the emergence of token-level event-driven memory, whereby the patching mechanism extracts local semantic chunks, while the error-driven DeltaNet updates its recurrent state exclusively when significant physical changes, defined as deltas, occur. This synergy effectively filters out background noise and captures sudden anomalous drifts. Our rigorous experiments on the Server Machine Dataset (SMD) benchmark demonstrate the structural superiority and sample efficiency of Patched-DeltaNet. By strictly outperforming recent architectures under unified evaluation constraints and identical compute budgets, our model yields an ROC-AUC of 0.957 and PA-F1 of 0.822, while drastically reducing computational complexity to the theoretical minimum of $\mathcal{O}(L/P)$.

AI.tldr: Patched-DeltaNet combines time-series patching with Gated Delta Networks, achieving O(L/P) complexity and strong anomaly detection (ROC-AUC 0.957, PA-F1 0.822) on SMD benchmark.
AI.motivation: Existing Transformer-based models (e.g., PatchTST) have O(L²) computational complexity, limiting deployment in resource-constrained environments. Need for efficient anomaly detection in mission-critical systems.
AI.method: Combines time-series patching (extracting local semantic chunks) with Gated Delta Networks (error-driven recurrent state updates triggered by significant physical changes/deltas), forming token-level event-driven memory that filters noise and captures anomaly drifts.
AI.result: Achieves ROC-AUC of 0.957 and PA-F1 of 0.822 on SMD benchmark, with theoretical minimum complexity of O(L/P). Outperforms recent architectures under unified evaluation constraints and identical compute budgets.
AI.conclusion: The patching-delta synergy enables event-driven memory that effectively filters noise and captures anomalies while drastically reducing computational complexity to linear, making it suitable for resource-constrained deployments.

title: Reward Bias Substitution: Single-Axis Bias Mitigations Redirect Optimization Pressure
summary: Single-axis mitigations of reward-model biases (e.g., reducing proxy reliance on length, sycophancy, or style) can rotate optimization pressure onto correlated proxies rather than eliminate it, a failure mode we call reward bias substitution. The failure is enabled by a measurement-versus-optimization gap between audit and policy-induced distributions during mitigation evaluation and policy training. We formalize mitigation outcomes into a regime taxonomy and prove that successful mitigation, bias substitution, and overcorrection produce identical observables under any audit-distribution scoring, including ranking accuracy and win-rate, even when granted oracle access to the true reward. Across published preference-learning mitigation work, no method we survey reports the evidence needed to certify successful mitigation. Augmenting evaluation with policy-induced distributions while tracking multiple biases provably closes the gap, and we translate this into actionable prescriptions for mitigation methods and benchmarks. We demonstrate bias substitution in language model RLHF, where a length penalty during GRPO training compresses responses as intended yet redirects optimization pressure onto confidence calibration, driving the policy into overconfidence while factual free-form accuracy falls. We also show a published length-debiasing operator that zeroes reward-length correlation on the audit distribution but reintroduces bias under best-of-N selection on three of four SOTA reward models, and a length-sycophancy coupling whose direction reverses under human-LLM judge disagreement.

AI.tldr: Reward bias mitigation in RLHF often fails by substituting one bias for another, and current evaluation methods cannot detect this failure; the paper formalizes this problem and provides actionable solutions.
AI.motivation: Current reward-model debiasing techniques (e.g., penalizing length) can simply shift optimization pressure onto other correlated biases (like overconfidence) rather than eliminating it, and existing evaluation metrics fail to detect this substitution—motivating a formal analysis of this failure mode.
AI.method: The authors formalize mitigation outcomes into a regime taxonomy, prove that successful mitigation, bias substitution, and overcorrection produce identical audit-distribution observables, and propose augmenting evaluation with policy-induced distributions while tracking multiple biases to close the measurement-optimization gap.
AI.result: (1) Length penalty in GRPO compresses responses but redirects optimization onto confidence calibration, causing overconfidence and reduced factual accuracy. (2) A published length-debiasing operator zeroes reward-length correlation on audit distribution but reintroduces bias under best-of-N selection on 3/4 SOTA reward models. (3) Length-sycophancy coupling reverses direction under human-LLM judge disagreement.
AI.conclusion: Current preference-learning mitigation methods fail to report evidence needed to certify successful mitigation; the proposed evaluation framework (policy-induced distributions + multi-bias tracking) can close the detection gap, and actionable prescriptions for mitigation methods and benchmarks are provided.

title: A novel ordinal multi-view aggregation scheme for oak defoliation
summary:
Forest decline driven by climate and biotic stressors threatens ecosystem functioning, making accurate monitoring of tree health essential. In this work, we address tree defoliation estimation as an ordinal classification problem using ground-level imagery. We propose a novel multi-view ensemble framework that aggregates predictions from Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) trained on different perspectives of individual trees (north, south, and crown). This approach leverages complementary visual information while preserving modelling consistency through a homogeneous ensemble design.
  A comprehensive evaluation is conducted by comparing multiple ordinal classification methods and analysing the contribution of each view and their combinations. Results show that modelling the ordinal structure of defoliation levels improves performance over nominal approaches, while the proposed multi-view ensemble consistently outperforms single-view and pairwise configurations. In particular, the three-view ensemble achieves the most robust and accurate predictions across all evaluation metrics.
  These findings highlight the potential of combining Deep Learning (DL), Ordinal Classification (OC), and multi-view aggregation for scalable, consistent, and objective forest health assessment in complex ecosystems such as Mediterranean dehesas.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a multi-view ensemble of CNNs for tree defoliation estimation as an ordinal classification problem using ground-level imagery (north, south, crown views). The three-view ensemble outperforms single-view and pairwise configurations, demonstrating that combining Deep Learning, Ordinal Classification, and multi-view aggregation improves forest health assessment accuracy.
AI.motivation: Forest decline from climate and biotic stressors threatens ecosystem functioning, necessitating accurate and scalable tree health monitoring methods.
AI.method: Formulates tree defoliation estimation as an ordinal classification problem; uses a multi-view ensemble framework aggregating predictions from CNNs trained on three perspectives (north, south, crown) of individual trees via a homogeneous ensemble design.
AI.result: Ordinal classification outperforms nominal approaches; the three-view ensemble achieves the most robust and accurate predictions across all evaluation metrics.
AI.conclusion: Combining Deep Learning, Ordinal Classification, and multi-view aggregation enables scalable, consistent, and objective forest health assessment in complex ecosystems like Mediterranean dehesas.

title: Learning Compositional Latent Structure with Vector Networks
summary: Deep networks are powerful function approximators, but they typically store many different computations in shared weight matrices, making it difficult to selectively reuse or adapt parts of them when a familiar structure appears in novel combinations. We introduce the Vector Network (VN), a hierarchical recurrent architecture in which each layer replaces a fixed weight matrix with a library of reusable rank-1 weight atoms.
