summary: Vision-language segmentation models have recently achieved strong performance by leveraging high-level semantic object categories expressed in natural language. However, this semantic dependence limits their ability to reason about intrinsic visual properties such as shape, geometry, or texture, which are essential in many real-world applications. In this work, we introduce Semantic-Agnostic aNd Shape-Aware (SANSA) segmentation, a new paradigm that requires segmentation models to operate solely from non-semantic textual descriptions. To this end, we propose two strategies to generate SANSA segmentation prompts based on either dictionary constraints or example guidance, both generating semantic-agnostic textual descriptions. These prompts are then used to finetune segmentation models under semantic-agnostic supervision. Experiments show that finetuning on SANSA prompts yields up to a 20% mIoU improvement on this new segmentation task, compared to pretrained state-of-the-art models, while maintaining strong performance on standard semantic prompts. These results highlight the importance of low- and mid-level visual reasoning for improving the generalization and controllability of vision-language segmentation models.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces SANSA segmentation, a new paradigm where vision-language models segment objects based on non-semantic (shape/texture/geometry) descriptions rather than object categories, achieving up to 20% mIoU improvement while maintaining performance on standard semantic prompts.
AI.motivation: Existing vision-language segmentation models rely on high-level semantic categories (e.g., "dog", "car"), limiting their ability to reason about intrinsic visual properties like shape, geometry, or texture, which are crucial for real-world applications.
AI.method: Two strategies to generate Semantic-Agnostic aNd Shape-Aware (SANSA) prompts: (1) dictionary-based constraints, (2) example guidance. These non-semantic textual descriptions are used to finetune segmentation models under semantic-agnostic supervision.
AI.result: Finetuning on SANSA prompts yields up to 20% mIoU improvement on the new segmentation task compared to pretrained SOTA models, while maintaining strong performance on standard semantic prompts.
AI.conclusion: Low- and mid-level visual reasoning (shape, geometry, texture) is important for improving generalization and controllability of vision-language segmentation models.

title: Joint Training of Multi-Token Prediction in Reinforcement Learning via Optimal Coefficient Calibration
summary: Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has emerged as the standard paradigm for improving reasoning capability of large language models, while Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) has been a widely adopted module in pretraining. Combining them is a natural approach, yet current RL practices detach MTP gradients because joint training degrades the performance. We revisit this failure from an optimization perspective. We show that the per-step effect of MTP on the RL objective can be decomposed into two terms: a first-order correlation and a second-order perturbation penalty. This decomposition unifies three MTP training regimes: Detach, Cross-Entropy loss, and Policy loss, and explains why each succeeds or fails. Further analysis of policy loss reveals that, although it aligns with intuition, performance still degrades: the correlation term decays while the quadratic penalty persists. Guided by the analysis, we propose Optimal Coefficient Calibration (OCC), an adaptive scheme that tracks the optimal coefficient online via a log-probability proxy at negligible cost. Across six competition-level mathematical reasoning benchmarks, OCC consistently matches or exceeds the detach baseline, delivering improved joint MTP-RL training performance.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes why joint Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) and Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) training degrades performance, decomposes MTP's effect on RL objective into correlation and perturbation terms, and proposes Optimal Coefficient Calibration (OCC) to adaptively balance them, achieving improved performance on math reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current RL practices detach MTP gradients because joint training degrades performance. The authors aim to understand and resolve this failure from an optimization perspective rather than treating it as an empirical artifact.
AI.method: The authors decompose MTP's per-step effect on the RL objective into a first-order correlation term and a second-order perturbation penalty. This unifies three training regimes (Detach, Cross-Entropy loss, Policy loss). They propose Optimal Coefficient Calibration (OCC), an adaptive scheme that tracks the optimal coefficient online via a log-probability proxy at negligible cost.
AI.result: Across six competition-level mathematical reasoning benchmarks, OCC consistently matches or exceeds the detach baseline, delivering improved joint MTP-RL training performance.
AI.conclusion: The decomposition explains why different MTP training regimes succeed or fail. The proposed OCC method effectively resolves the joint training degradation by adaptively calibrating coefficients, enabling MTP and RL to be trained together beneficially.

title: Gradient Step Plug-and-Play Model for Dental Cone-Beam CT Reconstruction
comment: CT Meeting 2026 - 9th International Conference on Image Formation in X-Ray Computed Tomography, Jun 2026, Salt lake City, United States
summary: The goal of this work is to reduce the effect of photon noise in dental cone-beam CT reconstruction. We consider an inverse problem formulation and develop a databased prior. To this end, we simulate fan-beam acquisitions and add photon noise to the projection data. The prior is obtained by training a gradient-step denoiser using reconstructed simulated acquisitions. The trained model is integrated into a plug-and-play gradient-step algorithm to reconstruct images from simulated projections. Experiments on synthetic data demonstrate the denoising capabilities of the trained model, while qualitative evaluations on real images showcase the algorithm's performance and generalization ability.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a plug-and-play gradient-step denoising prior to reduce photon noise in dental cone-beam CT reconstruction, demonstrating effectiveness on both synthetic and real data.
AI.motivation: To reduce the effect of photon noise in dental cone-beam CT reconstruction, which degrades image quality.
AI.method: Formulates an inverse problem, trains a gradient-step denoiser as a databased prior using simulated fan-beam acquisitions with photon noise, and integrates it into a plug-and-play gradient-step reconstruction algorithm.
AI.result: The trained model shows strong denoising capabilities on synthetic data and good performance and generalization ability on real images.
AI.conclusion: The proposed plug-and-play gradient-step approach effectively reduces photon noise in dental CBCT reconstruction and generalizes well to real-world data.

title: Bound-Constrained Sparse Representation for Electrical Impedance Tomography
summary: This study proposes a bound-constrained sparse representation (BC-SR) framework for electrical impedance tomography (EIT), aimed at improving conductivity estimation without explicit regularization. BC-SR adopts a representation-driven strategy, generating conductivity from low-dimensional latent variables via an implicit composite parameterization. Structural priors are embedded using a truncated graph-Laplacian basis, while a bound-preserving nonlinear mapping enforces admissible conductivity ranges and improves conditioning through implicit gradient modulation. The approach ensures robust convergence, even under noisy or incomplete data. Extensive validation on 2D/3D simulations, tank experiments, and in-vivo lung data shows that BC-SR improves physical consistency and structural fidelity, offering enhanced robustness compared to traditional methods. Additionally, BC-SR enables 3D time-difference EIT reconstruction, offering improved spatial resolution and a more coherent representation of 3D conductivity distributions, particularly for in-vivo lung data. This suggests potential for improved performance in EIT, particularly in clinical applications for respiratory monitoring.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a bound-constrained sparse representation (BC-SR) framework for electrical impedance tomography (EIT) that improves conductivity estimation without explicit regularization, using latent variables, truncated graph-Laplacian basis, and bound-preserving nonlinear mapping. Validated on 2D/3D simulations, tank experiments, and in-vivo lung data, BC-SR shows better physical consistency, structural fidelity, and robustness than traditional methods, with potential for clinical respiratory monitoring.
AI.motivation: To improve conductivity estimation in EIT without relying on explicit regularization, enhancing robustness and physical consistency under noisy or incomplete data conditions.
AI.method: BC-SR uses a representation-driven strategy: low-dimensional latent variables generate conductivity via implicit composite parameterization; structural priors are embedded using a truncated graph-Laplacian basis; a bound-preserving nonlinear mapping enforces admissible conductivity ranges and improves conditioning through implicit gradient modulation.
AI.result: Extensive validation on 2D/3D simulations, tank experiments, and in-vivo lung data shows improved physical consistency, structural fidelity, and robustness. BC-SR also enables 3D time-difference EIT reconstruction with better spatial resolution and coherent 3D conductivity distributions.
AI.conclusion: BC-SR offers a robust and physically consistent EIT reconstruction method, with promising potential for clinical applications, particularly in respiratory monitoring.

title: Hierarchical Synthetic Tabular Data Generation: A Hybrid Top-Down and Bottom-Up Framework
comment: Accepted as a poster at FMSD @ ICML 2026. 9 pages, 6 figures
summary: Existing approaches for synthetic tabular data generation are based on either purely generative models or LLMs, both of which struggle with data heterogeneity, logical consistency, rare-event coverage, and robustness in low-data regimes. In this paper, we propose a hierarchical hybrid top-down and bottom-up (H-TDBU) framework that decouples semantic structures from stochastic texture. In the top-down path, structure-driven logical constraints and cross-modal alignment rules are constructed, while in the bottom-up path, lightweight tabular generators are used to learn local statistical patterns from real data. The two paths are consolidated in a unified synthesis engine with an iterative feedback loop. We evaluate the framework on weak multimodal financial benchmarks combining tabular and sentiment-text data. Experimental results show that our H-TDBU approach improves train-synthetic-test-real performance over neural baseline methods while preserving semantic consistency. Our results suggest that hierarchical rule-guided synthesis provides an effective mechanism for combining controllability, semantic coherence, and statistical fidelity in synthetic data generation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes H-TDBU, a hierarchical hybrid top-down and bottom-up framework for synthetic tabular data generation that decouples semantic structures from stochastic texture. It combines structure-driven logical constraints (top-down) with lightweight local pattern learning (bottom-up) via an iterative feedback loop, outperforming neural baselines on weak multimodal financial benchmarks while preserving semantic consistency.
AI.motivation: Existing synthetic tabular data generation methods (purely generative models or LLMs) struggle with data heterogeneity, logical consistency, rare-event coverage, and robustness in low-data regimes. There is a need for an approach that combines controllability, semantic coherence, and statistical fidelity.
AI.method: A hierarchical hybrid top-down and bottom-up (H-TDBU) framework: (1) Top-down path constructs structure-driven logical constraints and cross-modal alignment rules; (2) Bottom-up path uses lightweight tabular generators to learn local statistical patterns from real data; (3) The two paths are consolidated via a unified synthesis engine with an iterative feedback loop.
AI.result: Evaluated on weak multimodal financial benchmarks (tabular + sentiment-text data). H-TDBU improves train-synthetic-test-real performance over neural baseline methods while preserving semantic consistency.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical rule-guided synthesis provides an effective mechanism for combining controllability, semantic coherence, and statistical fidelity in synthetic data generation, particularly in heterogeneous and low-data settings.

title: Do Clinical Models Change Treatment Decisions?
summary: Clinical foundation models are evaluated with factual or exam-style medical QA, but treatment decisions must change when patient context changes. We introduce ClinPivot, an auditable treatment-decision benchmark built from biomedical relations and pivoted patient contexts. ClinPivot asks whether models change treatment choices when new clinical constraints shift the action space. We find that strong medical QA performance does not reliably predict decision-making performance: frontier models and task-adapted Qwen variants often fail to change decisions correctly, and model rankings shift across evaluation regimes. Decision-structured supervision improves pivot-sensitive decision-making and medical QA under matched knowledge budgets, while lightweight replay reduces losses in general assistant ability.

AI.tldr: ClinPivot is a new benchmark testing whether clinical AI models adjust treatment decisions when patient context changes, revealing that strong medical QA performance doesn't predict good decision-making, and that models often fail to pivot correctly.
AI.motivation: Existing clinical foundation models are evaluated on factual or exam-style medical QA, but real treatment decisions require adapting to changing patient contexts—a gap the authors aim to address.
AI.method: ClinPivot is built from biomedical relations and pivoted patient contexts; it tests whether models change treatment choices when new clinical constraints shift the action space.
AI.result: Strong medical QA performance does not reliably predict decision-making performance; frontier models and Qwen variants often fail to change decisions correctly; model rankings shift across evaluation regimes.
AI.conclusion: Decision-structured supervision improves pivot-sensitive decision-making and medical QA under matched knowledge budgets, while lightweight replay reduces losses in general assistant ability.

title: Sketch2Motion: Text-driven 2D Sketch to 3D Animation via Diffusion-guided Skeleton Optimization
summary: Animation of 2D hand-drawn sketches provides an effective medium for visual communication. However, these sketches pose challenges, particularly in handling occlusions and accurately mapping motion. While 3D animation naturally addresses these challenges, estimating 3D motion remains a very complex task. Recent approaches to converting 2D sketches to 3D animations have mainly focused on specific types of motion, such as bipedal movements and facial expressions. We propose Sketch2Motion, a diffusion-guided framework for skeleton-based motion synthesis that combines classical character animation pipelines with deep generative priors. Our method represents motion using skeletal transformations, which are propagated to mesh deformations via linear blend skinning. To guide the resulting animation toward realistic and semantically meaningful motion, we integrate a text-to-video diffusion model via motion-aware score-distillation sampling (MoSDS), enabling optimization without paired motion data. Additionally, we apply physics-inspired smoothness, topological, and contact constraints to stabilize optimization and preserve motion plausibility. Further, we integrate a spring-mass simulator to introduce secondary motion effects. The proposed framework is generalized, fully differentiable, modular, and compatible with biped, quadruped, and non-living articulated characters. Experiments demonstrate that our approach produces temporally coherent, text-aligned animations that outperform baseline motion transfer methods that lack generative priors or explicit physical constraints. We will make our code and dataset publicly available.

AI.tldr: Sketch2Motion is a diffusion-guided framework that converts 2D hand-drawn sketches into 3D animations by combining skeleton-based motion synthesis with text-to-video diffusion priors, physics constraints, and a spring-mass simulator, enabling realistic, temporally coherent animations for biped, quadruped, and non-living articulated characters.
AI.motivation: 2D hand-drawn sketch animation faces occlusion and motion mapping challenges; 3D animation addresses these naturally but estimating 3D motion is complex, and existing methods are limited to specific motion types like bipedal movement and facial expressions.
AI.method: Represent motion via skeletal transformations propagated to mesh using linear blend skinning; integrate text-to-video diffusion model via motion-aware score-distillation sampling (MoSDS) for optimization without paired data; apply physics-inspired smoothness, topological, contact constraints; add spring-mass simulator for secondary motion effects.
AI.result: Produces temporally coherent, text-aligned animations that outperform baseline motion transfer methods lacking generative priors or physical constraints; generalizes to biped, quadruped, and non-living articulated characters.
AI.conclusion: Sketch2Motion offers a generalized, fully differentiable, modular framework that effectively bridges 2D sketch animation and 3D motion synthesis, with public code and dataset release planned.

title: Geometry-First Generative Spatial Single-Cell Reconstruction
comment: 32nd SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD 2026)
summary: Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) profiles large numbers of cells but loses spatial context, whereas spatial transcriptomics (ST) preserves partial spatial structure at lower resolution. Most existing integration methods either deconvolve spot mixtures or map cells onto a measured spot lattice, which ties reconstructions to a fixed grid and slide-specific coordinate systems, a limitation that is especially problematic in unpaired settings. We propose GEARS, a geometry-first framework that reconstructs an intrinsic single-cell spatial geometry guided by ST, without relying on cell-type labels, histological images, or cell-to-spot assignment. GEARS first learns a domain-invariant expression encoder that aligns ST spots and dissociated cells, and then trains a permutation-equivariant generator with a diffusion-based refiner with EDM-style preconditioning to generate local spatial geometries under pose-invariant supervision derived from ST coordinates. At inference, GEARS reconstructs geometry on many overlapping subsets of scRNA-seq cells, aggregates predicted pairwise distances across subsets, and solves a global distance-geometry problem to obtain canonical two-dimensional coordinates and a dense distance matrix. Extensive quantitative and qualitative experiments, including cross-section generalization, show that GEARS consistently improves global distance preservation, local neighborhood fidelity, and spatial distribution alignment compared to strong spatial mapping and deconvolution baselines.

AI.tldr: GEARS is a geometry-first framework that reconstructs single-cell spatial geometries from scRNA-seq and spatial transcriptomics data without relying on cell-type labels, histological images, or cell-to-spot assignment, achieving superior global and local spatial fidelity.
AI.motivation: Existing integration methods for scRNA-seq and ST data are tied to fixed grids and slide-specific coordinate systems, limiting their applicability in unpaired settings where spatial context needs to be reconstructed without predefined spatial anchors.
AI.method: GEARS learns a domain-invariant expression encoder to align ST spots and dissociated cells, trains a permutation-equivariant generator with diffusion-based refiner (EDM-style preconditioning) under pose-invariant supervision from ST coordinates, and at inference reconstructs geometry by aggregating predicted pairwise distances across overlapping subsets to solve a global distance-geometry problem.
AI.result: GEARS consistently outperforms strong spatial mapping and deconvolution baselines in global distance preservation, local neighborhood fidelity, and spatial distribution alignment across extensive experiments including cross-section generalization.
AI.conclusion: The geometry-first approach effectively decouples spatial reconstruction from fixed coordinate grids, enabling flexible and accurate spatial organization inference for unpaired scRNA-seq and ST data.

title: Data-Efficient On-Policy Distillation for Automatic Speech Recognition
summary: Building competitive automatic speech recognition (ASR) models usually requires large-scale au- dio supervision, which makes reproduction and specialization expensive. We study Ark-ASR, a 0.6B- parameter audio-conditioned language model trained with 100k hours of speech, and examine whether a strong Qwen-ASR teacher can transfer additional recognition capability through on-policy distillation. Across Mandarin and English ASR benchmarks, the proposed training recipe consistently improves over supervised fine-tuning alone and outperforms the same-scale Qwen3-ASR-0.6B baseline on four of five evaluation sets. This is achieved with only 100k hours of speech, compared with the 20M hours of super- vised audio reported for the Qwen3-Omni AuT encoder. The larger Qwen3-ASR-1.7B remains stronger, but the results show that teacher-guided on-policy training can substantially close the gap for compact ASR models under a much smaller audio budget. A support-overlap diagnostic further suggests that the teacher-data stage improves local student-teacher compatibility, matching recent analyses of when on-policy distillation is effective.

AI.tldr: Ark-ASR, a 0.6B audio-conditioned language model, uses on-policy distillation from a strong Qwen-ASR teacher to improve ASR performance using only 100k hours of speech, outperforming same-scale baselines and narrowing the gap with larger models trained on 20M hours.
AI.motivation: Large-scale ASR models require massive audio supervision (e.g., 20M hours), making reproduction and specialization expensive. The authors aim to reduce this data requirement while maintaining competitive performance.
AI.method: Train a 0.6B-parameter audio-conditioned language model (Ark-ASR) with 100k hours of speech using on-policy distillation from a stronger Qwen-ASR teacher, comparing against supervised fine-tuning alone and a same-scale Qwen3-ASR-0.6B baseline.
AI.result: The proposed recipe consistently improves over supervised fine-tuning alone and outperforms Qwen3-ASR-0.6B on 4/5 evaluation sets, using only 100k hours vs. 20M hours. Qwen3-ASR-1.7B remains stronger, but the gap is substantially narrowed. A support-overlap diagnostic confirms that the teacher-data stage improves local student-teacher compatibility.
AI.conclusion: Teacher-guided on-policy training can substantially close the performance gap for compact ASR models under a much smaller audio budget, matching theoretical analyses of when on-policy distillation is effective.

title: Adaptive Temporal Gating of Longitudinal Magnetic Resonance Imaging for Alzheimer's Prediction
summary: Predicting conversion from Mild Cognitive Impairment (MCI) to Alzheimer's Disease (AD) is critical for early intervention. Current deep learning paradigms predominantly rely on cross-sectional structural MRI, neglecting prognostic value in patient-specific anatomical trajectories. We introduce the Temporal Adaptive Fusion Network (TAF-Net), a hybrid CNN-Transformer architecture that models paired longitudinal 3D MRI scans. Central to TAF-Net is a Temporal Fusion Module governed by an Adaptive Temporal Gate, which learns patient-specific weightings to synthesize three spatiotemporal representations: explicit structural change, region-to-region temporal cross-attention, and bilateral feature concatenation. Evaluated on the Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative cohort for three-year MCI-to-AD conversion prediction, TAF-Net achieved the highest discriminative performance among all evaluated methods using only structural MRI, significantly outperforming the strongest baseline and approaching multimodal methods requiring PET, CSF, or genetic data. The architecture exhibited exceptional data efficiency, matching baseline performance with a fraction of training data. Ablation studies demonstrate that longitudinal fusion improves discrimination while reducing predictive variance by 48% compared to single-timepoint evaluation. Interpretability analyses reveal spatial attention aligned with established AD pathology in the medial temporal lobe and ventricles, while the gating mechanism prioritizes explicit volumetric change with strong positive correlation to conversion risk.

AI.tldr: TAF-Net is a hybrid CNN-Transformer that uses paired longitudinal 3D MRI scans with an Adaptive Temporal Gate to predict MCI-to-AD conversion, outperforming all structural MRI baselines and approaching multimodal methods.
AI.motivation: Current deep learning methods rely on cross-sectional structural MRI and neglect the prognostic value of patient-specific anatomical trajectories over time for predicting MCI-to-AD conversion.
AI.method: TAF-Net uses a hybrid CNN-Transformer architecture with a Temporal Fusion Module governed by an Adaptive Temporal Gate, synthesizing three spatiotemporal representations: explicit structural change, region-to-region temporal cross-attention, and bilateral feature concatenation.
AI.result: TAF-Net achieved the highest discriminative performance among all evaluated methods using only structural MRI, significantly outperforming the strongest baseline, approaching multimodal methods (PET, CSF, genetic), and reducing predictive variance by 48% compared to single-timepoint evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Longitudinal fusion with adaptive temporal gating improves conversion prediction accuracy and data efficiency, with interpretable attention aligning with known AD pathology in the medial temporal lobe and ventricles.

title: Refining Multidimensional Video Reward Models via Disentangled Influence Functions
summary: As Text-to-Video (T2V) generation models continue to evolve, the complexity of video evaluation necessitates a fine-grained assessment across various axes. To address this, recent works have focused on developing Multidimensional Video Reward Models (MVRMs), which decompose the evaluation process to better align with the multifaceted nature of human visual perception. However, training effective MVRMs is fundamentally challenged by the complex nature of video data. In this work, we identify a critical phenomenon termed Dimensional Heterogeneity: the reliability of a training sample can vary substantially across evaluation dimensions, meaning that a sample may provide reliable supervision for one objective while inducing high supervision risk for another. Consequently, prevailing data-centric methods that filter based on global scalar metrics are ill-posed for T2V tasks. To address this, we propose a disentangled influence framework that that efficiently estimates dimension-specific supervision risk. Leveraging this framework, we introduce two dimension-disentangled refinement strategies: Dimension-Disentangled Pruning, which removes extreme high-risk samples, and Dimension-Disentangled Reweighting, which softly down-weights high-risk supervision. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our disentangled strategies significantly outperform global filtering baselines, yielding reward models with superior alignment to ground truth.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies "Dimensional Heterogeneity" in T2V evaluation where samples vary in reliability across different dimensions. The authors propose a disentangled influence framework with dimension-specific strategies (pruning and reweighting) to build better Multidimensional Video Reward Models, outperforming global filtering methods.
AI.motivation: Training effective Multidimensional Video Reward Models (MVRMs) is challenged by the complex nature of video data. The paper identifies that samples can be reliable for one evaluation dimension but risky for another, making global filtering approaches inadequate.
AI.method: The authors propose a disentangled influence framework to estimate dimension-specific supervision risk, along with two strategies: (1) Dimension-Disentangled Pruning (removing extreme high-risk samples) and (2) Dimension-Disentangled Reweighting (softly down-weighting high-risk supervision).
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed disentangled strategies significantly outperform global filtering baselines, producing reward models with superior alignment to ground truth.
AI.conclusion: Dimension-specific handling of supervision risk is crucial for T2V evaluation, and the proposed disentangled framework offers an effective solution for training better multidimensional reward models.

title: Deconstructing Spatial Complexity: Hierarchical Decomposition for LLM Spatial Reasoning
summary: LLMs have shown remarkable proficiency in general language understanding and reasoning. However, they consistently underperform in spatial reasoning that severely limits their application, particularly in embodied intelligence. Inspired by the success of hierarchical reinforcement learning, this paper introduces a novel method for hierarchical task decomposition in LLM spatial reasoning. Our approach guides LLMs to decompose complex tasks into manageable sub-tasks by identifying key intermediate states and generating simplified sub-environments. However, we identify that LLMs often fail to derive optimal intermediate states due to their insufficient spatial prior, leading to sub-optimal task decomposition. To address this limitation and enhance its planning capability, we propose the MCTS-Guided Group Relative Policy Optimization (M-GRPO), where we reformulate the UCT formula by incorporating the LLM's prior predictive probabilities alongside its epistemic uncertainty. Furthermore, we implement a more fine-grained advantage function, enabling the model to learn optimal path planning. Experimental results demonstrate that our method substantially improves LLM performance on spatial tasks, including navigation, planning, and strategic games, achieving state-of-the-art results. This work paves the way for LLMs in real-world applications.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces M-GRPO, a method combining hierarchical task decomposition with Monte Carlo Tree Search-guided policy optimization to improve LLM spatial reasoning, achieving state-of-the-art results on navigation, planning, and strategic games.
AI.motivation: LLMs consistently underperform in spatial reasoning, limiting their application in embodied intelligence. The paper aims to bridge this gap by enhancing LLMs' spatial planning capabilities.
AI.method: (1) Hierarchical task decomposition approach that breaks complex spatial tasks into sub-tasks via key intermediate states and simplified sub-environments. (2) MCTS-Guided Group Relative Policy Optimization (M-GRPO), which reformulates the UCT formula using LLM's prior predictive probabilities and epistemic uncertainty, plus a fine-grained advantage function for optimal path planning.
AI.result: Substantial improvement on spatial reasoning tasks including navigation, planning, and strategic games, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.conclusion: This work paves the way for applying LLMs in real-world embodied intelligence applications by overcoming their spatial reasoning limitations.

title: EgoRelight: Egocentric Human Capture and Illumination Recovery for Relightable and Photoreal Avatar Rendering
summary: Mixed Reality (MR) headsets promise a future of immersive telepresence where virtual humans blend indistinguishably into real or virtual surroundings. Achieving this vision requires a method for capturing a user's motion, estimating appearance under novel lighting, and understanding the environment - all from the constrained viewpoint of a head-mounted display (HMD). Existing approaches treat these as isolated problems: they either focus on driving avatars with baked-in lighting or rely on studio setups for relighting. In this paper, we present EgoRelight, a holistic framework for egocentric telepresence that simultaneously captures full-body human performance, synthesizes photorealistic and relightable appearance, and estimates high dynamic range (HDR) environment maps from a single HMD. First, to ensure motion and surface reconstruction, we propose an egocentric perception module that leverages stereo down-facing cameras to extract dense depth maps, which serve as geometric control signals to drive a mesh-based avatar. Second, we introduce a novel neural appearance model that learns to synthesize view-dependent specular and view-independent diffuse shading separately. By employing a specialized ray-sampling strategy, our model generalizes to unseen illumination without relying on restrictive analytical BRDF priors. Third, we enable seamless avatar integration into the physical world via a test-time inverse rendering process, which recovers an HDR environment map by matching the pre-trained avatar's appearance to live egocentric camera observations. We demonstrate our system through a social telepresence application, where remote users are coherently relit according to their physical environment. Extensive experiments show that our components and the integrated system significantly outperform state-of-the-art baselines in geometric accuracy and rendering as well as relighting fidelity.

AI.tldr: EgoRelight is a holistic framework for egocentric MR telepresence that simultaneously captures full-body motion, synthesizes relightable photorealistic avatars, and estimates HDR environment maps from a single HMD, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.motivation: Current MR telepresence approaches treat motion capture, appearance modeling, and environment lighting as isolated problems, often relying on studio setups or baked-in lighting. A unified system that works from the constrained viewpoint of a single HMD is needed for truly immersive telepresence.
AI.method: (1) An egocentric perception module using stereo down-facing cameras to extract dense depth maps for driving a mesh-based avatar. (2) A neural appearance model that separately learns view-dependent specular and view-independent diffuse shading with a specialized ray-sampling strategy for generalization to unseen illumination. (3) A test-time inverse rendering process that recovers HDR environment maps by matching pre-trained avatar appearance to live egocentric camera observations.
AI.result: The system significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in geometric accuracy, rendering quality, and relighting fidelity, demonstrated through a social telepresence application where remote users are coherently relit according to their physical environment.
AI.conclusion: EgoRelight demonstrates that holistic egocentric telepresence—simultaneously capturing motion, relightable appearance, and environment lighting from a single HMD—is achievable, enabling seamless avatar integration into real physical surroundings for immersive MR experiences.

title: Robust Contrastive Graph Clustering with Adaptive Local-Global Integration
summary: Graph clustering is essential in graph analysis for revealing structural patterns and node communities. Despite recent advances in self-supervised contrastive learning that have improved clustering via structural and attribute signals, existing methods still struggle to flexibly capture high-order local structures and often overlook global semantics in complex graphs. These limitations lead to suboptimal node representations, especially in real-world graphs with fragmented structures and ambiguous cluster boundaries. To address these limitations, a contrastive graph clustering framework is proposed to jointly integrate multi-scale local structures with global semantics via attention mechanisms. At the local level, GNN-based topological signals extracted from multiple propagation depths are adaptively fused through attention-based weighting to capture multi-scale neighborhood features. At the global level, semantic prototypes derived from dynamically evolving cluster centers are adaptively aggregated through attention to guide node representations and enhance inter-cluster separability. The model is trained under a dual-view contrastive learning paradigm with a hybrid objective that combines instance-level and structure-aware losses to improve representation robustness and discrimination. Experiments on eight real-world graph datasets demonstrate that our method achieves competitive clustering performance. Code is available at https://github.com/vege12138/w2.

AI.tldr: A contrastive graph clustering framework that integrates multi-scale local structures with global semantics via attention mechanisms, achieving competitive performance on eight real-world datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing self-supervised contrastive clustering methods fail to flexibly capture high-order local structures and overlook global semantics in complex graphs, leading to suboptimal node representations, especially in graphs with fragmented structures and ambiguous cluster boundaries.
AI.method: (1) Local level: GNN-based topological signals from multiple propagation depths are adaptively fused via attention weighting to capture multi-scale neighborhood features. (2) Global level: Semantic prototypes from dynamically evolving cluster centers are adaptively aggregated through attention to guide node representations and enhance inter-cluster separability. (3) Training: Dual-view contrastive learning with a hybrid objective combining instance-level and structure-aware losses.
AI.result: Experiments on eight real-world graph datasets demonstrate competitive clustering performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses limitations of existing methods by jointly integrating multi-scale local structures and global semantics, improving clustering on complex real-world graphs.

title: Adaptive Reservoir Computing for Multi-Scenario Chaotic System Forecasting
summary: We present an adaptive reservoir computing framework for the CTF-4-Science Lorenz benchmark, which evaluates machine learning models across twelve distinct tasks spanning five qualitatively different scenarios: baseline forecasting, noisy signal reconstruction, forecasting under noise, few-shot learning, and parametric generalization. Rather than applying a uniform inference strategy, we tailor the training and prediction procedure of Echo State Networks (ESNs) to the specific demands of each evaluation scenario. Our key contributions are fourfold: (1) exact reservoir state synchronization that eliminates warmup approximation error in short-time prediction; (2) histogram-guided candidate selection that directly optimizes the long-time ergodic evaluation metric; (3) multi-seed reservoir search for few-shot regimes with severely limited training data; and (4) sequential multi-sequence training that resolves state-distribution mismatch in parametric generalization tasks. The proposed framework achieves a score of 74.91 on the public benchmark leaderboard, demonstrating that carefully adapted reservoir computing constitutes a competitive and computationally efficient approach for diverse chaotic system modeling challenges.

AI.tldr: An adaptive reservoir computing framework using Echo State Networks, tailored to different tasks in the CTF-4-Science Lorenz benchmark, achieves a score of 74.91 on the leaderboard through four key techniques: exact synchronization, histogram-guided selection, multi-seed search, and sequential multi-sequence training.
AI.motivation: To address the limitation of applying a uniform inference strategy across diverse chaotic system modeling tasks (baseline forecasting, noisy reconstruction, forecasting under noise, few-shot learning, parametric generalization) by proposing an adaptive framework that tailors ESN training to each scenario.
AI.method: Four core techniques: (1) exact reservoir state synchronization eliminating warmup approximation error; (2) histogram-guided candidate selection optimizing long-time ergodic metrics; (3) multi-seed reservoir search for few-shot learning; (4) sequential multi-sequence training resolving state-distribution mismatch in parametric generalization.
AI.result: Score of 74.91 on the CTF-4-Science Lorenz benchmark public leaderboard.
AI.conclusion: Carefully adapted reservoir computing is competitive and computationally efficient for diverse chaotic system modeling challenges.

title: VITAL: Visual-Semantic Dual Supervision for Enhanced and Interpretable Latent Reasoning in Medical MLLMs
summary: Latent reasoning enables reasoning over continuous hidden states rather than explicit tokens, avoiding the language bottleneck and inference overhead of chain-of-thought for medical VQA. However, existing methods suffer from modality collapse, insufficient visual supervision, and train-inference mismatch. Moreover, their opaque latent states offer no interpretability, which is critical in clinical applications. We propose VITAL, a latent-space reasoning framework for medical MLLMs with visual-semantic dual supervision: an auxiliary text decoder reconstructs reasoning chains from latent states, while a visual projector regresses ROI features from a frozen, independent medical vision encoder. Both modules are discarded at inference with zero overhead, yet can be re-attached post-hoc for dual interpretability, providing textual and visual explanations of the reasoning process without sacrificing efficiency. We construct a 61K dataset spanning 9 imaging modalities, exceeding prior medical visual latent reasoning datasets by an order of magnitude. Experiments on 7 benchmarks show that VITAL consistently and substantially outperforms the backbone, all latent reasoning baselines, and medical MLLMs trained on far larger data, achieving state-of-the-art results competitive with trillion-parameter proprietary models.

AI.tldr: VITAL is a latent-space reasoning framework for medical MLLMs that uses dual visual-semantic supervision to improve reasoning quality and interpretability without inference overhead, achieving SOTA on 7 benchmarks with a 61K multi-modality dataset.
AI.motivation: Existing latent reasoning methods suffer from modality collapse, insufficient visual supervision, train-inference mismatch, and lack of interpretability—critical issues for clinical applications.
AI.method: VITAL uses dual supervision: (1) an auxiliary text decoder reconstructs reasoning chains from latent states, (2) a visual projector regresses ROI features from a frozen medical vision encoder. Both are discarded at inference but can be re-attached post-hoc for interpretability. A 61K dataset across 9 modalities is constructed.
AI.result: Outperforms backbone, all latent reasoning baselines, and medical MLLMs trained on larger data across 7 benchmarks; achieves SOTA competitive with trillion-parameter proprietary models.
AI.conclusion: Dual supervision effectively addresses modality collapse and train-inference mismatch, while enabling zero-overhead reasoning with post-hoc interpretability, making it suitable for clinical deployment.

title: PhAME: Phenotype-Aware Molecular Editing via Latent Diffusion
summary: Small-molecule drug discovery requires simultaneous optimization of numerous properties of candidate molecules. These properties can be investigated through the analysis of high-dimensional biological signatures, such as cell morphology and transcriptomic perturbations, which provide a rich perspective on the underlying biological mechanisms. However, existing generative methods, which use those signatures for optimization, fail to meet two key requirements: providing precise guidance toward desired phenotypic signatures while maintaining structural proximity to a known hit. We introduce PhAME (Phenotype-Aware Molecular Editing), a latent diffusion framework that overcomes this challenge by recasting molecular optimization as editing in the latent space of a pretrained graph-based VAE. Our central contribution is a compositional classifier-free guidance scheme with two independent scales, one for the phenotype-conditioning and one for similarity to the seed structure, allowing practitioners to control the tradeoff between these two objectives. Empirical evaluations across diverse benchmarks, including docking score optimization and multimodal phenotypic generation, demonstrate that PhAME achieves state-of-the-art results while maintaining high chemical validity and novelty.

AI.tldr: PhAME is a latent diffusion framework for small-molecule drug discovery that edits molecules in VAE latent space using compositional classifier-free guidance with independent control over phenotype conditioning and structural similarity, achieving state-of-the-art results in docking optimization and phenotypic generation.
AI.motivation: Existing generative methods fail to simultaneously provide precise guidance toward desired phenotypic signatures while maintaining structural proximity to a known hit molecule.
AI.method: PhAME uses a latent diffusion framework built on a pretrained graph-based VAE, with a compositional classifier-free guidance scheme featuring two independent scales to control the tradeoff between phenotype-conditioning and similarity to the seed structure.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art results across diverse benchmarks including docking score optimization and multimodal phenotypic generation, while maintaining high chemical validity and novelty.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach effectively overcomes the challenge of balancing phenotypic guidance and structural similarity, enabling practical molecular optimization for drug discovery.

title: OR-Space: A Full-Lifecycle Workspace Benchmark for Industrial Optimization Agents
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents are increasingly used to assist with operations research (OR) modeling, yet existing OR-oriented benchmarks often reduce evaluation to one-shot translation from a self-contained problem statement into a mathematical formulation or solver program. Such settings abstract away two characteristics of real industrial OR workflows: persistent multi-artifact workspaces and multi-stage task lifecycles. We introduce OR-Space, a full-lifecycle workspace benchmark for evaluating industrial optimization agents across model construction, model revision, and grounded explanation. Each instance is an executable workspace containing business documents, structured data, optional code artifacts, solver outputs, and task-specific evaluators distributed across interdependent files. OR-Space defines three task modes: Build, where agents construct solver-ready optimization models from heterogeneous artifacts; Revise, where agents modify existing models under changing requirements or solver feedback while preserving valid prior logic; and Explain, where agents answer grounded questions about solutions, constraints, and business implications using evidence spread across workspace artifacts. By combining persistent workspaces with lifecycle-oriented tasks, OR-Space evaluates whether agents can perform reliable optimization work beyond end-to-end text generation. We describe the benchmark design, evaluation protocol, and quality-control pipeline, and position OR-Space as a benchmark for studying the reliability, failure modes, and practical readiness of LLM agents in industrial OR workflows.

AI.tldr: OR-Space is a full-lifecycle workspace benchmark for evaluating LLM agents on industrial optimization tasks across three modes (Build, Revise, Explain) using persistent multi-artifact workspaces, moving beyond one-shot translation benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing OR-oriented benchmarks reduce evaluation to one-shot translation from self-contained problem statements, ignoring real industrial OR workflows which involve persistent multi-artifact workspaces and multi-stage task lifecycles.
AI.method: OR-Space defines three task modes (Build, Revise, Explain) within executable workspaces containing business documents, structured data, code artifacts, solver outputs, and task-specific evaluators distributed across interdependent files.
AI.result: The benchmark evaluates agents on constructing solver-ready optimization models (Build), modifying existing models under changing requirements (Revise), and answering grounded questions using evidence across workspace artifacts (Explain).
AI.conclusion: OR-Space provides a more realistic benchmark for studying reliability, failure modes, and practical readiness of LLM agents in industrial OR workflows beyond end-to-end text generation.

title: Anomaly as Non-Conformity via Training-Free Graph Laplacian Energy Minimization
summary: Detecting subtle visual anomalies in images remains challenging, particularly when only normal samples are available a priori. Such unsupervised anomaly detection is typically solved by measuring feature similarity of a query patch to a memory of normal patches. However, similarity alone does not reveal how strongly a query patch violates the structure of the normal feature manifold. We propose a training-free Laplacian graph energy optimization formulation, named ANoCo that scores Anomaly by the cost of Non-Conformity of a query patch to align with a fixed normal manifold. For each query patch, we construct a bipartite query to normal graph weighted by cosine affinity, explicitly removing query-query and normal-normal edges to prevent evidence dilution. We formulate anomaly scoring as a convex Laplacian energy with anchored normal nodes, and solve in closed form. In particular, we do not use the optimized features themselves-the anomaly score is the magnitude of the update required to satisfy normality constraints, reframing the graph Laplacian as a non-conformity operator rather than a smoothing prior. The proposed method introduces no learnable parameters, message passing, or sampling, and has complexity comparable to a single linear solve. Across standard benchmarks, it delivers strong image-level AUROC, stable localization maps, and improved robustness over prior methods, demonstrating the effectiveness of using optimization-induced feature drift as anomaly measure.

AI.tldr: ANoCo detects anomalies by measuring how much a query patch needs to change (feature drift) to conform to a normal feature manifold, using a closed-form Laplacian graph energy optimization with no training required.
AI.motivation: Existing unsupervised anomaly detection methods measure feature similarity between query and normal patches, but similarity alone fails to capture how strongly a query violates the structure of the normal feature manifold.
AI.method: Constructs a bipartite graph between query and normal patches (no query-query or normal-normal edges), formulates anomaly scoring as convex Laplacian energy optimization with anchored normal nodes, and solves in closed form. Anomaly score = magnitude of feature update required to satisfy normality constraints.
AI.result: Strong image-level AUROC, stable localization maps, and improved robustness over prior methods on standard benchmarks, with no learnable parameters, message passing, or sampling.
AI.conclusion: Using optimization-induced feature drift as an anomaly measure is effective; the graph Laplacian acts as a non-conformity operator rather than a smoothing prior.

title: IRDS: Interpretable RLVR Data Selection via Verifier-Coupled Sparse Autoencoder Coverage
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has become a key technique for en- hancing LLM reasoning, yet its data ineffi- ciency remains a major bottleneck. Existing methods address this problem only partially, each missing at least one of subset-level cov- erage, verifier signal use, or interpretability. To address this gap, we present IRDS (Inter- pretable RLVR Data Selection), which selects RLVR training instances on a sparse autoen- coder (SAE) cluster basis so the selection itself is auditable on recognizable problem motifs. To select instances the model both fails on and can still learn from, we introduce a verifier- coupled coverage objective on the SAE basis and solve it by greedy log-determinant max- imization. Experiments on three instruction- tuned models and six math reasoning bench- marks show that IRDS achieves the highest overall accuracy, exceeding the strongest base- line by +3.9/+4.0 pp on the two Qwen models and by +0.5 pp on Llama-3.1-8B, while run- ning an order of magnitude cheaper than the trajectory-based baseline.

AI.tldr: IRDS selects RLVR training data using SAE clusters, achieving better accuracy and efficiency than prior methods for LLM reasoning.
AI.motivation: Existing RLVR data selection methods are incomplete—they miss at least one of subset-level coverage, verifier signal use, or interpretability, leading to data inefficiency.
AI.method: IRDS selects training instances on a sparse autoencoder (SAE) cluster basis, using a verifier-coupled coverage objective optimized via greedy log-determinant maximization.
AI.result: IRDS achieves highest accuracy across three models (Qwen: +3.9/+4.0 pp, Llama-3.1-8B: +0.5 pp) and runs 10x cheaper than trajectory-based baselines.
AI.conclusion: IRDS demonstrates that interpretable, verifier-aware subset selection via SAE clustering can significantly improve both effectiveness and efficiency of RLVR for LLM reasoning.

title: Look on Demand: A Cognitive Scheduling Framework for Visual Evidence Acquisition in Multimodal Reasoning
summary: Existing multimodal reasoning approaches predominantly follow two paradigms: converting visual inputs into text prior to reasoning, or performing end-to-end reasoning within a unified vision-language representation space. Despite their empirical progress, both paradigms suffer from fundamental structural limitations. The former relies on static visual-to-text conversion, which tends to compress and lose fine-grained visual details. The latter is prone to linguistic dominance induced by joint optimization and attention mechanisms, leading to systematically weakened faithfulness to visual evidence during reasoning. In this work, we argue that a central challenge is how and when visual evidence is introduced into the reasoning process. Motivated by this insight, we propose CSMR, a multimodal reasoning framework in which a language model controls the reasoning process by deciding when to invoke an independent visual perception module to acquire task-relevant visual evidence. Experiments across multiple multimodal reasoning benchmarks show that CSMR consistently outperforms representative baseline methods in accuracy under a zero-shot setting. Further experimental analysis confirms that these advantages primarily arise from the proposed cognitive scheduling mechanism.

AI.tldr: CSMR improves multimodal reasoning by having a language model control when to invoke a separate visual perception module, avoiding information loss from static visual-to-text conversion and linguistic dominance from unified representations.
AI.motivation: Both existing paradigms for multimodal reasoning (converting visual inputs to text before reasoning, and end-to-end reasoning in a unified space) have fundamental structural limitations: the former loses fine-grained visual details, while the latter suffers from linguistic dominance that weakens faithfulness to visual evidence.
AI.method: CSMR (Cognitive Scheduling for Multimodal Reasoning) framework where a language model controls the reasoning process by deciding when to invoke an independent visual perception module to acquire task-relevant visual evidence.
AI.result: CSMR consistently outperforms representative baseline methods in accuracy under zero-shot settings across multiple multimodal reasoning benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The advantages of CSMR primarily arise from the proposed cognitive scheduling mechanism, which properly controls when and how visual evidence is introduced during reasoning.

title: Bayesian Gated Non-Negative Contrastive Learning
summary: While Contrastive Learning (CL) has revolutionized self-supervised representation learning, its latent representations remain highly entangled and opaque, limiting their interpretability in safety-critical applications. We identify that a fundamental cause of this entanglement is the reliance on deterministic similarity measures, which treat all feature dimensions equally. In compositional scenes, this creates an Optimization Conflict: common background features, such as, "blue sky", are encouraged to align in positive pairs but simultaneously repelled in negative pairs, causing gradient oscillations that hinder precise semantic disentanglement. To address this, we propose BayesNCL (Bayesian Gated Non-Negative Contrastive Learning). Unlike standard approaches, BayesNCL introduces a probabilistic gating mechanism that dynamically filters out task-irrelevant, high-frequency common features while selectively retaining discriminative semantics. By formalizing feature selection as a variational inference problem with a sparse Bernoulli prior, our method effectively resolves the optimization conflict. Empirical experimental results on Imagenet-100 demonstrate that BayesNCL achieves a remarkable 142.1% improvement in semantic consistency compared to state-of-the-art baselines, yielding highly interpretable representations without compromising downstream task performance. Code is available at https://github.com/Cui-Peng-624/BayesNCL.

AI.tldr: BayesNCL introduces a Bayesian gated mechanism for contrastive learning that filters out common background features, improving semantic consistency by 142.1% on ImageNet-100 while maintaining downstream performance.
AI.motivation: Current contrastive learning produces entangled, uninterpretable representations due to deterministic similarity measures that treat all features equally, causing "Optimization Conflict" where common background features create gradient oscillations between positive and negative pairs.
AI.method: BayesNCL (Bayesian Gated Non-Negative Contrastive Learning) uses a probabilistic gating mechanism with a sparse Bernoulli prior, formalizing feature selection as a variational inference problem to dynamically filter out task-irrelevant high-frequency common features while retaining discriminative semantics.
AI.result: On ImageNet-100, BayesNCL achieves a 142.1% improvement in semantic consistency over SOTA baselines, yielding highly interpretable representations without compromising downstream task performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Bayesian gating mechanism effectively resolves the optimization conflict in contrastive learning, enabling more interpretable and semantically meaningful representations for safety-critical applications.

title: Parameter-Efficient Generative Modeling with Controlled Vector Fields
summary: We introduce a continuous-time generative modeling framework, motivated by the Chow-Rashevskii theorem, that builds expressive flows from a small set of fixed vector fields and learned scalar controls. Instead of learning an unconstrained high-dimensional vector field, our framework constructs the velocity by modulating fixed vector fields with learned scalar control functions. When the fixed fields are bracket-generating, their Lie algebra spans the ambient space, providing a mechanism for expressive transport with only a small number of learned control channels and offering a parameter-efficient geometric alternative to standard vector-field parameterizations. This decoupled formulation yields a structured and interpretable generative model in which the number of learned scalar output channels can be chosen independently of the ambient dimension. We formulate an expressivity principle showing that, under suitable controllability and well-posedness assumptions, such controlled flows can transport a source distribution to a target distribution. We train the resulting model using a continuous-normalizing-flow likelihood objective and present proof-of-concept experiments on synthetic distributions.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a continuous-time generative model where flows are built from fixed vector fields and learned scalar controls, inspired by the Chow-Rashevskii theorem, offering a parameter-efficient alternative to standard vector-field parameterizations.
AI.motivation: To develop a more parameter-efficient and interpretable generative modeling framework by leveraging the Chow-Rashevskii theorem and bracket-generating vector fields, reducing the need for learning high-dimensional vector fields.
AI.method: Constructs velocity fields by modulating fixed, bracket-generating vector fields with learned scalar control functions, trained via a continuous-normalizing-flow likelihood objective.
AI.result: Proof-of-concept experiments on synthetic distributions demonstrate the feasibility of this geometric, control-based approach to generative modeling.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a structured, interpretable, and parameter-efficient alternative to standard generative models, with expressivity guaranteed by controllability assumptions.

title: OccuReward: LLM-Guided Occupant-Centric Reward Shaping for Demographic Equity in Grid-Interactive Buildings
comment: 4 pages, 2 figures. Accepted at OccuSys 2026, co-located with ACM Sustainability Week 2026. Preprint version
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated promising capability in generating reward functions for deep reinforcement learning (DRL)-based building energy management. However, their potential to exhibit or exacerbate disparities in occupant comfort across heterogeneous demographic populations remains unexplored. We present OccuReward, a framework investigating how LLM-mediated reward design affects demographic equity. Our contribution is three-fold: the introduction of the Comfort Equity Index (CEI) as a novel feedback signal; a methodology for iterative, equity-aware LLM reward shaping; and a performance analysis of DRL agents under these refined objectives. Utilizing four empirically grounded occupant profiles from the ASHRAE Global Thermal Comfort Database II (13,440 votes), we deploy a Soft Actor-Critic agent in CityLearn v2. Our approach employs the Gemini API to generate reward function logic and weights--rather than performing per-step inference--across three refinement rounds. Results across 15 experimental runs reveal that elderly female occupants consistently experience the lowest satisfaction in initial rounds. By Round 3, equity-aware LLM refinement activates specific reward components that improve satisfaction for Young Males (+17.6%), Mid-aged Females (+28.2%), Health Sensitive (+53.8%), and Elderly Females (+567%), while simultaneously reducing energy costs by 3.2%. Our findings highlight that while reward-level intervention significantly improves equity, demographic disparities in AI-driven controllers persist, necessitating further research into algorithmic fairness in building systems.

AI.tldr: OccuReward introduces an LLM-mediated reward design framework for DRL-based building energy management that iteratively refines reward functions to improve demographic equity in occupant comfort. Using four occupant profiles from ASHRAE data, equity-aware refinement improved satisfaction across all groups (Elderly Females +567%) while reducing energy costs by 3.2%, though demographic disparities persist.
AI.motivation: LLMs show promise in generating reward functions for DRL-based building energy management, but their potential to create or worsen disparities in occupant comfort across different demographic populations remains unexplored. The paper addresses this gap by investigating how LLM-mediated reward design affects demographic equity.
AI.method: The framework introduces the Comfort Equity Index (CEI) as a feedback signal, uses iterative equity-aware LLM reward shaping via Gemini API (generating reward logic and weights rather than per-step inference), deploys a Soft Actor-Critic agent in CityLearn v2, and evaluates across four occupant profiles from ASHRAE Global Thermal Comfort Database II (13,440 votes) over three refinement rounds and 15 experimental runs.
AI.result: Elderly female occupants consistently had the lowest satisfaction initially. By Round 3, equity-aware refinement improved satisfaction for Young Males (+17.6%), Mid-aged Females (+28.2%), Health Sensitive (+53.8%), and Elderly Females (+567%), while simultaneously reducing energy costs by 3.2%.
AI.conclusion: Reward-level intervention significantly improves equity in DRL-based building energy management, but demographic disparities in AI-driven controllers persist, necessitating further research into algorithmic fairness in building systems.

title: BiasEdit: A Training-Free Bias-Detect-and-Edit Framework for Learning Fair Visual Classifiers
comment: Accepted to The Web Conference 2026 (formerly WWW) as an Oral presentation
summary: Visual data from the Web power image classifiers, which often underpin many web services, such as recommendation and content moderation. However, the raw Web data often contain spurious correlations and social biases, and neural networks are known for their tendency to learn biases present in data. This can reinforce unfairness in web services and the web data, leading to a vicious cycle. In the context of image classification, networks learn bias attributes for a specific class when a majority of images contain the same attribute only for a given class. Hence, training a fair and debiased classifier from a biased dataset demands handling an imbalanced problem between a majority of images with bias attributes (bias-aligned samples) and a minority without (bias-conflict samples). In this work, we introduce BiasEdit, a modular framework that automatically detects bias attributes from the original dataset and edits them to construct a debiased dataset. Specifically, BiasEdit first detects unknown bias attributes via statistical dependence and mutual information analysis of visual-linguistic representations, and then explicitly edits those attributes using text-guided image editing to generate realistic bias-conflict samples. Unlike prior works that assume known bias attributes or relies on synthetic mixing, our method operates without manual annotations and can leverage off-the-shelf vision-language and editing models. BiasEdit addresses a fundamental challenge in Web-sourced visual AI, mitigating dataset-induced bias and achieving state-of-the-art debiasing performance even when training data are fully biased.

AI.tldr: BiasEdit is a modular framework that automatically detects and edits bias attributes in visual datasets using vision-language models and text-guided image editing, enabling state-of-the-art debiased image classification without manual annotations.
AI.motivation: Web-sourced visual data often contain spurious correlations and social biases that neural networks learn and amplify, creating a vicious cycle of unfairness in web services like recommendation and content moderation.
AI.method: BiasEdit first detects unknown bias attributes via statistical dependence and mutual information analysis of visual-linguistic representations, then explicitly edits those attributes using text-guided image editing to generate realistic bias-conflict samples.
AI.result: BiasEdit achieves state-of-the-art debiasing performance, even when training data are fully biased, and operates without manual annotations by leveraging off-the-shelf vision-language and editing models.
AI.conclusion: BiasEdit addresses a fundamental challenge in Web-sourced visual AI by mitigating dataset-induced bias through automatic detection and editing, breaking the vicious cycle of bias amplification in web services.

title: Dynamic Topic Modeling with a Higher-Order Hypergraphical Representation
summary: Dynamic topic modeling is widely used to analyze evolving trends in scientific literature, medical records, and social media. Traditional topic models represent each topic through a single probability vector on the multinomial simplex and implicitly couple word occurrence and repetition within one probabilistic mechanism. However, this formulation restricts the dependence structure among words and overlooks informative higher-order interactions, particularly in dynamic corpora with overlapping semantics. To address these limitations, we introduce a hypergraph representation of text where each document is modeled as a hyperedge connecting all co-occurring words, with repetition intensities encoded as node weights. This representation naturally separates word occurrence from repetition and induces a novel hypergraph-based multinomial distribution with a nonlinear normalization depending on the observed word set of each document. Building on this likelihood, we develop a dynamic topic modeling framework via structured low-rank factorizations with explicit temporal regularization on topic-word profiles. Moreover, we establish local convergence guarantees and derive non-asymptotic error bounds despite the intrinsic nonconvexity induced by bilinear factorization and document-specific nonlinear normalization. Numerical experiments on synthetic data and an application to the International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR) corpus demonstrate consistent improvements over existing multinomial-based topic models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a hypergraph-based representation for dynamic topic modeling that separates word occurrence from repetition, using a novel hypergraph multinomial distribution with nonlinear normalization, low-rank factorizations, and temporal regularization, with theoretical convergence guarantees and empirical improvements on synthetic data and ICLR corpus.
AI.motivation: Traditional dynamic topic models represent topics as single probability vectors on the multinomial simplex, which couples word occurrence and repetition in one mechanism, restricting dependence structure and overlooking higher-order interactions in dynamic corpora with overlapping semantics.
AI.method: A hypergraph representation where documents are hyperedges connecting co-occurring words with node weights for repetition intensities; a hypergraph-based multinomial distribution with nonlinear normalization; dynamic topic modeling via structured low-rank factorizations with explicit temporal regularization on topic-word profiles.
AI.result: Local convergence guarantees and non-asymptotic error bounds established despite nonconvexity; numerical experiments on synthetic data and ICLR corpus show consistent improvements over existing multinomial-based topic models.
AI.conclusion: The hypergraph-based approach effectively addresses limitations of traditional topic models by decoupling word occurrence from repetition and capturing higher-order interactions, yielding better performance in dynamic settings.

title: Localizing Input Uncertainty Quantification for Large Language Models via Shapley Values
summary: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly integrated into high-stakes decision-making, the ability to reliably quantify uncertainty has become a critical requirement for safety and trust. However, current uncertainty quantification methods primarily operate at the output level, often failing to distinguish whether uncertainty arises from the model's lack of knowledge or from ambiguity in the user's input. While input-centric uncertainty quantification has recently emerged as a promising direction, it remains relatively underexplored and typically relies on coarse, input-level information. Consequently, users are provided with scalar uncertainty scores that offer little actionable guidance on which parts of the input should be clarified to improve reliability. To address this limitation, we propose Shapley-based input uncertainty Quantification (ShaQ), a framework for span-level attribution of input-induced uncertainty. Our approach models ambiguous spans in the input as players in a cooperative game and quantifies their contributions using Shapley values, defined via the weighted average of marginal reductions in conditional entropy obtained by clarifying each span coalition. Unlike existing input-level approaches, our formulation captures complex interactions among spans and provides a principled decomposition in which individual attributions sum exactly to the total input-induced uncertainty. We evaluate ShaQ on the AmbigQA and AmbiEnt benchmarks, where it achieves state-of-the-art performance in ambiguity detection. We further demonstrate its utility on MediTOD, showing that ShaQ can localize under-specified clinical utterances and facilitate human-AI collaboration in high-stakes settings. Overall, ShaQ improves uncertainty estimation and provides actionable insights for targeted input clarification.

AI.tldr: ShaQ is a Shapley-based framework that attributes input-induced uncertainty to specific spans in text, helping users identify which parts of ambiguous input need clarification for more reliable LLM outputs.
AI.motivation: Current uncertainty quantification methods fail to distinguish whether uncertainty stems from model knowledge gaps vs. ambiguous user inputs, and provide only coarse scalar scores without actionable guidance on which input parts need clarification.
AI.method: ShaQ models ambiguous spans as players in a cooperative game, using Shapley values to quantify each span's contribution to input-induced uncertainty via weighted average of marginal reductions in conditional entropy when clarifying span coalitions.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on AmbigQA and AmbiEnt benchmarks for ambiguity detection, and demonstrated utility on MediTOD for localizing under-specified clinical utterances.
AI.conclusion: ShaQ improves uncertainty estimation by providing span-level attribution, enabling targeted input clarification and facilitating human-AI collaboration in high-stakes settings.

title: REVEAL: Reference-Grounded Reasoning for Multimodal Manipulation Detection
summary: Multimodal manipulation detection aims to simultaneously identify forged image--text pairs and localize tampered regions, yet existing methods typically rely on memorizing isolated artifacts and struggle with imperceptible manipulation traces or domain shifts. Inspired by human comparative reasoning, we reformulate this task as a reference-grounded verification problem, where authenticity is assessed by comparing a query against retrieved authentic evidence. We propose REVEAL Reference-Enabled Verification for Evidence Analysis and Localization), a framework explicitly designed for this comparative paradigm. To support this paradigm, we construct a large-scale reference library comprising 170K authentic news image--text pairs featuring over 40K public figures. Technically, REVEAL employs a difference-aware fusion mechanism to capture fine-grained discrepancies between the query and retrieved evidence. Furthermore, we introduce a task-decoupled Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture to jointly execute instance-level detection and fine-grained grounding, effectively mitigating optimization conflicts between these heterogeneous objectives. Extensive experiments demonstrate that REVEAL significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, and notably enables \emph{training-free domain adaptation} by simply updating the reference library, offering a robust and practical solution for detecting evolving misinformation. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/REVEAL-Reference-A006.

AI.tldr: REVEAL is a reference-grounded framework for multimodal manipulation detection that compares query image-text pairs against retrieved authentic evidence, using difference-aware fusion and task-decoupled MoE architecture, achieving SOTA performance with training-free domain adaptation via library updates.
AI.motivation: Existing methods memorize isolated artifacts and struggle with imperceptible manipulation traces or domain shifts. The authors aim to reformulate detection as a reference-grounded verification problem inspired by human comparative reasoning.
AI.method: Constructs a 170K reference library of authentic news pairs; employs difference-aware fusion for fine-grained query-evidence comparison; uses task-decoupled Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) to jointly handle detection and grounding without optimization conflicts.
AI.result: Significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods; enables training-free domain adaptation by simply updating the reference library.
AI.conclusion: The reference-grounded comparative paradigm offers a robust and practical solution for detecting evolving misinformation, with adaptability across domains without retraining.

title: Commit to the Bit: Reactive Reinforcement Learning Done Right
summary: Reinforcement learning algorithms are commonly analyzed (and designed) under the Markov assumption. This is unrealistic, as most environments encountered in practice are either partially observable, or require function approximation that restricts the agent to access non-Markovian state features. We consider the problem of learning an optimal reactive policy in a finite environment with deterministic observations (or equivalently, hard state aggregation). We introduce a new algorithm, Committed Q-learning, and prove almost-sure convergence to the optimal reactive policy under an intuitive assumption we call rewire-robustness. This assumption is strictly weaker than the $q_\star$-realizability condition used in prior work. Our algorithm is a variant of classical Q-learning in which the behavior policy commits to a single action upon entering a feature, and only resamples actions when the observed feature changes. A crucial part of our analysis is the introduction of quasi-Markov environments.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Committed Q-learning, a reinforcement learning algorithm that converges to optimal reactive policies in non-Markovian environments with deterministic observations, under a weaker assumption than prior methods.
AI.motivation: Standard RL algorithms assume Markovian environments, which is unrealistic for real-world scenarios involving partial observability or function approximation that produces non-Markovian features.
AI.method: Committed Q-learning is introduced, which modifies classical Q-learning by having the behavior policy commit to a single action upon entering a feature and only resample when the observed feature changes. The analysis introduces quasi-Markov environments.
AI.result: The algorithm achieves almost-sure convergence to the optimal reactive policy under a "rewire-robustness" assumption, which is strictly weaker than the $q_*$-realizability condition used in prior work.
