They propose a self-bootstrapping paradigm where a small-scale seed model generates filtered trajectories that activate Plan in any target model, avoiding external distillation.
AI.result: The self-bootstrapping pipeline successfully activates Plan in every tested model (3B-14B range) and consistently outperforms competitive baselines on multi-hop QA benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that successful training of retrieval-augmented reasoning agents requires not just reward design but also model-specific feasibility (initial entropy, training stability, prerequisite sub-skills). Their self-bootstrapping paradigm eliminates the need for distillation from stronger external models.

title: AREA: Attribute Extraction and Aggregation for CLIP-Based Class-Incremental Learning
comment: Accepted to ICML 2026. Code is available at https://github.com/LAMDA-CL/ICML2026-AREA
summary: Class-Incremental Learning (CIL) is important in building real-world learning systems. In CLIP-based CIL, the model performs classification by comparing similarity between visual and textual embeddings obtained from template prompts, e.g., ``a photo of a [CLASS]''. This seemingly monolithic matching process can be decomposed into two conceptually distinct stages: attribute extraction and attribute aggregation. For example, a model may recognize cat using attributes such as fur texture and whiskers. When learning a new class like car, the model must extract additional attributes like wheels and adjust how they are aggregated in the shared representation space. However, since only data from the current task is available, incremental updates can bias both attribute extraction and aggregation toward new classes, leading to catastrophic forgetting. Therefore, we propose AREA for attribute extraction and aggregation in CLIP-based CIL. To stabilize extraction, we anchor class-level visual and textual attributes on the hyperspherical embedding space via principal geodesic analysis. To stabilize aggregation, we learn lightweight task-specific experts with scoring and residual refinement, regularized by a variational information bottleneck objective. During inference, we perform routing over task attribute manifolds via optimal transport for more concise prediction. Experiments show that AREA consistently outperforms SOTA methods. Code is available at https://github.com/LAMDA-CL/ICML2026-AREA.

AI.tldr: AREA proposes a method for CLIP-based Class-Incremental Learning that decomposes classification into attribute extraction and aggregation, using principal geodesic analysis for stable extraction and task-specific experts with information bottleneck for stable aggregation.
AI.motivation: Class-Incremental Learning suffers from catastrophic forgetting when updating models incrementally with new classes, as both attribute extraction and attribute aggregation become biased toward new classes due to limited data availability.
AI.method: (1) Stabilizes attribute extraction via principal geodesic analysis on hyperspherical embedding space. (2) Stabilizes aggregation via lightweight task-specific experts with scoring and residual refinement, regularized by variational information bottleneck. (3) Inference uses optimal transport routing over task attribute manifolds.
AI.result: AREA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods on CLIP-based Class-Incremental Learning benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Decomposing the monolithic CLIP matching process into attribute extraction and aggregation, with targeted stabilization techniques for each, effectively mitigates catastrophic forgetting in incremental learning scenarios.

title: Latent Diffusion for Missing Data
summary: Diffusion models have emerged as powerful generative approaches for missing-data imputation, yet most existing methods operate directly in data space and degrade when training data are heavily incomplete. We investigate whether shifting diffusion to a learned latent representation improves robustness under missing-completely-at-random (MCAR) corruption. To this end, we propose a two-stage framework: a robust VAE-based imputer first learns compact semantic features from incomplete observations, and a diffusion model is then trained in the resulting latent space. Across training missing rates, we perform a controlled comparison against pixel-space diffusion models under the same incomplete-data setting. The latent diffusion model maintains high sample quality and remains stable up to 50\% missingness, while pixel-space diffusion degrades progressively as missingness increases. For downstream imputation, latent diffusion also achieves consistently better performance than pixel-space diffusion. These findings indicate that latent-space modeling mitigates artifact amplification from zero-imputed inputs and provides a more robust generative prior for incomplete-data learning. Overall, our results support latent diffusion as a strong and practically useful alternative to pixel-space diffusion for missing-data problems.

AI.tldr: Latent diffusion models are more robust than pixel-space diffusion for missing-data imputation under MCAR corruption, maintaining quality up to 50% missingness.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion models for missing-data imputation operate in data space and degrade with heavily incomplete training data. The authors investigate whether shifting diffusion to a learned latent representation improves robustness.
AI.method: A two-stage framework: (1) a robust VAE-based imputer learns compact semantic features from incomplete observations, (2) a diffusion model is trained in the resulting latent space.
AI.result: Latent diffusion maintains high sample quality up to 50% missingness and achieves consistently better downstream imputation performance compared to pixel-space diffusion, which degrades progressively.
AI.conclusion: Latent-space modeling mitigates artifact amplification from zero-imputed inputs and provides a more robust generative prior, supporting latent diffusion as a strong alternative for missing-data problems.

title: From Knowing to Doing: A Memory-Controlled Benchmark for LLM Trading Agents on Stock Markets
summary:
Evaluating whether large language model (LLM) agents can profit in capital markets is increasingly framed as end-to-end trading: place an agent in a historical market, let it trade, and measure portfolio returns. This setup is vulnerable to two evaluation failures. First, long backtests often overlap with the knowledge cutoffs of frontier LLMs, allowing memorized tickers, dates, prices, and market narratives to substitute for investment reasoning. Second, raw returns are a noisy proxy for stock-selection ability, since positive performance may come from market beta, style exposure, or favorable regimes rather than genuine alpha.
  We introduce KTD-Fin (Knowing-To-Doing Financial Benchmark), an end-to-end stock-market trading benchmark that addresses both issues. KTD-Fin uses a data-side masking protocol to anonymize key identifiers and calendar information consistently across prompts and tools, separating historical market memory from investment decision-making. It also incorporates a Barra-style performance attribution framework that decomposes portfolio returns into market, style, and stock-selection alpha components.
  Across ten frontier LLM agents evaluated on the Chinese CSI300 over a 2024--2026 window, masking substantially changes agent rationales, pushing them towards anonymized factor-based reasoning. Attribution analysis further shows that LLM agents' cumulative returns under leakage-controlled evaluation are largely explained by passive market and style exposure, with limited evidence of persistent stock-selection alpha. These findings suggest that financial LLM benchmarks should evaluate not only whether an agent makes money, but also whether the source of returns reflects transferable investment skill. We release KTD-Fin as a reproducible template for leakage-controlled and attribution-aware evaluation of LLM trading agents.

AI.tldr: KTD-Fin is a financial benchmark that prevents data leakage (by anonymizing identifiers/dates) and decomposes returns (market, style, alpha) to fairly evaluate whether LLM trading agents actually possess stock-selection skill, finding most "profits" stem from passive exposures rather than genuine alpha.
AI.motivation: Current end-to-end LLM trading evaluations suffer from two failures: (1) backtests overlap with LLM training data, letting memorized market knowledge substitute for reasoning; (2) raw returns conflate market beta/style exposure with genuine stock-selection skill.
AI.method: KTD-Fin uses (a) a masking protocol anonymizing identifiers and calendar info across prompts/tools, and (b) a Barra-style attribution framework decomposing returns into market, style, and stock-selection alpha components.
AI.result: Across 10 frontier LLMs on CSI300 (2024-2026), masking shifted reasoning toward anonymized factor-based analysis; attribution showed returns largely explained by passive market/style exposure with limited persistent alpha.
AI.conclusion: Financial LLM benchmarks should evaluate not just whether agents make money, but whether return sources reflect transferable skill. KTD-Fin provides a reproducible template for leakage-controlled, attribution-aware evaluation.

title: HarmoVid: Relightful Video Portrait Harmonization
summary: We present a method for harmonizing the lighting of a foreground video to match a target background scene, adjusting shadows, color tone, and illumination intensity (relightful harmonization). Unlike images, acquiring labeled data for videos, where identical motions are recorded under different lighting conditions, is practically infeasible and non-scalable. While one way to create such paired data is to apply existing image-based harmonization models frame by frame to a video, the resulting outputs often suffer from significant temporal jitters. We overcome this problem by introducing a novel lighting deflickering model that can stabilize the global and local lighting flickering artifacts. Our video diffusion model learns from these upgraded deflickered data with a volume of real and synthetic videos to generate high-quality video harmonization results. We further propose an asymmetric alpha mask conditioning technique to learn the clean boundaries from real videos. Experiments demonstrate that our model achieves strong temporal coherence, naturalness, cleaner boundaries, and physically meaningful lighting behavior, while maintaining strong relighting expressiveness compared to prior image-based and video-based harmonization methods.

AI.tldr: A video lighting harmonization method using a lighting deflickering model and video diffusion to match foreground videos to target background scenes with temporal coherence.
AI.motivation: Labeled video data for lighting harmonization is infeasible to collect, and image-based frame-by-frame harmonization causes temporal jitters.
AI.method: Proposes a lighting deflickering model to stabilize flickering artifacts, a video diffusion model trained on deflickered data, and asymmetric alpha mask conditioning for clean boundaries.
AI.result: Achieves strong temporal coherence, naturalness, cleaner boundaries, and physically meaningful lighting behavior with strong relighting expressiveness.
AI.conclusion: The method outperforms prior image-based and video-based harmonization methods in video lighting harmonization.

title: Bilinear Coordinate Alignment for Training-Free Task-Vector Transfer
summary: Fine-tuning large-scale pre-trained models is a recent prevalent paradigm for adapting general representations to specialized tasks. However, when a new version of a pre-trained model becomes available, expertise acquired through fine-tuning cannot be directly reused because it is tied to the parameterization of the original model, requiring another costly fine-tuning. To address this inefficiency, recent work uses task vectors, defined as the parameter difference between a fine-tuned model and its base model, to transfer expertise across models. While existing methods bridge disparate models by matching activations or gradients, a significant performance gap remains relative to direct fine-tuning, suggesting that these partial correspondences are insufficient. In this work, instead of viewing a task vector merely as a parameter offset, we revisit the formation of task vectors and show that they can be derived as accumulated bilinear interactions between input-side activations and output-side gradients. Motivated by this observation, we formulate task-vector transfer as a dual-space alignment problem and propose BiCo, a training-free framework for transferring task vectors through Bilinear Coordinate alignment. BiCo estimates orthogonal Procrustes mappings in both spaces using a single forward-backward pass on a small calibration set, without any parameter update. Across extensive computer vision and natural language processing benchmarks, BiCo consistently outperforms existing transfer methods across models that differ in width, depth, and pre-training configuration.

AI.tldr: BiCo is a training-free framework that transfers task vectors across different pre-trained models by aligning bilinear interactions between activations and gradients, outperforming existing transfer methods without requiring parameter updates.
AI.motivation: When a new version of a pre-trained model becomes available, fine-tuning expertise cannot be directly reused as it is tied to the original model's parameters, requiring costly re-fine-tuning.
AI.method: BiCo formulates task-vector transfer as a dual-space alignment problem, estimating orthogonal Procrustes mappings in both input-activation and output-gradient spaces using a single forward-backward pass on a small calibration set, without any parameter update.
AI.result: BiCo consistently outperforms existing transfer methods across models that differ in width, depth, and pre-training configuration in extensive computer vision and natural language processing benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Task vectors can be derived as accumulated bilinear interactions between input-side activations and output-side gradients, and aligning both spaces enables effective training-free transfer across disparate models.

title: Prompt Codebooks: Discrete Compositional Optimization for Language Model Instruction Refinement
summary: Automatic prompt optimization (APO) has driven significant gains in LLM-based agentic workflows. However, existing methods treat each task's prompt as a monolithic, instance-blind string optimized through global edits, producing brittle updates and preventing the reuse of learned sub-behaviors. We propose Prompt Codebooks (PCO), a novel compositional prompt optimization framework that recasts APO as discrete learning over a finite vocabulary of natural-language instincts - atomic, reusable instruction units. PCO organizes prompt-construction knowledge in a discrete codebook and routes each input to a small subset of entries via an LLM-based encoder; a generator composes them into a prompt for the frozen target model; a critic emits a structured verdict that decomposes by attribution into per-variable textual gradients, jointly training the encoder, generator, and codebook under a language-valued min-max objective. The resulting routing is per-instance: different inputs in the same task receive different instinct compositions, a regime structurally inexpressible under instance-blind methods. Across six benchmarks on Qwen3-8B and LLaMA-3.1-8B, PCO improves over zero-shot by up to +30.36 points, surpasses the strongest prior baseline (GEPA) by +3.34 on HotpotQA and +1.11 in aggregate, and reduces deployed prompt length by up to 14.1x versus MIPROv2 and 3.0x versus GEPA using only K=16 instincts.

AI.tldr: Prompt Codebooks (PCO) is a compositional prompt optimization framework that learns a discrete vocabulary of reusable natural-language "instincts" and routes per-instance prompts, outperforming existing methods on six benchmarks while reducing prompt length by up to 14.1x.
AI.motivation: Existing APO methods treat prompts as monolithic, instance-blind strings with global edits, producing brittle updates and preventing reuse of learned sub-behaviors across inputs.
AI.method: PCO uses a discrete codebook of atomic instruction units ("instincts"), an LLM-based encoder for per-input routing, a generator to compose prompts, and a critic providing structured verdicts with per-variable textual gradients, all trained under a language-valued min-max objective.
AI.result: On Qwen3-8B and LLaMA-3.1-8B across six benchmarks, PCO improves up to +30.36 over zero-shot, surpasses GEPA by +3.34 on HotpotQA and +1.11 in aggregate, and reduces prompt length by 14.1x vs MIPROv2 and 3.0x vs GEPA with only K=16 instincts.
AI.conclusion: PCO demonstrates that compositional, per-instance prompt routing with a discrete codebook significantly improves both performance and efficiency over monolithic prompt optimization approaches.

title: Gamma-World: Generative Multi-Agent World Modeling Beyond Two Players
summary: World models for interactive video generation have largely focused on single-agent settings, where future observations are generated from a single control signal. However, many generated environments require multi-agent interaction: multiple players, robots, or embodied agents act simultaneously within a shared space. Scaling world models to such settings requires a principled multi-agent design: agents should remain independently controllable, permutation-symmetric, and support efficient inference while maintaining consistency across time and perspectives. In this paper, we present our generative multi-agent world model for interactive simulation. It introduces Simplex Rotary Agent Encoding, a parameter-free extension of 3D RoPE that represents agents as vertices of a regular simplex in rotary angle space. This gives each agent a distinct phase while making all agents permutation-equivalent, enabling scalable agent identity without learned per-slot identities or a fixed agent ordering. To avoid dense all-to-all attention across agents, we further propose Sparse Hub Attention, where learnable hub tokens mediate token interaction across agents, reducing cross-agent attention cost from quadratic to linear in the number of agents. For real-time rollout, we distill a full-context diffusion teacher into a causal student that generates temporal blocks sequentially with KV caching, enabling action-responsive generation at 24 FPS. Experiments in multiplayer virtual environments show that our model improves video fidelity, action controllability, and inter-agent consistency over slot-based and dense-attention baselines, while generalizing from two to four players without additional training.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a generative multi-agent world model for interactive video simulation that uses Simplex Rotary Agent Encoding for permutation-equivalent agent identity and Sparse Hub Attention to reduce cross-agent computation from quadratic to linear, enabling real-time 24 FPS generation.
AI.motivation: Existing world models focus on single-agent settings, but many environments require multi-agent interaction. Scaling to multi-agent settings needs principled design where agents are independently controllable, permutation-symmetric, and efficient.
AI.method: (1) Simplex Rotary Agent Encoding - parameter-free extension of 3D RoPE representing agents as vertices of a regular simplex for permutation-equivalent identity; (2) Sparse Hub Attention - learnable hub tokens mediate cross-agent interactions, reducing cost from O(n²) to O(n); (3) Distillation from full-context diffusion teacher to causal student with KV caching for 24 FPS real-time rollout.
AI.result: The model improves video fidelity, action controllability, and inter-agent consistency over slot-based and dense-attention baselines, and generalizes from two to four players without additional training.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-agent world model effectively scales interactive video generation to multiple agents with principled permutation symmetry and linear complexity, achieving real-time performance and strong generalization.

title: Mitigating Adaptive Attacks against Reasoning Models with Activation Consistency Training
summary: As LLMs gain stronger reasoning capabilities, their extended chain-of-thought introduces new degrees of complexity for defending against adversarial jailbreaks and prompt injection. We study consistency training, a family of fine-tuning objectives that enforce identical behavior on clean prompts and adversarial rewrites, and evaluate its two main variants, output-level (BCT) and activation-level (ACT), across five reasoning models. We formulate both methods as a prompt injection defense and find ACT to be competitive with other training-based defenses while requiring only self-supervised pairs of clean and wrapped prompts. Our experiments also generalize both techniques within the jailbreak setting, demonstrating that ACT remains more robust to adaptive attacks. We also provide mechanistic evidence that ACT's defense against jailbreaks is encoded as a roughly linear shift in activation space at the assistant-turn boundary. After ACT training, we can recover a single steering direction that controls refusal on reasoning models with minimal effect on benign inputs. We find that ACT remains robust even when the model's chain-of-thought is replaced with a compliant trace from the undefended base model, pivoting to refuse prefilled jailbreaks. Together, these results suggest that supervising internal representations is a surprisingly effective and interpretable approach to various forms of safety training in reasoning models.

AI.tldr: This paper studies consistency training (output-level BCT and activation-level ACT) as a defense against adversarial jailbreaks and prompt injection in reasoning LLMs. ACT proves competitive with training-based defenses, robust to adaptive attacks, and works via a linear activation shift at the assistant-turn boundary. A single steering direction can control refusal, and ACT remains robust even when chain-of-thought is replaced with compliant traces.
AI.motivation: LLMs with stronger reasoning capabilities and extended chain-of-thought introduce new complexity for defending against adversarial jailbreaks and prompt injection. The paper aims to find effective defenses for these emerging threats in reasoning models.
AI.method: The authors study consistency training (two variants: output-level BCT and activation-level ACT), formulate both as prompt injection defense, and evaluate across five reasoning models. They provide mechanistic analysis via activation space shifts and test robustness against adaptive attacks and prefilled jailbreaks.
AI.result: ACT is competitive with other training-based defenses, more robust to adaptive attacks, and works via a roughly linear shift in activation space at the assistant-turn boundary. A single steering direction controls refusal with minimal effect on benign inputs. ACT remains robust even when chain-of-thought is replaced with compliant traces.
AI.conclusion: Supervising internal representations (activation-level consistency training) is a surprisingly effective and interpretable approach to various forms of safety training in reasoning models.

title: Risk-Controlled Lean-as-Judge for Natural-Language Mathematical Reasoning
summary: Lean is increasingly used to judge natural-language mathematical answers, but its signal is partial: many answers never formalize, and a failed proof may reflect an ill-typed statement or a missing library fact, not a wrong answer. On MATH-500 we show this signal is (i) sharply coverage-dependent, that is the proof-winning answer is correct 96% of the time at high proved coverage but 20% at low, and (ii) sparse and often unfaithful: a 7B autoformalizer proves a class for only 28% of problems, and a manual audit finds only approximately 43% of those proofs faithful. We propose COVCAL, a selector over Lean-trace diagnostics that certifies a finite-sample selective-risk bound on accepted answers or abstains, under two regimes (a conservative Bonferroni bound and a tighter dev-then-cal rule). Feasibility depends on autoformalization coverage: with the 7B formalizer the signal is too sparse and Bonferroni abstains on all 20 bootstrap partitions, whereas a prover-specialized formalizer reaches 79% coverage and flips it to feasible on 17 of 20, accepting approximately 48% of problems at 0.98 accepted accuracy. Since self-consistency alone is already 91% accurate, our contribution is a precise account of when, and with which formalizer, a partial formal signal can be trusted under risk control.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the limitations of using Lean to judge natural-language math answers, showing the signal is coverage-dependent, sparse, and often unfaithful. It proposes COVCAL, a risk-controlled selector that certifies accuracy bounds on accepted answers, demonstrating feasibility depends heavily on autoformalization coverage quality.
AI.motivation: Lean's proof signal for judging natural-language math answers is partial and unreliable—many answers never formalize, and failed proofs may reflect typing issues or missing library facts rather than wrong answers. The paper aims to characterize this signal's quality and propose a principled method to achieve risk-controlled acceptance.
AI.method: The authors (i) empirically analyze Lean signal on MATH-500 across coverage levels, (ii) propose COVCAL, a selector using Lean-trace diagnostics to certify finite-sample selective-risk bounds under two regimes (Bonferroni bound and dev-then-cal rule), and (iii) compare two autoformalizers: a 7B general model and a prover-specialized formalizer.
AI.result: Proof-winning answer is 96% accurate at high proved coverage but only 20% at low coverage. A 7B formalizer proves only 28% of problems with ~43% faithful proofs. COVCAL with Bonferroni abstains on all 20 bootstrap partitions for the 7B formalizer, but with a prover-specialized formalizer (79% coverage) it accepts ~48% of problems at 0.98 accuracy on 17/20 partitions.
AI.conclusion: The feasibility of risk-controlled Lean-based evaluation hinges critically on autoformalization coverage. Self-consistency alone achieves 91% accuracy, but COVCAL provides precise, certified guarantees on when partial formal signals can be trusted, contingent on formalizer quality.

title: From Pixels to Words -- Towards Native One-Vision Models at Scale
summary: Current vision-language models (VLMs) typically stitch together separate image encoders and language decoders via multi-stage alignment, a modular framework that inevitably fragments pixel-level signals across frames and scatters early pixel-word interactions. In parallel, native VLMs, despite impressive performance on single images, remain largely unexplored in multi-image, video understanding, and spatial intelligence. Hence, we introduce NEO-ov, a native foundation model that learns cross-frame and pixel-word correspondence end-to-end, without any external encoders, auxiliary adapters, or post-hoc fusion. By eliminating module boundaries entirely, NEO-ov enables fine-grained and unified spatiotemporal modeling to emerge natively inside the model. Notably, NEO-ov largely narrows the gap to modular counterparts while excelling at fine-grained visual perception, validating that native "one-vision" architectures are not only feasible but competitive at scale. Beyond empirical performance, we unveil systematic architectural analyses and detailed training recipes to facilitate subsequent native multimodal modeling. Our code and models are publicly available at: https://github.com/EvolvingLMMs-Lab/NEO.

AI.tldr: NEO-ov is a native vision-language foundation model that eliminates modular boundaries (separate encoders/decoders/adapters) to learn cross-frame and pixel-word correspondence end-to-end, achieving competitive performance on fine-grained visual perception tasks while narrowing the gap with modular VLMs.
AI.motivation: Current VLMs use modular architectures (separate image encoders + language decoders) that fragment pixel signals across frames and limit early pixel-word interactions. Native VLMs for multi-image/video tasks are under-explored.
AI.method: NEO-ov is a native foundation model trained end-to-end without external encoders, auxiliary adapters, or post-hoc fusion, enabling unified spatiotemporal modeling through direct cross-frame and pixel-word correspondence learning.
AI.result: NEO-ov narrows the performance gap with modular VLMs while excelling at fine-grained visual perception tasks, demonstrating that native "one-vision" architectures are feasible and competitive at scale.
AI.conclusion: Native multimodal architectures (without modular separation) are not only viable but competitive; the paper provides systematic architectural analyses and training recipes to advance future native multimodal modeling.

title: Fitting Unknown Number of Hyperplanes with Manifold Optimization
summary: Fitting an unknown number of hyperplanes to data is a fundamental yet challenging problem in machine learning, characterized by its non-convexity, non-differentiability, and unknown model order. Existing approaches often struggle with local optima or lack geometric consistency. To address these limitations, we propose a novel framework based on Manifold Optimization. We reformulate the problem as an unsupervised learning task on the unit sphere manifold $\mathcal{S}^{\textbf{dim}-1}$. This formulation effectively handles the non-convex constraints and linearizes the distance measurement, rendering the gradient descent tractable. We propose a Two-Stage Manifold Optimization algorithm. In Phase I, we employ a Riemannian Expectation-Maximization process with a heavy-tailed kernel to robustly estimate posterior probabilities, effectively resolving the ambiguities of point distribution between intersecting hyperplanes. In Phase II, upon convergence of the soft estimates, the probabilistic weights degenerate into hard matching, generating a precise local optimum that strictly satisfies the geometric definition. Furthermore, we introduce a projected density estimation strategy for initialization to facilitate global convergence by significantly reducing the feature description space and search complexity. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both geometric accuracy and robustness.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a Two-Stage Manifold Optimization framework for fitting an unknown number of hyperplanes to data, reformulating the problem on a unit sphere manifold to handle non-convexity, using Riemannian EM with a heavy-tailed kernel in Phase I and hard matching in Phase II, with a projected density estimation strategy for initialization.
AI.motivation: Fitting an unknown number of hyperplanes is a fundamental yet challenging problem due to non-convexity, non-differentiability, and unknown model order. Existing approaches suffer from local optima or lack geometric consistency.
AI.method: 1) Reformulates the problem as unsupervised learning on the unit sphere manifold S^{dim-1}; 2) Phase I: Riemannian Expectation-Maximization with a heavy-tailed kernel for robust posterior estimation; 3) Phase II: Probabilistic weights degenerate into hard matching for precise local optima; 4) Projected density estimation strategy for initialization to reduce search complexity.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show the proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both geometric accuracy and robustness.
AI.conclusion: The manifold optimization framework effectively addresses the limitations of existing hyperplane fitting approaches, achieving superior geometric consistency and robustness.

title: CyberJurors: A Multi-Agent Simulation Task for E-Commerce Disputes Verdict
summary: E-commerce platforms have begun recruiting crowdsourced jurors to adjudicate massive volumes of transaction disputes. Unlike formal legal judgment, E-commerce dispute verdicts require grounding pivotal clues from redundant, multi-round, multimodal evidence and making decisions under flexible platform-specific conventions. These characteristics render existing methods insufficient for this scenario. To bridge this gap, we introduce a pioneering task, E-commerce Dispute Verdicts (EDV), and present VerdictBench, a multimodal benchmark comprising 6,000 real-world cases designed to reflect crowdsourced jury decisions. Building upon this, we propose CyberJurors, a multi-agent framework to clarify the dispute logic and regulate the verdict process. At the individual level, Individual Verdict Chain-of-Thought decomposes the EDV task into four structured reasoning stages, enabling fine-grained clue perception and clarifying causal logic between pivotal clues and the dispute focus. At the collective level, Jury Consensus Verdict simulates multi-round discussion and voting among jurors, while incorporating verdict precedents to mitigate cognitive biases toward either disputant. Experiments on VerdictBench show that CyberJurors outperforms state-of-the-art LLMs, MLLMs, and court simulators, while achieving stronger alignment with real-world jury voting patterns. Code and dataset are available at https://github.com/YanhuiS/CyberJurors and https://huggingface.co/datasets/piggi/VerdictBench.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EDV (E-commerce Dispute Verdicts), a new task for AI adjudication of e-commerce disputes, along with VerdictBench (6,000 real-world cases) and CyberJurors, a multi-agent framework that outperforms existing LLMs and court simulators by decomposing verdict reasoning into structured stages and simulating jury discussions.
AI.motivation: E-commerce platforms need to handle massive volumes of transaction disputes via crowdsourced jurors, but existing methods fail because e-commerce verdicts require grounding clues from redundant, multi-round, multimodal evidence and making decisions under flexible platform-specific conventions.
AI.method: CyberJurors is a multi-agent framework with two levels: (1) Individual level - Individual Verdict Chain-of-Thought decomposes EDV into four structured reasoning stages for fine-grained clue perception; (2) Collective level - Jury Consensus Verdict simulates multi-round discussion and voting among jurors, incorporating verdict precedents to reduce cognitive biases.
AI.result: CyberJurors outperforms state-of-the-art LLMs, MLLMs, and court simulators on VerdictBench, achieving stronger alignment with real-world jury voting patterns.
AI.conclusion: The proposed task (EDV), benchmark (VerdictBench), and framework (CyberJurors) together address the gap in AI-assisted e-commerce dispute resolution, demonstrating that structured multi-agent reasoning aligns better with crowdsourced jury decisions than existing approaches.

title: Universal Time Series Generation with Neural Controlled Differential Equations
summary: Recent work on the sequence universality of State Space Models (SSMs) has introduced efficient, maximally expressive continuous-time approaches for time-series modelling. While these works focus on discriminative settings, we extend this perspective to generative time-series modelling by proving that maximally expressive Structured Linear Controlled Differential Equations (SLiCEs) are universal time-series generators, in the sense that they can approximate the induced path laws of continuous causal pushforwards on compact latent sets in $W_\infty$. Building on these theoretical results, we propose Generative SLiCEs (G-SLiCEs), a maximally expressive continuous-time model for flow matching on path-space. Empirically, we show that expressivity improves performance in probabilistic forecasting and downstream tasks, while retaining the advantages of continuous-time models such as generalising to arbitrary observation grids. This is particularly beneficial for irregular grids, where fixed-grid models often struggle.

AI.tldr: The paper extends state space models to generative time-series modeling by proving Structured Linear Controlled Differential Equations (SLiCEs) are universal generators, and proposes G-SLiCEs for flow matching on path-space, showing improved performance on probabilistic forecasting.
AI.motivation: To extend maximally expressive continuous-time SSM approaches from discriminative to generative time-series modeling, addressing the lack of theoretical guarantees for universal generation in this domain.
AI.method: Proves universality of SLiCEs as time-series generators, then proposes Generative SLiCEs (G-SLiCEs) for flow matching on path-space.
AI.result: Expressivity improves performance in probabilistic forecasting and downstream tasks, with advantages on irregular grids where fixed-grid models struggle.
AI.conclusion: Maximally expressive continuous-time models are beneficial for generative time-series modeling, especially on irregular observation grids.

title: From paper to benchmark: agentic, framework-based reproduction of under-specified methods in machine health intelligence
summary: Industrial Prognostics and Health Management (PHM) provides a representative case study for a broader challenge in applied machine learning: translating published papers into executable, benchmark-ready implementations. Reproducing under-specified methods in PHM is particularly difficult due to restricted access to industrial datasets, incomplete reporting of preprocessing and evaluation protocols, and implicit design choices (e.g., windowing, target construction, data splits) that critically affect performance. Existing paper-to-code systems generate implementations for individual papers, but these artifacts are often not directly comparable due to inconsistencies in assumptions and evaluation settings. We introduce \emph{agentic, framework-based PHM paper reproduction}, where an agent translates a paper into a shared PHM benchmark framework via a \emph{slot-binding interface}. This interface maps equations and protocol descriptions into structured components (task definitions, dataset adapters, windowing, targets, models, and evaluators), while explicitly recording unresolved assumptions. The resulting implementations are validated against standardized task contracts and evaluation hooks, enabling consistent and comparable benchmarking. We evaluate this approach on 16 PHM papers, comparing framework-enhanced, skill-based and prompt-based agentic reproduction against a recent framework-free paper-reproduction agent. We assess reproduction success, model-based code evaluation, framework binding of paper assumptions, and cross-paper benchmark comparability under standardized protocols. Our results show that coupling agentic generation with a shared framework transforms paper reproduction from isolated code synthesis into executable, assumption-aware, and systematically comparable benchmark implementations.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an agentic, framework-based approach to reproduce PHM papers into a shared benchmark framework using a slot-binding interface, enabling consistent and comparable implementations across 16 PHM papers.
AI.motivation: Reproducing PHM papers is difficult due to restricted data access, incomplete reporting of preprocessing/evaluation protocols, and implicit design choices that critically affect performance, making existing paper-to-code artifacts not directly comparable.
AI.method: The approach uses an agentic system with a slot-binding interface that maps equations and protocol descriptions into structured components (task definitions, dataset adapters, windowing, targets, models, evaluators) within a shared PHM benchmark framework, explicitly recording unresolved assumptions.
AI.result: Evaluated on 16 PHM papers, the framework-enhanced agentic reproduction outperformed framework-free and prompt-based approaches in reproduction success, model-based code evaluation, and cross-paper benchmark comparability.
AI.conclusion: Coupling agentic generation with a shared framework transforms paper reproduction from isolated code synthesis into executable, assumption-aware, and systematically comparable benchmark implementations.

title: Learning Theory of the SVRG: Generalization and Convergence Analysis
summary: Variance reduction (VR) methods employ stochastic gradients with decreasing variance, and they have been widely applied to solve large-scale optimization problems in machine learning because of their efficiency. Existing theoretical studies of VR methods are mainly focused on the convergence analysis, leaving the generalization behavior largely unexplored. In this paper, we bridge this gap by developing the first non-vacuous generalization analysis of the representative VR method: Stochastic Variance Reduced Gradient (SVRG), through the lens of algorithmic stability. In particular, we establish sharp stability bounds of the SVRG in both convex and strongly convex settings by exploiting its algorithmic structure. The obtained bounds are data-dependent, because the training errors are incorporated along the trajectory. Our analysis clarifies the interplay between optimization and generalization, leading to optimal excess population risk bounds in both settings. Our approach differs substantially from existing analyses of stochastic algorithms in the sense that we decompose the SVRG update as an SGD-like step plus a zero-mean correction term and then introduce novel Lyapunov functions to absorb the additional gradient terms induced by the reference points. Our analytical framework can be generalized to other VR methods, and we demonstrate the generalization by the well-known Stochastic Average Gradient Accelerated (SAGA) method.

AI.tldr: This paper develops the first non-vacuous generalization analysis of SVRG via algorithmic stability, establishing sharp data-dependent stability bounds in convex and strongly convex settings, and introduces a novel Lyapunov-based analytical framework that extends to other VR methods like SAGA.
AI.motivation: Existing theoretical studies of VR methods focus on convergence analysis, leaving generalization behavior largely unexplored. This paper bridges this gap by developing generalization analysis of SVRG.
AI.method: The authors decompose SVRG update as an SGD-like step plus a zero-mean correction term, and introduce novel Lyapunov functions to absorb additional gradient terms induced by reference points. The analysis uses algorithmic stability to derive sharp stability bounds.
AI.result: Established sharp, data-dependent stability bounds for SVRG in both convex and strongly convex settings, leading to optimal excess population risk bounds. The framework generalizes to other VR methods like SAGA.
AI.conclusion: The analysis clarifies the interplay between optimization and generalization, providing the first non-vacuous generalization bounds for VR methods through algorithmic stability.

title: Mechanistically Interpreting the Role of Sample Difficulty in RLVR for LLMs
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Reward (RLVR) is empirically shown to notably enhance the reasoning performance of large language models (LLMs), particularly in mathematics and programming. However, the mechanistic role of Sample Difficulty in RLVR remains poorly understood. In this paper, we investigate RLVR through the lens of difficulty-wise and one-sample analysis. We find that sample difficulty has a non-monotonic effect on RLVR: easy and medium-difficulty problems yield the strongest and most stable reasoning improvements, whereas overly hard problems often provide weak learning signals, induce degenerate behaviors such as answer repetition or skipping necessary computation, and can ultimately degrade the model's pre-existing capabilities. Beyond the obverse of response, we further analyze the model's internal feature dynamics using Temporal Sparse Autoencoders (T-SAE). Easy problems mainly reinforce direct-answer and basic-computation features while suppressing deliberative-reasoning features; hard problems activate reasoning-related features but become useful only when successful trajectories are sampled; medium-difficulty problems provide a more balanced signal, strengthening both computation and multi-step reasoning features. Motivated by these findings, we propose difficulty-adaptive strategies for hard-sample utilization, using backward-reasoning reformulation and T-SAE-guided training signals to improve reward density and credit assignment during RLVR. Overall, our results identify sample difficulty as a key factor governing both the optimization dynamics and representation evolution of RLVR.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how sample difficulty affects Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Reward (RLVR) for LLMs, finding that easy and medium-difficulty problems yield the best reasoning improvements, while overly hard problems can degrade performance. The authors also propose difficulty-adaptive strategies to better utilize hard samples.
AI.motivation: The mechanistic role of Sample Difficulty in RLVR remains poorly understood, despite RLVR's empirical success in enhancing LLM reasoning performance.
AI.method: The authors conduct difficulty-wise and one-sample analysis of RLVR, examine internal feature dynamics using Temporal Sparse Autoencoders (T-SAE), and propose difficulty-adaptive strategies including backward-reasoning reformulation and T-SAE-guided training signals.
AI.result: Sample difficulty has a non-monotonic effect: easy/medium problems yield strongest improvements, while hard problems can induce degenerate behaviors (answer repetition, skipping computation) and degrade pre-existing capabilities. T-SAE analysis reveals distinct feature dynamics across difficulty levels.
AI.conclusion: Sample difficulty is a key factor governing optimization dynamics and representation evolution in RLVR, and difficulty-adaptive strategies can improve reward density and credit assignment for hard samples.

title: Stochastic Gradient Descent with Momentum is Algorithmically Stable
summary: Stochastic gradient descent with momentum (SGDM) is one of the most widely used optimization algorithms in machine learning. While optimization properties of SGDM have been extensively studied in the literature, it remains insufficiently understood whether and when SGDM can generalize well to unseen data. In particular, it has been conjectured that while momentum accelerates training, it may degrade generalization. In this paper, we close this gap by developing a comprehensive generalization analysis of SGDM through the lens of algorithmic stability. More specifically, we introduce a generalized SGDM framework that encompasses both Polyak's and Nesterov's momentum schemes, and establish tight on-average model stability bounds for smooth and convex problems. Notably, the obtained bounds exploit small optimization error bounds along the trajectory, apply to any momentum parameter in the interval $[0, 1)$, and do not require the commonly assumed Lipschitzness of loss functions. We further derive optimization error bounds for the generalized SGDM, and combine them with our generalization analyses to obtain optimal excess population risk bounds for SGDM with both Polyak's and Nesterov's momentum.

AI.tldr: This paper provides the first comprehensive generalization analysis of Stochastic Gradient Descent with Momentum (SGDM) using algorithmic stability, proving tight bounds for both Polyak's and Nesterov's momentum schemes on smooth convex problems without requiring Lipschitz loss functions.
AI.motivation: While SGDM is widely used, it was not well understood whether momentum hurts generalization (as previously conjectured). The authors aim to close this theoretical gap by analyzing the generalization performance of momentum-based optimization.
AI.method: The authors develop a generalized SGDM framework covering both Polyak's and Nesterov's momentum, derive tight on-average model stability bounds for smooth convex problems, and combine these with optimization error bounds to obtain optimal excess population risk bounds.
AI.result: Tight stability bounds are obtained that exploit small optimization errors along the trajectory, apply to any momentum parameter in [0,1), and do not require Lipschitz loss functions. Optimal excess population risk bounds are derived for both momentum variants.
AI.conclusion: Contrary to prior conjectures, SGDM can generalize well when properly analyzed through algorithmic stability, with provably optimal bounds achievable for both momentum schemes.

title: You Live More Than Once: Towards Hierarchical Skill Meta-Evolving
summary: Test-time skill evolving is regarded as a new paradigm for enhancing deployed agentic systems. Existing works mainly focus on hard-coded skill evolving strategies or parametric learning that rely on expensive parameter updates in the underlying LLMs. In this paper, we demonstrate that test-time refinement of the skill evolving framework itself is necessary for continuous improvement of the agent systems in different downstream scenarios, and lightweight algorithmic adaptation is feasible. Specifically, we propose HiSME, a lightweight hierarchical skill meta-evolving solution that jointly optimizes skills and the skill evolving strategy by learning meta-skills from agents' task execution traces. Experiments on diverse agentic benchmarks show that meta-evolving can produce a higher-quality skill library than pure skill evolving and can derive diverse meta-skills for different scenarios, thereby facilitating future continual experience learning. Our code is temporarily public at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/HiSME-BD45.

AI.tldr: HiSME is a lightweight hierarchical skill meta-evolving framework that jointly optimizes skills and evolving strategies by learning meta-skills from agent execution traces, enabling continuous improvement without expensive LLM parameter updates.
AI.motivation: Existing test-time skill evolving methods rely on hard-coded strategies or costly parameter updates; there is a need for lightweight, adaptable skill evolution that continuously improves across different downstream scenarios.
AI.method: Proposes HiSME, a hierarchical meta-evolving solution that learns meta-skills from agents' task execution traces to jointly optimize both the skill library and the skill evolving strategy in a lightweight manner.
AI.result: Experiments on diverse agentic benchmarks show HiSME produces higher-quality skill libraries than pure skill evolving and derives diverse meta-skills for different scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Meta-evolving facilitates future continual experience learning and is a feasible lightweight alternative to parametric updates for improving deployed agentic systems.

title: Stabilizing distribution-free probabilistic forecasts
summary: Multi-step-ahead forecasts are often updated as new observations become available, since shorter forecast horizons typically improve forecast quality. However, such improvements come at the cost of forecast instability, i.e., variability in forecasts for the same target period. This instability can trigger costly changes to plans formulated based on the forecasts and may erode trust in the forecasting system. In this work, we integrate forecast stability alongside forecast quality into the training of distribution-free probabilistic time-series forecasting models, allowing us to control this trade-off. We propose a method for generating stabilized forecasted conditional quantile functions using regression splines parameterized by a neural network. This approach enables joint optimization of quality and stability, as it allows us to directly penalize dissimilarities arising from forecast updates. Furthermore, it allows assigning varying importance to stabilizing different parts of the forecast distributions (e.g., central parts vs. tails) to focus on the parts most relevant for the intended downstream use (e.g., the upper tail for inventory management). We empirically evaluate the proposed method on two datasets with different statistical properties and show that it can effectively reduce forecast instability without a substantial loss in forecast quality, and that it can target stabilization effort toward specific parts of the forecast distributions.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a neural network-based method that integrates forecast stability into the training of distribution-free probabilistic time-series forecasting models, enabling control over the trade-off between forecast quality and stability through regression splines parameterized by neural networks.
AI.motivation: Multi-step-ahead forecasts are often updated with new observations, improving quality but causing forecast instability (variability for the same target period). This instability can trigger costly plan changes and erode trust in forecasting systems.
AI.method: Proposes generating stabilized forecasted conditional quantile functions using regression splines parameterized by a neural network, enabling joint optimization of quality and stability by directly penalizing dissimilarities from forecast updates, with capability to assign varying importance to different parts of forecast distributions.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on two datasets with different statistical properties shows the method effectively reduces forecast instability without substantial loss in forecast quality, and can target stabilization toward specific parts of forecast distributions.
AI.conclusion: Integrating forecast stability alongside quality into training is feasible and effective; the proposed approach offers a practical way to control the quality-stability trade-off in probabilistic forecasting.

title: HRBench: Benchmarking and Understanding Thinking-Mode Switch Strategies in Hybrid-Reasoning LLMs
summary: Hybrid-reasoning large language models (LLMs) expose explicit controls over reasoning effort, allowing users or systems to trade off answer quality against inference cost. However, existing methods for adaptive thinking-mode selection are typically evaluated under different models, datasets, and implementation assumptions, making it difficult to compare their practical behavior. We introduce HRBench, a unified evaluation framework for studying thinking-mode switching in hybrid-reasoning LLMs. HRBench organizes the design space along two axes: three switching strategy families, prompt-based selection, external routing, and speculative execution, and four training regimes, training-free, SFT, offline and online RL, yielding 12 controlled evaluation settings. We evaluate these settings across 6 LLMs, from Qwen3.5-2B to Kimi-K2.5-1.1T, and 5 reasoning benchmarks covering mathematics, science, and code, while reimplementing 12+ representative prior methods within the same pipeline. Our analysis characterizes how different switching strategies occupy distinct effectiveness-efficiency trade-off regions: prompt-based methods often provide favorable token-accuracy trade-offs, routing methods offer more stable cost reduction, and speculative methods tend to improve accuracy at higher token cost. We further find that training affects strategies differently, and that the preferred strategy varies with model scale and task domain. HRBench provides reference implementations and a unified evaluation platform to support more controlled research on efficient reasoning in hybrid-reasoning LLMs. Our data, code and repository are available at https://github.com/usail-hkust/HRBench.

AI.tldr: HRBench is a unified evaluation framework for studying thinking-mode switching in hybrid-reasoning LLMs, spanning 3 switching strategies × 4 training regimes = 12 settings, evaluated across 6 LLMs (2B to 1.1T) and 5 benchmarks. Key finding: different strategies occupy distinct effectiveness-efficiency trade-off regions.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for adaptive thinking-mode selection are evaluated under different models, datasets, and implementation assumptions, making it difficult to compare their practical behavior. A unified framework is needed.
AI.method: HRBench organizes the design space along two axes: (1) three switching strategy families (prompt-based selection, external routing, speculative execution), and (2) four training regimes (training-free, SFT, offline RL, online RL), yielding 12 controlled evaluation settings. They reimplement 12+ prior methods within the same pipeline and evaluate across 6 LLMs and 5 reasoning benchmarks.
AI.result: Prompt-based methods offer favorable token-accuracy trade-offs; routing methods provide more stable cost reduction; speculative methods improve accuracy at higher token cost. Training affects strategies differently, and the preferred strategy varies with model scale and task domain.
AI.conclusion: HRBench provides reference implementations and a unified evaluation platform to support more controlled research on efficient reasoning in hybrid-reasoning LLMs.

title: Semi-Supervised Hypothesis Testing by Betting on Predictions
summary: We introduce a testing-by-betting framework that leverages predictions on unlabeled data to enhance the power of sequential hypothesis testing. Given limited samples from the joint distribution of $(X,Y)$, and additional unlabeled samples from the marginal of $X$, we ask how unlabeled data can be used to hypothesize about the distribution of $Y$, and the conditional distribution of $Y\mid X$. We introduce an e-statistic and use it to construct a sequential test. Under standard distributional assumptions -- label shift or concept shift -- we establish that the test is anytime valid. Furthermore, we show that for binary data, the e-statistic has non-trivial power. Crucially, our approach retains these properties even when the underlying predictions are inaccurate. Through simulations and applications to large language models evaluation, we demonstrate power gains over baseline approaches, including prediction-powered inference. These gains persist even with relatively limited unlabeled data and when predictions have low accuracy due to weak correlation between $X$ and $Y$.

AI.tldr: Introduces a testing-by-betting framework using unlabeled data predictions to improve sequential hypothesis testing power, with anytime-valid e-statistics under label/concept shift, showing gains over baselines even with poor predictions.
AI.motivation: To leverage abundant unlabeled data (X only) alongside limited labeled (X,Y) samples to enhance the statistical power of sequential hypothesis testing about Y's distribution.
AI.method: Proposes a testing-by-betting framework with an e-statistic that uses predictions on unlabeled data; establishes anytime validity under label shift or concept shift assumptions.
AI.result: Binary data analysis shows non-trivial power; simulations and LLM evaluation demonstrate gains over baseline methods (e.g., prediction-powered inference) even with weak X-Y correlation or limited unlabeled data.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively uses unlabeled data to boost testing power while maintaining validity despite inaccurate predictions, with practical applications in sequential testing settings.

title: Measuring Progress Toward AGI: A Cognitive Framework
summary: Despite widespread discussion of AGI, there is no clear framework for measuring progress toward it. This ambiguity fuels subjective claims, makes it difficult to track progress, and risks hindering responsible governance. As a starting point to address this gap, we present a framework for understanding system capabilities in relation to human cognitive abilities. Drawing from decades of research in psychology, neuroscience, and cognitive science, we introduce a Cognitive Taxonomy that deconstructs general intelligence into 10 key cognitive faculties. We then propose a rigorous evaluation protocol in which a system's performance is measured across a suite of targeted, held-out cognitive tasks, generating a 'cognitive profile' that can be used to understand a system's strengths and weaknesses. We hope this framework will provide a practical roadmap and an initial step toward more rigorous, empirical evaluation of AGI.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a framework for measuring AGI progress by decomposing general intelligence into 10 cognitive faculties drawn from psychology/neuroscience/cognitive science, and evaluating systems via a cognitive profile across targeted tasks.
AI.motivation: There is no clear framework for measuring progress toward AGI, leading to subjective claims, difficulty tracking progress, and challenges for responsible governance.
AI.method: The authors introduce a Cognitive Taxonomy deconstructing general intelligence into 10 key cognitive faculties based on decades of research, then propose a rigorous evaluation protocol using a suite of targeted, held-out cognitive tasks to generate a system's cognitive profile.
AI.result: The framework produces a 'cognitive profile' mapping a system's strengths and weaknesses across the 10 cognitive faculties.
AI.conclusion: This framework provides a practical roadmap and initial step toward more rigorous, empirical evaluation of AGI, aiming to address the current ambiguity in measuring progress.

title: High Performance, Low Reliability: Uncertainty Benchmarking for Tabular Foundation Models
comment: 6 pages, 2 figures, 2 tables. Accepted at ESANN 2026 (European Symposium on Artificial Neural Networks, Computational Intelligence and Machine Learning), 22-24 April 2026, Bruges (Belgium)
summary: Recent Tabular Foundation Models (TFMs) have demonstrated state-of-the-art predictive performance, often surpassing Gradient-Boosted Decision Trees (GBDTs). However, the trustworthiness of these models, particularly their uncertainty quantification, has been largely overlooked. We investigate this gap through an extensive study comparing TFMs, GBDTs, and classical baselines on the 112 datasets of the TALENT benchmark. Our results reveal a performance-uncertainty trade-off: although TFMs achieve the highest predictive performance, measured by AUC, they exhibit lower conditional coverage under conformal prediction, measured by SSCS, compared to GBDTs. Complementary experiments on synthetic datasets further characterize the regimes in which this effect intensifies. We conclude that while TFMs advance predictive frontiers, achieving well-calibrated uncertainty remains a major open challenge for their reliable adoption. Code is available at: https://github.com/jose-melo/high-performance-low-reliability

AI.tldr: TFMs outperform GBDTs in predictive performance but have worse uncertainty calibration, revealing a performance-uncertainty trade-off.
AI.motivation: To investigate the overlooked trustworthiness (especially uncertainty quantification) of Tabular Foundation Models, given their recent superior predictive performance over GBDTs.
AI.method: Extensive study comparing TFMs, GBDTs, and classical baselines on 112 TALENT benchmark datasets, plus complementary synthetic experiments to characterize the performance-uncertainty trade-off.
AI.result: TFMs achieve higher AUC (predictive performance) but lower conditional coverage under conformal prediction (SSCS) compared to GBDTs, demonstrating a systematic performance-uncertainty trade-off.
AI.conclusion: Achieving well-calibrated uncertainty remains a major open challenge for reliable adoption of TFMs, despite their predictive advances.

title: Efficient Post-training of LLMs for Code Generation With Offline Reinforcement Learning
summary: Post-training using online reinforcement learning (RL) is an important training step for LLMs, including code-generating models. However, online RL for code generation involves LLM inference and verification of the generated output, which can take considerable time and resources. In this paper, we explore the application of offline RL to code-generating models by leveraging existing code datasets. Our experiments demonstrate that offline RL is an effective training strategy for improving LLM performance. We show that offline RL can be especially beneficial for small LLMs and challenging coding problems.

AI.tldr: Offline reinforcement learning can effectively improve code-generating LLMs, especially for small models and challenging problems, without the computational costs of online RL.
AI.motivation: Online RL for code generation is computationally expensive due to LLM inference and verification. The authors explore offline RL as a more efficient alternative by using existing code datasets.
AI.method: The authors apply offline reinforcement learning to code-generating LLMs by leveraging existing code datasets, without requiring online LLM inference or output verification during training.
AI.result: Offline RL is an effective training strategy for improving LLM code generation performance. It is especially beneficial for small LLMs and challenging coding problems.
AI.conclusion: Offline RL is a promising, cost-effective alternative to online RL for training code-generating LLMs, offering particular advantages in resource-constrained scenarios.

title: A Multi-dimensional Framework for Evaluating Generalization in EEG Foundation Models
summary: Evaluating foundation models under appropriate adaptation settings is essential for understanding the quality and transferability of the learned representations. Recent EEG foundation models have demonstrated promising transfer capabilities across tasks and datasets, motivating their growing use in neurotechnology and clinical applications. However, these models are typically evaluated under full fine-tuning on well-curated downstream datasets, a setting that does not reflect biomedical domain constraints such as limited labeled data, reduced sensor coverage, or parameter-efficient adaptation. In this work, we propose a multi-dimensional evaluation framework for assessing EEG models under realistic low-resource conditions. Empirical analysis of both supervised EEG models and recent EEG foundation models, including LaBraM, CSBrain, and CBraMod, across 6 different datasets is performed under the proposed multi-dimensional evaluation framework. We find that EEG foundation models consistently provide performance gains on long-context tasks such as sleep stage prediction and mental health state classification. In contrast, for short-window Brain Computer Interface style tasks, supervised models achieve comparable despite having substantially fewer parameters. Additional analyses demonstrate that current foundation models provide limited robustness to short-window tasks and channel constrained settings. Together, these findings motivate the use of multi-dimensional evaluation protocols that characterize model behavior under realistic use constraints.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a multi-dimensional evaluation framework for assessing EEG foundation models under realistic low-resource conditions, finding that foundation models excel on long-context tasks but show limited advantage over supervised models on short-window BCI tasks and under channel-constrained settings.
AI.motivation: Current EEG foundation models are typically evaluated under full fine-tuning on well-curated datasets, which does not reflect real-world biomedical constraints such as limited labeled data, reduced sensor coverage, or parameter-efficient adaptation. A more realistic evaluation framework is needed.
AI.method: The authors propose a multi-dimensional evaluation framework and empirically analyze supervised EEG models alongside foundation models (LaBraM, CSBrain, CBraMod) across 6 datasets under low-resource conditions.
AI.result: EEG foundation models outperform on long-context tasks (sleep stage prediction, mental health classification), but supervised models with fewer parameters achieve comparable results on short-window BCI tasks. Foundation models show limited robustness to short-window tasks and channel-constrained settings.
AI.conclusion: Multi-dimensional evaluation protocols that characterize model behavior under realistic use constraints are necessary for proper assessment of EEG foundation models.

title: DenoiseRL: Bootstrapping Reasoning Models to Recover from Noisy Prefixes
summary: Reinforcement learning has become a central paradigm for advancing reasoning in large language models, yet most existing methods still depend on stronger teacher models or heavily curated difficult datasets, limiting scalable capability improvement. In this paper, we introduce DenoiseRL, a reinforcement learning framework that substitutes external supervision with recovery-oriented optimization over failures from weak models. Instead of relying on stronger supervision or carefully engineered data, DenoiseRL learns directly from incorrect reasoning traces by converting them into opportunities for improvement, making training more scalable and less dependent on external resources. This yields a richer and more diverse learning signal, improving exploration efficiency from imperfect model behavior. As a result, DenoiseRL improves reasoning performance and overall training efficiency while reducing the need for expensive data curation or stronger teacher models. Empirically, DenoiseRL consistently outperforms strong on-policy RL baselines across competitive mathematical and general reasoning benchmarks and promotes stronger self-corrective behavior as training difficulty increases, highlighting an effective and scalable alternative pathway for improving reasoning in large language models.

AI.tldr: DenoiseRL is a reinforcement learning framework that improves LLM reasoning by learning from incorrect traces via recovery-oriented optimization, eliminating the need for stronger teacher models or curated difficult datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing RL methods for LLM reasoning depend on stronger teacher models or heavily curated difficult datasets, which limits scalability and increases resource requirements.
AI.method: DenoiseRL substitutes external supervision with recovery-oriented optimization over failures from weak models, converting incorrect reasoning traces into learning opportunities.
AI.result: DenoiseRL consistently outperforms strong on-policy RL baselines on mathematical and general reasoning benchmarks, and promotes stronger self-corrective behavior as training difficulty increases.
AI.conclusion: Learning from mistakes without external supervision offers an effective and scalable alternative pathway for improving reasoning in LLMs.

title: Semantic Optimal Transport for Sparse Autoencoder Feature Matching and Circuit Compression
summary: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have become a central tool for interpreting language models. However, two key SAE analyses that remain difficult to scale are (1) matching semantically similar features across multi-layers and (2) compressing large feature circuits into interpretable supernodes. Although these have been treated as separate problems, we show that both are instances of a more fundamental challenge, which we frame as the estimation of semantic distances between SAE features that lie on different activation manifolds. We introduce a distributional framework for this problem, in which each feature is represented not by a single decoder vector like in the literature, but by an activation-weighted distribution over the hidden states that express it. By projecting these distributions into a shared reference space and comparing them with Wasserstein distance, our method provides a unified semantic metric for cross-layer feature comparison. We prove that our representation is invariant to activation rescaling, stable under perturbations, and recovers true matches under finite-sample margin conditions. Empirically, our method outperforms decoder-vector and LLM-based baselines and captures subtle functional distinctions between related features. Notably, our method compresses large feature circuits into interpretable supernodes automatically.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a distributional framework for comparing SAE features across layers using Wasserstein distance, unifying cross-layer feature matching and circuit compression into a single approach.
AI.motivation: Two key SAE analyses—matching semantically similar features across layers and compressing feature circuits into interpretable supernodes—are difficult to scale and have been treated separately despite both involving estimating semantic distances between features on different activation manifolds.
AI.method: Each SAE feature is represented by an activation-weighted distribution over hidden states (rather than a single decoder vector). These distributions are projected into a shared reference space and compared using Wasserstein distance, providing a unified semantic metric for cross-layer comparison.
AI.result: The method outperforms decoder-vector and LLM-based baselines, captures subtle functional distinctions between related features, and automatically compresses large feature circuits into interpretable supernodes.
AI.conclusion: The proposed distributional framework is theoretically sound (invariant to rescaling, stable under perturbations, recovers true matches) and empirically effective for both cross-layer feature matching and circuit compression in SAE-based interpretability.

title: GONDOR to the Rescue: Satisficing Planning with Low Memory
summary: Greedy Best-First Search (GBFS) is the dominant approach for solving search problems where the goal can be estimated with a heuristic, such as planning, route finding, navigation, and pathfinding. This is especially true when the memory is tightly constrained, such as planning on edge devices. To alleviate that, we present GONDOR (Greedy Online Navigation with Dynamic Outpost-based Re-search), a memory-efficient extension of GBFS that allows search to continue under strict memory limits by periodically compressing the search tree while retaining a sparse set of anchor states, then upon reaching the goal reconstructs the path by re-searching between the sparse states. We analyze the algorithm and discuss several variants defined by different outpost selection policies. In addition, we explore using Bloom filters for compact duplicate detection in the closed list. Experiments across numeric planning domains and heuristic configurations show that GONDOR consistently improves coverage under low memory budgets compared to standard GBFS. We release the implementation of GONDOR and the Bloom-filter variant to facilitate further research on memory-efficient heuristic search.

AI.tldr: GONDOR is a memory-efficient extension of Greedy Best-First Search that compresses the search tree into sparse anchor states and re-searches between them, enabling effective planning under strict memory constraints.
AI.motivation: GBFS is dominant for heuristic search but struggles under tight memory constraints (e.g., edge devices). Existing approaches lack efficient memory compression while preserving solution quality.
AI.method: Periodically compresses the search tree into sparse anchor states (outposts), retains them, then re-searches between outposts upon goal discovery. Explores multiple outpost selection policies and integrates Bloom filters for compact duplicate detection.
AI.result: GONDOR consistently improves coverage under low memory budgets compared to standard GBFS across numeric planning domains and heuristic configurations.
AI.conclusion: GONDOR offers a practical, memory-efficient heuristic search method, with open-source implementation released for further research.

title: Efficient Pre-Training of LLMs through Truncated SVD Layers
summary: The massive scaling of Large Language Models (LLMs) has made pretraining increasingly cost-prohibitive. While low-rank representation and orthonormal weight matrices could in principle reduce parameter counts and computational overhead, most existing methods rely on static rank selection and do not enforce weight orthonormality due to high computational cost. This paper introduces TSVD, a framework that maintains low rank and strict orthonormality throughout the training process. It utilizes a spectral energy-based heuristic for adaptive rank selection, and a caching mechanisms to maintain orthonormality. Theoretical analysis justifies the advantage of the approach in pretraining dynamics and experiments across various model scales demonstrate that it is effective empirically. TSVD matches or exceeds the performance of full-parameter baselines while significantly reducing compute requirements. The approach thus offers a well-founded, practical, and scalable path toward efficient high-performance LLM pretraining.

AI.tldr: TSVD introduces a framework for efficient LLM pretraining that maintains low-rank weight matrices with strict orthonormality, using spectral energy-based adaptive rank selection and caching mechanisms, achieving full-parameter performance with significantly reduced compute.
AI.motivation: LLM pretraining has become cost-prohibitive due to massive scaling, and existing low-rank methods rely on static rank selection and fail to enforce orthonormality due to high computational cost.
AI.method: TSVD uses spectral energy-based heuristic for adaptive rank selection and caching mechanisms to maintain strict orthonormality throughout training, along with theoretical analysis of pretraining dynamics.
AI.result: TSVD matches or exceeds full-parameter baselines across various model scales while significantly reducing compute requirements.
AI.conclusion: TSVD offers a well-founded, practical, and scalable path toward efficient high-performance LLM pretraining.

title: Diffusion Large Language Models for Visual Speech Recognition
summary: Existing Visual Speech Recognition (VSR) systems commonly rely on left-to-right autoregressive decoding, which can force premature decisions on visually ambiguous tokens before sufficient context is available. We propose DLLM-VSR, to the best of our knowledge, the first Diffusion Large Language Model (DLLM)-based VSR framework, formulating transcription as iterative masked denoising with flexible-order decoding. With confidence-based unmasking, DLLM-VSR commits high-confidence positions early and uses the committed tokens as bidirectional context to refine ambiguous ones. To adapt DLLMs to VSR, we introduce a two-stage masked-denoising training strategy that separates visual-to-text content alignment from length modeling. We further observe a performance gap with oracle-length decoding, which assumes access to the true transcript length, indicating that reducing target-length uncertainty can improve DLLM-based VSR. To reduce this gap, we develop length-guided candidate decoding, which uses video duration to construct plausible transcript-length hypotheses, decodes under multiple hypotheses, and reranks candidates using length plausibility and decoding confidence. The proposed method achieves a state-of-the-art WER of 19.5\% on LRS3 using only its labeled training data.

AI.tldr: DLLM-VSR is the first Diffusion Large Language Model-based Visual Speech Recognition framework that replaces traditional left-to-right decoding with iterative masked denoising and flexible-order decoding, achieving 19.5% WER on LRS3.
AI.motivation: Existing VSR systems use left-to-right autoregressive decoding, which makes premature decisions on visually ambiguous tokens before sufficient context is available.
