AI.method: Proposes Thinking as Compression (TaC) which directly prompts thinking models to generate thinking traces as compressed context, and TaC-C which adds a reward-driven optimization framework for budget control and to avoid shortcut behaviors.
AI.result: At 4x and 8x compression ratios, TaC-C outperforms the strongest competitor by 17.4% and 23.4% in F1, and 15.7% and 21.7% in EM on long-context QA benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Thinking models can naturally serve as effective context compressors without dedicated modules, and reward-driven optimization further improves compactness and controllability.

title: OpenURMA: A Clean-Room Open Implementation of the Unified Bus Protocol
summary:
Modern datacenter RDMA is bottlenecked at the network interface, not the wire. A NIC running RoCE or InfiniBand holds per-connection state for every (application, remote-endpoint) pair - hundreds of megabytes at 1024-application fanout - and pays a four-traversal PCIe round trip on a 64-byte operation, inflating latency an order of magnitude beyond the wire. Both follow from the Queue Pair over PCIe abstraction RDMA inherits from InfiniBand.
  Huawei's Unified Bus (UB), a public 2025 specification, changes the abstraction: it decouples per-application endpoint state from per-host transport state so connection context grows additively, exposes ordering as opt-in, and reaches remote memory through native CPU load/store to an on-chip-bus controller. UB ships in Huawei's closed Ascend 950 silicon.
  OpenURMA is the first clean-room open implementation of UB's transport and transaction layers, realised at three tiers - synthesisable RTL on Alveo U50, a cycle-level two-node SystemC simulator, and a gem5 full-system scaffold - each with a matched OpenRoCE (RoCEv2 RC) baseline. The contribution is the implementation, harness, and controlled comparison closed silicon does not admit. On the canonical 64-byte remote fetch - LOAD on UB-spec Sec.8.3, READ on RoCEv2 RC - UB's load/store path delivers ~500 ns end-to-end, 4.37x below the matched baseline (2186 ns), sustains 2.80x higher throughput, and fits in ~14% of a U50's LUTs.

AI.tldr: OpenURMA is an open-source implementation of Huawei's Unified Bus (UB) transport protocol that achieves ~500ns end-to-end latency for remote memory operations, 4.37x faster than RoCEv2, by replacing per-connection queue pairs with load/store semantics over an on-chip bus.
AI.motivation: Modern RDMA (RoCE/InfiniBand) is bottlenecked by NIC-side per-connection state (hundreds of MB at scale) and four PCIe traversals per 64-byte operation, inflating latency an order of magnitude beyond wire speed.
AI.method: Implements Huawei's UB specification at three tiers: (1) synthesizable RTL on Alveo U50 FPGA, (2) cycle-level SystemC simulator, (3) gem5 full-system simulator, each with matched OpenRoCE baseline for controlled comparison.
AI.result: ~500ns end-to-end for 64-byte LOAD (vs 2186ns for RoCEv2 READ = 4.37x improvement), 2.80x higher throughput, and only ~14% of U50's LUT utilization.
AI.conclusion: UB's load/store abstraction with decoupled endpoint/transport state and opt-in ordering fundamentally outperforms the Queue Pair over PCIe model, and OpenURMA provides the first open implementation to validate this at the silicon level.

title: LiveBrowseComp: Are Search Agents Searching, or Just Verifying What They Already Know?
summary: Are LLM-based search agents genuinely searching, or using the web to verify what they already know? We study this question on BrowseComp with three diagnostics. Our analysis reveals Intrinsic Knowledge Dependence (IKD): even with tool access, agents often rely on intrinsic knowledge -- information encoded in the model before retrieval -- rather than on external evidence. Agents answer up to 44.5% of BrowseComp questions without tools, generate more than half of their search queries from internally produced hypotheses rather than retrieved leads, and perform worse than closed-book baselines when answer-supporting evidence is removed. These results suggest that static search benchmarks can reward memory-backed verification rather than evidence-driven discovery, conflating what agents already know with what they can find. We then introduce LiveBrowseComp, a deep-search benchmark designed to evaluate agents beyond intrinsic coverage. It contains 335 human-authored questions whose answers depend on facts published within the 90 days preceding benchmark construction, drawn from six updated sources and filtered to exclude globally salient events. On LiveBrowseComp, all evaluated agents fall below 2% closed-book accuracy, search-augmented scores drop by 25-40 points relative to BrowseComp, and prior model rankings no longer reliably predict performance. LiveBrowseComp is available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/Forival/LiveBrowseComp.

AI.tldr: LLM-based search agents often rely on their intrinsic knowledge rather than external web evidence when solving search benchmarks (up to 44.5% accuracy without tools). The authors introduce LiveBrowseComp, a benchmark requiring knowledge of recent (90-day) facts, where all agents drop below 2% closed-book accuracy and search-augmented scores fall 25-40 points.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether LLM-based search agents genuinely perform evidence-driven web searching or merely use the web to verify knowledge they already possess, and to expose the conflation between intrinsic knowledge and genuine search capability in existing benchmarks.
AI.method: Three diagnostic analyses on BrowseComp: (1) closed-book accuracy measurement, (2) tracing whether search queries originate from internal hypotheses or retrieved leads, (3) removing answer-supporting evidence. Then introduces LiveBrowseComp — 335 human-authored questions requiring facts from the most recent 90 days, sourced from six updated sources, excluding globally salient events.
AI.result: Agents answer up to 44.5% of BrowseComp questions without tools, generate over half of queries from internal hypotheses, and degrade when supporting evidence is removed. On LiveBrowseComp, all agents score below 2% closed-book accuracy, search-augmented scores drop 25-40 points, and prior rankings fail to predict performance.
AI.conclusion: Static search benchmarks conflate what agents already know with what they can discover, rewarding memory-backed verification. LiveBrowseComp provides a more reliable evaluation of genuine evidence-driven search beyond intrinsic knowledge coverage.

title: Multi-Adapter Representation Interventions via Energy Calibration
summary: Representation intervention has emerged as a promising paradigm for aligning large language models toward desired behaviors without modifying model weights. Existing methods typically apply a fixed intervention uniformly across all inputs. However, we find that the appropriate intervention direction and strength vary substantially across samples, and such indiscriminate intervention leads to degradation of general capabilities on benign inputs. To address these challenges, we propose Multi-Adapter Representation Interventions via Energy Calibration (MARI). Specifically, we introduce a competitive multi-adapter mechanism in which specialized experts capture non-linear correction patterns and adaptively determine the appropriate intervention direction and strength for different samples. Furthermore, we design an energy-based gating module that leverages internal propagation dynamics to distinguish inputs that are applicable for intervention. Extensive experiments across diverse model families and parameter scales demonstrate that MARI achieves state-of-the-art alignment performance. Our method significantly improves performance on TruthfulQA, BBQ, and safety benchmarks, while maintaining and even improving general capabilities on tasks such as MMLU and ARC. Our code is available at https://github.com/V1centNevwake/MARI.

AI.tldr: MARI introduces a competitive multi-adapter mechanism with energy-based gating for adaptive, sample-specific representation interventions, achieving state-of-the-art alignment without degrading general capabilities.
AI.motivation: Existing representation intervention methods apply fixed, uniform interventions across all inputs, but the appropriate intervention direction and strength vary substantially across samples, causing degradation of general capabilities on benign inputs.
AI.method: MARI proposes (1) a competitive multi-adapter mechanism where specialized experts capture non-linear correction patterns and adaptively determine intervention direction/strength per sample, and (2) an energy-based gating module that uses internal propagation dynamics to distinguish inputs suitable for intervention.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art alignment performance across diverse model families and scales, significantly improving TruthfulQA, BBQ, and safety benchmarks while maintaining/improving general capabilities on MMLU and ARC.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, sample-specific representation intervention via multi-adapter and energy-based gating effectively resolves the trade-off between alignment and general capability degradation.

title: AlphaTransit: Learning to Design City-scale Transit Routes
summary: Designing a transit network requires many sequential route extension decisions, but their quality is often visible only after the full network is assembled. This delayed-feedback challenge lies at the heart of the Transit Route Network Design Problem (TRNDP), where route interactions can be deceptive: an extension that appears useful locally can create transfer bottlenecks, produce redundant overlap, or reduce overall throughput. To guide route construction under delayed simulator feedback, we introduce AlphaTransit, a search-based planning framework for cityscale bus network design. AlphaTransit couples Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) with a neural policy-value network: the policy proposes route extensions, the value estimates downstream design quality, and search uses these predictions to refine each decision. This provides decision-time lookahead during route construction without running simulator rollouts inside the search tree. We evaluate AlphaTransit on a new Bloomington TRNDP benchmark with realistic road topology and censusderived demand, under mixed and full transit demand settings. In the Bloomington network, AlphaTransit attains the highest service rate in both demand settings, reaching 54.6% and 82.1%, respectively. Relative to reinforcement learning without search, these correspond to 9.9% and 11.4% service rate gains; relative to MCTS without learned guidance, they correspond to 2.5% and 11.2% gains. These results suggest that coupling learned guidance with MCTS is more effective than using either approach alone for transit network design. Our code and data are publicly available in https://github.com/poudel-bibek/AlphaTransit.

AI.tldr: AlphaTransit combines Monte Carlo Tree Search with neural policy-value networks to solve the Transit Route Network Design Problem (TRNDP), achieving significant service rate gains (up to 82.1%) on a Bloomington benchmark by providing decision-time lookahead during route construction.
AI.motivation: Transit network design suffers from delayed-feedback: route extension quality is only visible after the full network is built, and local improvements can create deceptive global issues like transfer bottlenecks or redundant overlap.
AI.method: AlphaTransit couples MCTS with a neural policy-value network — the policy proposes route extensions, the value estimates downstream quality, and search refines decisions using these predictions to provide lookahead without simulator rollouts inside the search tree.
AI.result: On the Bloomington TRNDP benchmark, AlphaTransit achieves 54.6% (mixed demand) and 82.1% (full demand) service rates, outperforming RL without search by 9.9%/11.4% and MCTS without learned guidance by 2.5%/11.2%.
AI.conclusion: Coupling learned guidance (neural policy-value networks) with MCTS is more effective for transit network design than using either approach alone.

title: Utility-Aware Multimodal Contrastive Learning for Product Image Generation
summary: Product images strongly influence consumer decision-making in online marketplaces. Empowered by multimodal contrastive learning, generative AI can output images that closely align with text prompts. Yet existing generative AI models do not directly optimize marketplace performance. This is a critical gap, since semantic alignment alone does not guarantee that an image will sell. To address this limitation, we propose a \textit{utility-aware multimodal contrastive learning} framework that incorporates consumer demand into a novel Utility-Aware InfoNCE loss. Optimizing this utility-aware objective guides generation toward images that are both semantically coherent and demand-enhancing. This effect arises directly from a shift in the learned image-text representation space toward demand-driven visual cues, which we also validate through the theoretical bound of the proposed objective. In downstream applications on Amazon and Airbnb, product images generated and edited by our method outperform state-of-the-art models in increasing demand and preserving fidelity, while maintaining text-image consistency. Notably, our utility-aware framework preserves inverse U-shaped demand patterns for attributes such as aesthetics and uniqueness, improving demand-based performance while preserving fidelity and semantic consistency. Human-subject experiments further validate its commercial effectiveness. As generative AI technology continues to evolve, our utility-aware component can be flexibly embedded into emerging generative models to improve direct commercial use.

AI.tldr: Proposes a utility-aware multimodal contrastive learning framework that incorporates consumer demand into generative AI image generation, outperforming existing models on Amazon and Airbnb by producing images that are both semantically coherent and demand-enhancing.
AI.motivation: Existing generative AI models optimize for semantic alignment between text and images but do not directly optimize marketplace performance (sales), creating a critical gap since semantically aligned images do not guarantee commercial success.
AI.method: Introduces a Utility-Aware InfoNCE loss within a multimodal contrastive learning framework that incorporates consumer demand signals, shifting the learned image-text representation space toward demand-driven visual cues, supported by theoretical bounds.
AI.result: On Amazon and Airbnb, generated/edited images outperform state-of-the-art models in increasing demand while preserving fidelity and text-image consistency; preserves inverse U-shaped demand patterns for aesthetics and uniqueness; validated by human-subject experiments.
AI.conclusion: The utility-aware component can be flexibly embedded into emerging generative models to improve direct commercial use, bridging the gap between semantic alignment and marketplace performance.

title: CORE: Contrastive Reflection Enables Rapid Improvements in Reasoning
summary: Language models can use verifiable rewards to improve at a wide variety of reasoning tasks. However, both parametric (e.g. RLVR) and non-parametric (e.g. prompt optimization) approaches to doing so typically require hundreds of training samples and thousands of model rollouts, making them expensive in the best case and intractable in the worst. To address this challenge, we introduce Contrastive Reflection (CORE), a non-parametric learning algorithm that compares past reasoning traces to generate insights: short natural-language descriptions of reasoning strategies and constraints that capture differences between successful and unsuccessful problem attempts. Across four reasoning tasks, we demonstrate that CORE enables more rapid improvement than both parametric (GRPO) and non-parametric (GEPA, episodic RAG, and MemRL) methods, while using fewer rollouts. Under fixed rollout budgets with as few as five training samples, we then show that CORE also achieves comparable or greater performance gains than each baseline. Finally, we highlight how CORE is also substantially more context-efficient than non-parametric baselines, requiring fewer prompt tokens while storing learned knowledge as compact, interpretable natural-language insights. Our results therefore suggest that distilling contrasts between successful and unsuccessful reasoning traces into abstract and useful insights can provide a more efficient and interpretable route to model self-improvement than weight updates, prompt optimization, or direct reuse of stored reasoning traces.

AI.tldr: CORE is a non-parametric learning algorithm that improves language model reasoning by generating compact, interpretable insights from contrasting successful and failed reasoning traces, achieving faster and more efficient improvement than parametric (e.g., RLVR, GRPO) and other non-parametric methods, especially under low-resource settings with as few as five training samples.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches for improving language model reasoning with verifiable rewards (both parametric and non-parametric) are expensive, requiring hundreds of training samples and thousands of model rollouts, making them inefficient or intractable in resource-constrained scenarios.
AI.method: Contrastive Reflection (CORE) is a non-parametric algorithm that compares past successful and unsuccessful reasoning traces to generate short natural-language insights — abstract descriptions of reasoning strategies and constraints that capture the key differences between correct and incorrect attempts.
AI.result: Across four reasoning tasks, CORE achieves more rapid improvement than GRPO, GEPA, episodic RAG, and MemRL while using fewer rollouts. With as few as five training samples, CORE matches or exceeds baseline performance. It also requires fewer prompt tokens than non-parametric baselines, storing knowledge compactly.
AI.conclusion: Distilling contrasts between successful and unsuccessful reasoning into abstract, interpretable insights offers a more efficient and interpretable path to model self-improvement than weight updates, prompt optimization, or direct reuse of traces.

title: CubePart: An Open-Vocabulary Part-Controllable 3D Generator
summary: Interactive 3D assets used in games and simulation are typically decomposed into specific semantic parts to support animation, physics, and scripted behaviors, yet most generative 3D models produce either monolithic meshes or arbitrary part decompositions that cannot be aligned with application-specific requirements. We present CubePart, a generative framework for open-vocabulary, part-controllable 3D mesh generation that exposes part structure as an explicit inference-time control signal. Given a global text prompt and a user-defined parts schema expressed as an open-ended list of part names, our method generates a set of meshes - one per schema element - that assemble into a coherent object while respecting the specified semantic structure. To enable this capability, we introduce a scalable data pipeline to construct a large open-vocabulary, part-labeled 3D dataset, along with a two-stage generative architecture that separates global shape synthesis from part-level decoding. We demonstrate that the resulting assets can be directly integrated into game engines and driven by animation and behavior scripts without manual post-processing. Project Page: https://cubepart.github.io/

AI.tldr: CubePart generates 3D meshes with user-defined semantic part structures from text prompts, enabling direct use in games and simulations without manual post-processing.
AI.motivation: Interactive 3D assets require specific semantic part decompositions for animation, physics, and scripting, but current generative models produce monolithic or arbitrary parts that don't align with application-specific needs.
AI.method: A two-stage generative architecture with (1) a scalable data pipeline for open-vocabulary part-labeled 3D dataset construction, and (2) separation of global shape synthesis from part-level decoding. Users provide a global text prompt plus an open-ended list of part names as control signals.
AI.result: Generates coherent 3D meshes per schema element that assemble into objects respecting specified semantic structure; assets integrate directly into game engines and support animation/behavior scripts without manual post-processing.
AI.conclusion: CubePart enables open-vocabulary, part-controllable 3D generation that bridges the gap between generative models and practical asset requirements for interactive applications.

title: SwarmHarness: Skill-Based Task Routing via Decentralized Incentive-Aligned AI Agent Networks
summary: Vast quantities of compute (GPU cycles on personal workstations, idle inference servers, and edge devices between jobs) go unused because no incentive-aligned protocol exists for their owners to share them safely and profitably. Existing approaches either require a trusted central coordinator (cloud marketplaces), demand heavy blockchain infrastructure (Golem, BrokerChain), or lack an incentive layer entirely (BOINC, Petals). We propose SwarmHarness, a decentralised protocol in which HarnessAPI skill nodes self-organise into a compute swarm without any central authority. SwarmHarness has three interlocking components: a SwarmRegistry built on a Distributed Hash Table (DHT) for peer discovery and capability advertisement; a SwarmRouter that dispatches tasks to nodes using a utility function over capability, load, latency, and trust; and SwarmCredit, an incentive mechanism that attributes compute-credit rewards to contributing nodes via a Shapley-value approximation. Nodes earn credits by serving tasks and spend credits to submit them; idle nodes that never contribute drain credits and lose routing priority, creating a self-regulating participation economy. As nodes specialise toward high-reward skills and routing signals act as digital pheromones, the network exhibits emergent collective intelligence analogous to biological swarms. Beyond compute sharing, SwarmHarness is a foundational primitive for autonomous distributed AI agent networks in which agents hire compute, route subtasks, and settle credits without human intermediation.

AI.tldr: SwarmHarness is a decentralized protocol enabling owners of idle compute resources (GPUs, edge devices) to share them safely and profitably without central authority, using DHT-based peer discovery, a utility-driven task router, and a Shapley-value-based credit incentive mechanism that creates a self-regulating participation economy.
AI.motivation: Vast quantities of compute (GPU cycles on personal workstations, idle inference servers, edge devices) go unused because no incentive-aligned protocol exists for safe and profitable sharing. Existing approaches require trusted central coordinators, heavy blockchain infrastructure, or lack incentive layers entirely.
AI.method: SwarmHarness has three interlocking components: (1) SwarmRegistry built on a DHT for peer discovery and capability advertisement; (2) SwarmRouter that dispatches tasks using a utility function over capability, load, latency, and trust; (3) SwarmCredit, a Shapley-value approximation based incentive mechanism where nodes earn credits by serving tasks and spend credits to submit them, with idle nodes draining credits and losing routing priority.
AI.result: The protocol creates a self-regulating participation economy where nodes specialize toward high-reward skills and routing signals act as digital pheromones, enabling emergent collective intelligence analogous to biological swarms without central coordination.
AI.conclusion: SwarmHarness is a foundational primitive for autonomous distributed AI agent networks in which agents hire compute, route subtasks, and settle credits without human intermediation.

title: CaMBRAIN: Real-time, Continuous EEG Inference with Causal State Space Models
summary: Electroencephalography (EEG) is a critical, non-invasive method to monitor electrical brain activity. EEGs can span anywhere from a couple seconds to multiple hours, posing a major hurdle for existing deep learning methods due to two major factors: (1) existing EEG models are predominantly built upon the attention mechanism, incurring quadratic scaling as the sequence length increases, and (2) raw EEG signals must be processed in a sliding-window fashion due to fixed-length input requirements, preventing global understanding of the entire signal. To this extent, we propose CaMBRAIN - the first Causal, Mamba-based state space model (SSM) capable of real-time inference of EEG signals, arguing that bidirectional approaches are needlessly expensive given the causal, unidirectional nature of EEG. However, training such a model is non-trivial, as crucial EEG events can be extremely brief - within fractions of a second - yet separated by long intervals spanning minutes. Current EEG methods use self-supervised objectives that optimize for signal reconstruction, but these are not well suited for streaming SSMs; they fail to explicitly train the hidden state to retain the salient long-range context needed for streaming inference. We therefore introduce a multi-stage self-supervised training pipeline specifically tailored to encourage long-range memory retention and strong performance on EEG signals, while preserving the linear-time complexity of state space models. CaMBRAIN achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) results across 3 different EEG datasets with >10x higher throughput than existing models, enabling the first model capable of long-range, continuous inference of variable-length EEG signals.

AI.tldr: CaMBRAIN introduces a causal Mamba-based state space model for real-time EEG inference, paired with a multi-stage self-supervised training pipeline that encourages long-range memory retention. It achieves SOTA results across 3 EEG datasets with >10x higher throughput than existing attention-based models.
AI.motivation: Existing deep learning EEG models rely on attention mechanisms with quadratic scaling in sequence length, and require fixed-length sliding-window processing that prevents global understanding of the entire signal. EEG signals can span from seconds to hours with critical events being extremely brief yet separated by long intervals.
AI.method: Proposes CaMBRAIN, a causal Mamba-based state space model (SSM) for linear-time complexity real-time EEG inference. Introduces a multi-stage self-supervised training pipeline specifically designed to train the hidden state for long-range memory retention, tailored for streaming SSMs rather than signal reconstruction objectives.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art results across 3 different EEG datasets with >10x higher throughput than existing models, enabling the first model capable of long-range, continuous inference of variable-length EEG signals.
AI.conclusion: The causal Mamba-based approach is well-suited for EEG given its unidirectional nature, and the novel training pipeline successfully addresses the challenge of retaining long-range context in streaming SSMs, making continuous real-time EEG inference feasible.

title: Calibrating Conservatism for Scalable Oversight
summary: Agentic AI systems capable of autonomous planning and extended environmental interaction pose a fundamental control problem: how can humans maintain meaningful oversight of systems that may exceed their own capabilities? Existing approaches to scalable oversight rely on complex assumptions, remain largely heuristic, or lack practical methods for sequential settings with statistical guarantees. We introduce Calibrated Collective Oversight (CCO), which aggregates diverse auxiliary scoring functions into a penalty measuring deviation from a conservative baseline. Inspired by Attainable Utility Preservation, CCO enables collective conservatism: actions face a penalty proportional to overseer concern, so high-utility actions are still selected when overseers find them unobjectionable and overridden only when concern accumulates. CCO calibrates this conservatism online using Conformal Decision Theory, ensuring that undesirable outcomes remain below a user-specified target threshold with finite-time bounds and no distributional assumptions. On a modified version of SWE-bench, weaker overseers successfully constrain an adversarially misaligned stronger agent; on MACHIAVELLI, CCO substantially reduces ethical violations while preserving reward. In both settings, empirical violation rates closely match the specified targets, as predicted by the theory.

AI.tldr: Calibrated Collective Oversight (CCO) introduces a statistically-grounded method for humans to maintain oversight of autonomous AI agents by aggregating diverse scorer concerns into a calibrated penalty, achieving guaranteed control over undesirable outcomes with finite-time bounds and no distributional assumptions.
AI.motivation: Existing scalable oversight approaches rely on complex assumptions, remain heuristic, or lack practical methods with statistical guarantees for sequential decision-making settings where AI systems may exceed human capabilities.
AI.method: CCO aggregates diverse auxiliary scoring functions into a penalty measuring deviation from a conservative baseline. It calibrates conservatism online using Conformal Decision Theory, ensuring undesirable outcomes stay below a user-specified threshold with finite-time bounds and no distributional assumptions.
AI.result: On SWE-bench, weaker overseers successfully constrained an adversarially misaligned stronger agent. On MACHIAVELLI, CCO substantially reduced ethical violations while preserving reward. Empirical violation rates closely matched specified targets across both settings.
AI.conclusion: CCO provides a practical, theoretically-grounded framework for scalable oversight of autonomous AI systems with statistical guarantees, enabling collective conservatism where actions are penalized proportionally to overseer concern and only overridden when concern accumulates.

title: LNN-PINN: A Unified Physics-Only Training Framework with Liquid Residual Blocks
summary: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) have attracted considerable attention for their ability to integrate partial differential equation priors into deep learning frameworks; however, they often exhibit limited predictive accuracy when applied to complex problems. To address this issue, we propose LNN-PINN, a physics-informed neural network framework that incorporates a liquid residual gating architecture while preserving the original physics modeling and optimization pipeline to improve predictive accuracy. The method introduces a lightweight gating mechanism solely within the hidden-layer mapping, keeping the sampling strategy, loss composition, and hyperparameter settings unchanged to ensure that improvements arise purely from architectural refinement. Across four benchmark problems, LNN-PINN consistently reduced RMSE and MAE under identical training conditions, with absolute error plots further confirming its accuracy gains. Moreover, the framework demonstrates strong adaptability and stability across varying dimensions, boundary conditions, and operator characteristics. In summary, LNN-PINN offers a concise and effective architectural enhancement for improving the predictive accuracy of physics-informed neural networks in complex scientific and engineering problems.

AI.tldr: LNN-PINN introduces a liquid residual gating architecture into physics-informed neural networks, improving predictive accuracy across multiple benchmark problems without changing sampling, loss, or hyperparameter settings.
AI.motivation: PINNs struggle with limited predictive accuracy on complex problems, motivating the need for architectural improvements that preserve the existing physics modeling and optimization pipeline.
AI.method: The authors propose LNN-PINN, which inserts a lightweight liquid residual gating mechanism into the hidden-layer mapping of PINNs, while keeping sampling strategy, loss composition, and hyperparameters unchanged.
AI.result: Across four benchmark problems, LNN-PINN consistently reduces RMSE and MAE under identical training conditions, with absolute error plots confirming accuracy gains.
AI.conclusion: LNN-PINN offers a concise and effective architectural enhancement for improving PINN predictive accuracy in complex scientific and engineering problems.
